Issue #25952: Need broadcast matmul support
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In uniad model, 
I am trying to matmul between two tensors of shape, 
input_a=torch.randn(1, 1, 1, 2, 2)
input_b=torch.randn(1, 4, 6, 2, 12)

We are encountered with a issue.

Issue: 
 RuntimeError: TT_FATAL @ /home/ubuntu/punith/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/matmul_op.cpp:1545: a_shape[i] == b_shape[i]
E       info:
E       bmm (non-bcast matmul) expects input tensors of shapes BCMK*BCKN=BCMN or equivalent
E       backtrace:

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run the following code,

```
import torch
import pytest 
import ttnn 

@pytest.mark.parametrize("device_params", [{"l1_small_size": 4 * 8192}], indirect=True)
def test_uniad_MotionTransformerAttentionLayer(device, reset_seeds):
    input_a=torch.randn(1, 1, 1, 2, 2)
    input_b=torch.randn(1, 4, 6, 2, 12)
    print("torch",torch.matmul(input_a,input_b).shape)
    ttnn_input=ttnn.from_torch(input_a,device=device,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.bfloat16)
    ttnn_inputb=ttnn.from_torch(input_b,device=device,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.bfloat16)
    ttnn.matmul(ttnn_input,ttnn_inputb)
```


### Expected behavior

It should pass without any error.

### Please complete the following environment information

Device - n150

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25945: Conv3d align api with pytorch
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` API differs from pytorch where the most of the params are in `Conv3dConfig`.
- The following members of the `Conv3dConfig` struct should be moved to the operation api:

kernel_size
stride
padding
padding_mode
groups
dilation (when it gets added https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25940)
dtype

- `memory_config` and `compute_kernel_config` should be left as operation params

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25941: Conv3d group support
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` supports only `groups=1`.

`groups` is a member of the Conv3dConfig that is passed to `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` op.

`ttnn.conv2d` group support can be used as a reference.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25940: Conv3d dilation support
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` doesn't support dilation.
This is one of missing features of this operation.

`ttnn.conv2d` can be used as a guide for implementation of dilation parameter.

Dilation should be a member of the `Conv3dConfig` that is passed to `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` op.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25936: Conv3d T dim padding
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently `ttnn.experimental.conv3d` op doesn't support padding of the T dimension of the input tensor where the shape of the input tensor is (N,C,T,H,W) where
- N  batch size
- C  input channels
- T  depth
- H  height
- W width

The code asserts that only padding of zero is passed for T dimension
```
TT_FATAL(

config.padding[0] == 0,

"Padding must be (0,x,x). got ({}, {}, {})",

config.padding[0],

config.padding[1],

config.padding[2]);
```

Note: T dimension is called D in pytorch docs for [conv3d](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Conv3d.html)

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25919: Install boost::small_vector with TTNN using a custom namespace
------------------------------------------------------------
Rather than reimplementing boost::small_vector, we’ll include it in the TTNN development package using the bcp tool.
The bcp tool allows us to extract specific components from the Boost library and optionally rename the boost namespace to a custom one.

Example:
```
./bootstrap.sh
./b2 tools/bcp
./dist/bin/bcp --namespace=ttnn_boost boost/container/small_vector.hpp ~/ttnn-boost/
```

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25917: replace RT args for reshard with config vector
------------------------------------------------------------
Reshard op for large tensors runs out of rt args.
Fix that by replacing rt args with config vectors that are passed as tensors to the kernels

Issue #25911: Hang when in0 shard width == in0_block_w
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

If in0_block_w == shard_width_in_tiles, then extract_shard_sub_blocks in the program factory is set to false. However, if this is the case, then the in0 sender kernel (reader_bmm_tile_layout_in0_sender_padding) never ends up populating the CB data in the loops, leading to a hang after the first compute iteration.

There doesn't appear to be a case where in0 is sharded and extract_shard_sub_blocks is false is needed. Perhaps it can be removed.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Can use matmul_default_height_sharded pytest as an example with a custom program config that sets in0_block_w == shard width == K


### Expected behavior

No hang.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25910: 1D matmul block size restrictions not being enforced for sharded output
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In 1D matmul, out_block_h/w must equal per_core_M/N (respectively). However, get_multi_dim_per_core_factor() does not enforce this, leading to some asserts getting triggered in validate()

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Can use matmul_default_height_sharded pytest as an example with the following inputs.

M = 5376
N = K = 288
output mem config: ttnn.L1_HEIGHT_SHARDED_MEMORY_CONFIG

### Expected behavior

get_multi_dim_per_core_factor() should enforce the out_block_h/w constraints

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25909: Invalid number of cores being used to compute per core M/N and block sizes
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In create_matmul_1d_systolic_array_program_config(), num_cores is computed from the device's grid size (e.g., 8x8 for WH) is there is no user defined core coord. However, this can lead to invalid per_core_M/out_subblock_h and/or per_core_N/out_subblock_w combinations depending on M and N. 

### Steps to reproduce the issue

See matmul_default_height_sharded pytest as an example. Use the inputs:

M = 5376
N = K = 288
num_cores = 10

### Expected behavior

The expected behavior is to use the largest # of cores possible that satisfy the constraints in validate(). So we should start with the device's entire grid and work down until we find a num cores that satisfies the constraints.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25905: poin_to_point fast follow
------------------------------------------------------------
Point to point has essential functionality but needs:
- A semaphore wait in the writer kernel of the sending device to ensure the destination (temporary) buffer exists on the receiver device. This is a priority, otherwise there will be PCC issues in models
- Handle unaligned RM pages sizes. (basic mask and second buffer stuff in the reader/writer kernels)
- Clean up program dispatch on unused devices so we don't need the null op stuff

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25903: Matmul unit tests don't cover ProgramCache
------------------------------------------------------------
Matmul family of unit tests do not test the same stimulus twice, so if there are issues with Program Cache or bugs in updating runtime args with a cache hit, they won't get caught until sweeps are run.


We should explore adding 1 or 2 tests to check program cache to unit tests

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25898: Row-major Reshard with different page widths
------------------------------------------------------------
In this function: 
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/data_movement/sharded/reshard/device/reshard_program_factory.cpp#L37-L44

We basically map an input shard spec to an output shard spec but it assumes both have the same page-width.

The way to do this would be to use the lowest-common denominator for a different page-widths.

Example if we are going from one width-sharded to another width-shaded, where the page width of the first one is 4 elements and the second width is 8 elements, we should see the second width-shard to have two pages of 4 elements.
That way we can map input to output pages.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run the following TTNN Python test to reproduce the behaviour:
```Python
import ttnn
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc

device_id = 0
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=device_id, l1_small_size=10 * 1024)

torch_random_input = torch.ones(1, 1, 1024, 64)

ttnn_random_input = ttnn.from_torch(torch_random_input, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16)
ttnn_random_input_rm = ttnn.to_layout(ttnn_random_input, ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT)

ttnn_max_pooling = ttnn.max_pool2d(
            input_tensor=ttnn_random_input_rm,
            batch_size=1,
            input_h=int(32),
            input_w=int(32),
            channels=64,
            kernel_size=(3, 3),
            stride=(1, 1),
            dilation=(2, 2),
            padding=(0, 0),
            ceil_mode=True,
            memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG,
        )

# Calculate torch golden result
f = nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size=(3, 3), stride=(1, 1), padding=(0, 0), dilation=(2, 2), ceil_mode=True)
# Convert torch input from (1, 1, N*H*W, C) to (N, C, H, W)
torch_reshaped_input = torch_random_input.view(1, 1, 32, 32, 64).permute(0, 1, 4, 2, 3).reshape(1, 64, 32, 32) # Reshape to (N, C, H, W)
torch_max_pooling = f(torch_reshaped_input)
print("Torch average pooling result:", torch_max_pooling)
print("Torch average pooling result shape:", torch_max_pooling.shape)
# Convert torch result back from (N, C, H, W) to (1, 1, N*H*W, C)
torch_max_pooling = torch_max_pooling.permute(0, 2, 3, 1).reshape(1, 1, -1, 64)

print("Max Pooling Result:", ttnn_max_pooling)

# Convert ttnn result to torch tensor for comparison
ttnn_max_pooling_torch = ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_max_pooling)

assert_with_pcc(ttnn_max_pooling_torch, torch_max_pooling)

ttnn.close_device(device)
```

### Expected behavior

The same PyTorch op executes correctly without crashing. The expectation is that the TTNN op executes correctly as well.

### Please complete the following environment information

- OS version: Ubuntu 22.04
- Code version: latest main

Issue #25883: High atol with ttnn.rms_norm Compared to Custom Implementation
------------------------------------------------------------
ttnn.rms_norm is producing a high absolute tolerance (atol) error when tested with the provided input and weight configuration. However, a custom implementation using primitive operations such as ttnn.pow, ttnn.mean, ttnn.multiply, etc., We got much better atol and pcc. Below test represents the discrepency over the results.
 
Based on the outcomes, I would like to understand the reason behind the difference that we observe.
 
**Steps to Reproduce :**
 
1. Use the attached [test_rms_comp.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21467451/test_rms_comp.txt) file to run the test in the path tt-metal/models/common/rms_inputs/test_rms_cmp.py.

weight = torch.load("models/common/rms_inputs/weight.pt")`

Issue #25867: Gracefully handle possible GitHub API errors with the release when downloading assets via `install_dependencies.sh`
------------------------------------------------------------
From @bbradelTT https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16362116606/job/46254884269?pr=24832

my response

I'll detail my process here. So, the log that fails is
```
[INFO] Installing Tenstorrent Hugepages Service ...
  3850K .......... .......... .......... .......... ...       100%  307M=0.02swget: missing URL
Usage: wget [OPTION]... [URL]...

Try `wget --help' for more options.
```

wget - a linux command line tool for downloading stuff failed because it didn't have a URL? Odd. That smells to me that a variable that was meant to be the URL wasn't set correctly.

Taking a look in install_dependencies.sh where this is coming from by grepping for [INFO] Installing Tenstorrent Hugepages Service in codebase, we see that

```bash
    TT_TOOLS_LINK=$(wget -qO- https://api.github.com/repos/tenstorrent/tt-system-tools/releases/latest | jq -r '.assets[] | select(.name | endswith(".deb")) | .browser_download_url')
    TT_TOOLS_NAME=$(wget -qO- https://api.github.com/repos/tenstorrent/tt-system-tools/releases/latest | jq -r '.assets[] | select(.name | endswith(".deb")) | .name')

    echo "[INFO] Installing Tenstorrent Hugepages Service $TT_TOOLS_NAME..."
    TEMP_DIR=$(mktemp -d)
    wget -P $TEMP_DIR $TT_TOOLS_LINK
```

so since the log is supposed to spit out the link, there's a good chance that the initial call to get the link failed and we got no output in return.

My guess is GitHub spat back a bad response, and it was likely because we were being rate-limited. Of course. Things like back-offs + retries / proper error message handling will help with this.

For now retrying the job is sufficient. Let me see if something like a -o pipefail with some handling for empty responses in this section passes pipelines to make this easier to spot.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25866: Require whitelisting of datasets to download required data for model demos and/or evaluation
------------------------------------------------------------
**Issue Description**
([demo](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/models/demos/vgg_unet/demo/demo.py))VGG_Unet - requires data downloaded from Kaggle for the model demo.
([Datast_eval](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/models/demos/sentence_bert/demo/dataset_evaluation.py)] SentenceBERT - requires validation dataset from [sts-benchmark-tr](https://github.com/emrecncelik/sts-benchmark-tr) repo.
([demo](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/models/demos/mobilenetv2/demo/demo.py))MobilenetV2 - requires data downloaded from HF ImageNet dataset for model demo.
([UFLDv2](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/models/demos/ufld_v2/demo/dataset_evaluation.py)) - requires data downloaded from Kaggle for the model dataset_evaluation task.
[Segformer Classification Demo](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/models/demos/segformer/demo/demo_for_image_classification.py) - requires ImageNet-1k dataset from HuggingFace
Add the dataset "validation-data-ade-20k" to the CIv2 as well? (requested [here](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25888#issuecomment-3130975339))
Here is the reference:
ONEDRIVE_ZIP_LINK="https://tinyurl.com/4xtuxr3k"

Currently, the single-card-demo tests are skipped for the above reason.

Enable the support to download datasets or make them available through CIv2 for testing the demos for the above models.
**URLs to be Whitelisted**
VGG_UNet Demo - https://www.kaggle.com/api/v1/datasets/download/mateuszbuda/lgg-mri-segmentation
Imagenet-1k (MobileNet &Segformer Classification Demo) - https://huggingface.co/datasets/imagenet-1k/resolve/main/imagenet-1k.py

Issue #25860: BH and WH installed in the same system is not supported with no workaround
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

A user has a WH card and a BH card installed in the same system, but hits this exception when trying to run any program:
```
2025-07-26 12:57:45.675 | critical |          Always | Expected all devices to be blackhole but device 1 is wormhole_b0 (assert.hpp:107)
```

Using `TT_METAL_VISIBLE_DEVICES=<WH-or-BH-ids>` also did not correctly mask the device discovery so we should investigate why this homogeneous check routine does not respect `visible_devices`.

**Update**: looks like this is helping the offending code - https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-umd/blob/e08a4e3b08ea724334deb1277f9eb266e5732531/device/pcie/pci_device.cpp#L130. We need to filter this list of PCI devices by the `TT_METAL_VISIBLE_DEVICES` mask

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Using tt-metal commit `0c4012a502aa259637d6e9bc5a86098448228df2`
2. Install a WH and BH card into the same system
3. `python -c "import ttnn; device = ttnn.CreateDevice(device_id=0)"`
4. See error


### Expected behavior

We should be able to mask out the detection of devices with `TT_METAL_VISIBLE_DEVICES`

### Please complete the following environment information

- tt-metal commit: `0c4012a502aa259637d6e9bc5a86098448228df2`

Issue #25840: Implement uneven padding support for pooling ops
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently the pooling ops can only pad evenly along the horizontal/vertical. 

For example a padding of `(1, 1)` will pad one row to the top, one row to the bottom, one row to the left, and one row to the right. We would like to specify padding specifically for top, bottom left and right. That is,

change the API from `padding = (vertical, horizontal)` to `padding = (top, bottom, left, right)`

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25839: ttnn.reciprocal pcc error
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

I get a PCC error on ttnn.reciprocal in this test:

```
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: © 2023 Tenstorrent Inc.
# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0

import pytest
import torch
import ttnn
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc

def test_reciprocal(device):
    input_shape = (784, 784)
    torch_input_a = torch.rand(input_shape, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_input_b = torch.rand(input_shape, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_input_c = torch.rand(input_shape, dtype=torch.float32)

    torch_multiply_result = torch_input_c * torch_input_a
    torch_subtract_result = torch_input_b - torch_multiply_result
    torch_reciprocal_result = torch.reciprocal(torch_subtract_result)

    ttnn_tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_a,
        layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT,
        device=device,
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )
    ttnn_tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_b,
        layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT,
        device=device,
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )
    ttnn_tensor_c = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_c,
        layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT,
        device=device,
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )

    ttnn_multiply_result = ttnn.multiply(ttnn_tensor_c, ttnn_tensor_a)
    assert_with_pcc(torch_multiply_result, ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_multiply_result), 0.999)

    ttnn_subtract_result = ttnn.subtract(ttnn_tensor_b, ttnn_multiply_result)
    assert_with_pcc(torch_subtract_result, ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_subtract_result), 0.999)

    ttnn_subtract_host = ttnn.from_device(ttnn_subtract_result)
    ttnn_subtract_tile = ttnn.to_layout(ttnn_subtract_host, ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT)
    ttnn_subtract_device = ttnn.to_device(
        ttnn_subtract_tile,
        device,
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )
    assert_with_pcc(torch_subtract_result, ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_subtract_device), 0.999)

    ttnn_reciprocal_result = ttnn.reciprocal(ttnn_subtract_device)
    assert_with_pcc(torch_reciprocal_result, ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_reciprocal_result), 0.999)
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run the test left in `Describe the bug`


### Expected behavior

The test should pass

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

Issue #25836: Investigate ND hang of stable diffusion (old demo version) on e2e perf
------------------------------------------------------------

Issue #25825: `llama3_subdevices/text_demo.py`: broken `stop_at_eos` flag
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

The `stop_at_eos` flag in `llama3_subdevices/text_demo.py` currently never stops the decode loop even after the `eos` token has been generated. The screenshot below illustrates this issue:

<img width="1634" height="1788" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ca8e7613-a756-4461-b2b8-1f13be7df384" />

The flag controlling the decode loop `users_decoding` does get set to False, but is reset to True before the loop can be broken.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run any `text_demo` pytest with `--stop_at_eos=True`.

### Expected behavior

The loop will continue to run until `max_generated_tokens` even if `eos` has been produced.

Issue #25818: Add ability to group token2Expert values
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24723 implements Token2Expert [1, B, S, E (/D)] tensor. It indicates if a token was activated by a given on expert on a given device; if so the entry is the TopK score for that expert, 0 if not.

### Describe the solution you'd like
Since matmul is most efficient when run in tiles, the ideal granularity for sparsity on the height dimension is 16/32 (tiny tile vs full tile).

To accomplish this, we need a R:1 reduce where input is:
Token2Expert [1, B, S, E (/D)] tensor

Output is
Token2Expert [1, B, S/R, E (/D)].

Here, the reduction can be count non-zero, logical OR or `sum`. Note: `sum` is acceptable as long as the values are guaranteed to be not negative.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

Described here https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DbwoV6neTNAgEGVzzGEKOXwJm6MnnQUfMQ4f5OQjFJc/edit?tab=t.0
and here https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24405#issuecomment-3045488116

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25815: Make pow function round outputs to-nearest bfloat16 instead of truncating it.
------------------------------------------------------------
PR #25025 significantly improved the accuracy of `pow`.

Right now, some values are within 1 ULP of error from golden outputs. This 1ULP error sometimes happen because storing float32 data from LRegs to bfloat16 DST registers truncates values. https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-llk/issues/494

To fix this, we want to round output to bfloat16 before writing it to DST register. This means adding a `is_fp32_dest_acc_en ` template parameter to pow-related LLK functions and performing this round-to-nearest when false (similar to sqrt-related functions).

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25799: To Torch Dispatching Hanging
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

- When performing slicing using `inp_tt[:, 1:2]` on a large TTNN tensor (`(2048*32, 2)`), the program hangs during `ttnn.to_torch()` call.
- No output is printed, and the device does not exit or throw an error.
- Some modifications can avoid the issue:
1. Changing the slice to inp_tt[:, 0:1] runs successfully.
2. Reducing the input tensor size, e.g. (2048*16, 2), also avoids the hang.

<img width="1456" height="377" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/08e42e09-8e95-4742-8d9f-9cb10526a267" />

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the following python script:

```python
import torch
import ttnn

device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=0)
inp_shape = (2048*32, 2)
memory_config = ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG

print("Creating input tensor")
inp = torch.rand(inp_shape)
print("Converting to TTNN tensor")
inp_tt = ttnn.from_torch(inp, memory_config=memory_config, device=device)
print("Running Getitem")
output = inp_tt[:, 1:2]
print("Converting to torch")
output = ttnn.to_torch(output)
print(output)
```

2. Observe that execution hangs at the **slicing step** and never prints the final output

### Expected behavior

- The `output` is successfully printed to the terminal.
- The device closes gracefully after execution.

### Please complete the following environment information

- Platform: `Ubuntu 22.04` / `Wormhole`
- Image: `ghcr.io/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tt-metalium-ubuntu-22.04-release-amd64:v0.59.0-rc57`
- Tag: `v0.59.0`

Issue #25786: Investigate performance of volatile ptr vs mem barrier for risc writing to L1
------------------------------------------------------------
In kernels, when writing to L1, we normally always use volatile pointers to ensure writes are committed without reordering/caching so that ex other threads or noc will always get the updated value.

We should investigate using memory barriers like `asm volatile ("" ::: "memory")` instead, which could allow the compiler to better optimize the writes as opposed to volatile, which requires each write to be issued immediately.

An example is this PR https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25657, which switches to volatile pointers to avoid issues. The compiler ends up emitting less optimized code that issues more L1 writes since the pointer is volatile, whereas previously the compiler was able to combine certain writes together. As a result, we're manually combining the writes in the pr to reduce the affects on perf. We could use memory barriers instead to allow for more optimal compilation and reduce these manual optimizations.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25772: Graph capture related hangs
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Op constraint / op runtime queries often put device in a bad state. As a workaround we perform a reset (for example, https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-mlir/blob/main/test/unittests/OpModel/TTNN/Lib/TestOpModelLib.cpp#L1523). Otherwise, the tests may hang. 

A similar issue was reported in #24010 and https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25337 fixes it. But there are other hangs that are of the same nature.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Checkout the branch `gfeng/test-20250721` @ 229d3b4e0a4e10ca207fbc2018d074ec2bc0debb 
2. Build: `./build_metal.sh --build-tests`
3. Run `./build/test/ttnn/unit_tests_ttnn --gtest_filter="Reshape*"`.


### Expected behavior

Op constraint / op runtime queries should be called multiple times, without hanging, and without device resets in between them.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

Issue #25770: Step 4: Control Plane New Routing Table Generation
------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 4: Routing Table Generation

## Overview

Update the routing table generation to use the mapped logical-physical view formed from the logical and physical graph alignment. The routing tables should be generated using physical chip IDs and Ethernet channels for information in order to map to firmware correctly.

## Current State

Currently, routing tables are generated based on the logical representation alone, using directional routing for both intra-mesh and inter-mesh communication. The current system generates:

1. **Intra-Mesh Routing Tables**: For communication between chips within the same mesh using directions for next hop
2. **Inter-Mesh Routing Tables**: For communication between chips across different meshes using directions for next hop

**Current Intra-Mesh Routing Table (2×2 Mesh)**
- Uses directions for next hop

| Source | Dest 0 | Dest 1 | Dest 2 | Dest 3 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Chip 0 | C | E | S | E |
| Chip 1 | W | C | W | S |
| Chip 2 | N | N | C | E |
| Chip 3 | W | N | W | C |

**Legend**: C=Center, N=North, S=South, E=East, W=West

**Current Inter-Mesh Routing Table (2 Meshes, 2×2 each)**
- Uses directions for next hop

| Source | Dest Mesh 0 | Dest Mesh 1 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| M0C0 | C | E |
| M0C1 | W | E |
| M0C2 | N | E |
| M0C3 | N | E |
| M1C0 | W | C |
| M1C1 | W | E |
| M1C2 | W | N |
| M1C3 | W | N |

**Legend**: M0C0 = Mesh 0 Chip 0, C=Center, N=North, S=South, E=East, W=West

**Current Limitations:**
- Intermesh routing uses directions unnecessarily
- No flexibility in physical layout (directions lock mapping to physical connections)
- Mesh and Chip ID gives no flexibility in connectivity

## Requirements

### New Routing Table Generation

The Routing tables should be generated using the mapped view formed from the **Logical and Physical Graph.** Should use physical chip ids and ethernet channels for information in order to map to firmware correctly.

### Example Generation

<img width="2468" height="1180" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0f7627dd-d770-44dd-ad46-bf230138bea0" />

### Diagram: 2 Hosts · 4 Meshes (2 chips each)

The figure shows eight physical chips:

- **Graph 1** (Logical Graph) (1 MGD file)
    - **Mesh 0** (blue): P1, P2
    - **Mesh 1** (green): P3, P4
- **Graph 2** (lower band) (Logical Graph) (1 MGD file)
    - **Mesh 2** (wheat): P5, P6
    - **Mesh 3** (purple): P7, P8

Edge labels are **src chan / dst chan**:

- **0/0** = intra‑mesh link
- **3/3** = inter‑mesh link *within* a host
- **2/2** = inter‑host gateway link

Assume the channel ids to be the same from src and dst for simplicity

### Example Routing‑Table Snippets

#### Intra‑Mesh (Mesh 0 on Host A)

| Src Chip | Dst Chip | Out Chan |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **P1** | P2 | **0** |
| **P2** | P1 | **0** |

#### Inter‑Mesh (within Host A)

| Src Chip | Src Chan | Dest Mesh | Next Hop `<Chip, Chan>` |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **P2** | **3** | Mesh 1 | ⟶ <P3, 3> |
| **P3** | **3** | Mesh 0 | ⟶ <P2, 3> |

### Key Changes

1. **Use Physical Chip IDs**: Routing tables should reference actual physical chip IDs rather than logical mesh/chip combinations
2. **Ethernet Channel Mapping**: Use actual Ethernet channel information for routing decisions
3. **Mapped View Integration**: Generate routing based on the mapped logical-physical view
4. **Firmware Compatibility**: Ensure routing information maps correctly to firmware interfaces

## Implementation Tasks

- [ ] Update RoutingTableGenerator to use mapped logical-physical view
- [ ] Modify routing table generation to use physical chip IDs
- [ ] Implement Intermesh routing algorithm
- [ ] Implement Intramesh routing algorithm
- [ ] Implement Ethernet channel mapping in routing tables
- [ ] Ensure firmware compatibility of routing information
- [ ] Add validation for routing table correctness
- [ ] Ensure support for multiple logical graphs in routing

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Intermesh routing routes are optimized and does not have congestion
- [ ] Intramesh routing routes are optimized and does not have congestion
- [ ] Routing tables are generated correctly from mapped logical-physical view
- [ ] Physical chip IDs are used in routing tables
- [ ] Ethernet channels are correctly mapped
- [ ] Firmware compatibility is maintained
- [ ] Multiple logical graphs are supported in routing

## Dependencies

- Step 3: Physical to Logical Chip Mapping (mapped view)
- Existing RoutingTableGenerator infrastructure
- Firmware interfaces for routing information
- Physical chip ID and Ethernet channel management

## Future steps
- [ ] Consider dynamic routing

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25769: Step 3: Control Plane Physical to Logical Graph Mapping
------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 3: Physical to Logical Chip Mapping

## Overview

Implement the mapping engine that aligns the discovered physical fabric with the logical MeshGraph requirements. This stage enables multiple logical mesh graphs to coexist on the same physical infrastructure through resource allocation and port mapping, allowing routing and control-plane services to operate on a single, coherent view.

## Current State

Currently, the system uses a one-to-one mapping with rigid validation where the physical layout must exactly match the logical requirements specified in the MGD file. This approach lacks flexibility and prevents multiple logical topologies from sharing the same physical fabric.

## Requirements

### Objectives

- Verify physical fabric has sufficient capacity for logical topology
- Map each logical `FabricNodeId` to a physical `PhysicalChipID`
- Map Ethernet-port bindings to satisfy connectivity requirements in Logical Map
- Support multiple logical graphs on single physical fabric

### Example Configuration

Below is a diagram of an example configuration. This would be 2 separate users that have allocated a multi-mesh graph across 2 devices, that have been allocated onto 2 devices. Each Graph will have a MGD file, but live on the same physical graph.

<img width="1310" height="973" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/57dcc7ce-47d4-4ca1-8f50-0138b5179b0a" />

### Mapping Strategies

#### Config-Driven Mapping

**When to use:** Production racks with fixed cable plans or partial fabrics that cannot be inferred automatically.

**Example layout:**
```yaml
# mapping_config.yaml

logical_to_physical:
  M0D0:   3
  M0D1:   2
  M1D0:   5
port_overrides:
  - src: [M0D0, E, 0]     # force logical edge to specific wires for intermesh
    dst: [M1D0, W, 1]
```

### Data Structure Considerations

**Potential Edge/Node Hierarchy:**
| Base Class | Subclasses | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Edge** | Physical Edge<br>Logical Edge<br>Mapped Edge | Mapped Edge inherits from both Physical and Logical |
| **Node** | Physical Node<br>Logical Node<br>Mapped Node | Mapped Node inherits from both Physical and Logical |

We could also consider re-using some of the infrastructure defined in the Physical graph and consolidate the Edge and Chip structure.

## Implementation Tasks

- [ ] Design mapping engine architecture
- [ ] Implement config-driven mapping parser
- [ ] Create mapping validation logic
- [ ] Implement capacity verification
- [ ] Save information for physical to logical mapping in data structure
- [ ] Add support for multiple logical graphs
- [ ] Add error handling for mapping failures

### Future
- [ ] Create mapping visualization tools
- [ ] Implement mapping optimization algorithms
- [ ] Create mapping configuration templates

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Physical fabric capacity is correctly verified against logical requirements
- [ ] Valid Logical FabricNodeIds can be successfully mapped to physical PhysicalChipIDs in more than one way
- [ ] Ethernet-port bindings satisfy logical connectivity requirements
- [ ] Multiple logical graphs can be mapped to single physical fabric
- [ ] Mapping validation catches invalid configurations
- [ ] Mapping performance is acceptable for large fabrics
- [ ] Mapping results are consistent and deterministic
- [ ] Error handling provides clear feedback for mapping failures

## Dependencies

- Step 1: Physical Chip Discovery (PhysicalGraph)
- Step 2: Logical Chip Discovery (MeshGraph)
- Existing FabricNodeId and PhysicalChipID structures
- Ethernet port management infrastructure

## Future Considerations
### Automatic Mapping

Potentially allow automatic allocation of physical resources based on resource constraints.

**Research References:**
- **"VF2++: An Improved Subgraph Isomorphism Algorithm"** – Pattern Recognition Letters 2018. (Exact mapping) [ScienceDirect](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0166218X18300829?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- **Glasgow Subgraph Solver GitHub Repository** – Source code and benchmarks. (Exact/CP) [GitHub](https://github.com/ciaranm/glasgow-subgraph-solver?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- **"AI‑Empowered Virtual Network Embedding: A Comprehensive Survey"** – IEEE Comms Surveys & Tutorials 2024. (Heuristic/ML approaches) [ACM Digital Library](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1109/COMST.2024.3424533?utm_source=chatgpt.com)
- **"Topology‑Aware Task Mapping for Reducing Communication Contention on Large Parallel Machines"** – BG/L case study, SC conference paper. (Simulated annealing on torus) [ResearchGate](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/4245406_Topology-aware_task_mapping_for_reducing_communication_contention_on_large_parallel_machines?utm_source=chatgpt.com)

Issue #25767: Step 2: Control Plane Logical Graph Generation
------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2: Logical Graph Generation (Mesh Graph Descriptor & Generation)

## Overview

Evolve the Mesh Graph Descriptor (MGD) format and MeshGraph generation to support logical-physical separation. The MGD file will no longer retain physical topology specific specifications and will only describe the logical layout of the chip, enabling mapping to any physical topology with sufficient connections.

## Current State

The current Mesh Graph Descriptor file is tied to the physical layout of the system and specifies:

- Architecture and Ethernet Ports of the Chip
- Chip Layout on board
- Mesh topology comprised of boards
- Intermesh connectivity with specific directional connections

**Current MGD Example:**
```cpp
ChipSpec: {
  arch: wormhole_b0,
  ethernet_ports: {
    N: 2,
    E: 2,
    S: 2,
    W: 2,
  }
}

Board: [
  { name: 1x2, // Board type
    type: Mesh, 
    topology: [1, 2]} // Board Shape
]

Mesh: [
{
  id: 0,
  board:  1x2,
  device_topology: [1, 2],
  host_topology: [1, 1],
  host_ranks: [[0]]},
{
  id: 1, // Mesh ID
  board: 1x2, // Board type
  device_topology: [1, 2], // Mesh Shape
  host_topology: [1, 1], // Device Topology
  host_ranks: [[0]]}, // Host IDs
]

Graph: [
  [[0, E0], [1, W0]], // Mesh ID, Direction/Port
  [[0, E1], [1, W1]],
  ...
]
```

**Current Limitations:**
- Allows no flexibility in connectivity, must be cabled exactly as specified
- Intermesh connectivity is hard to determine based on layout
- Tied to a physical topology, cannot be used on a different physical topology

## Requirements

### New MGD Format

Mesh Graph Descriptor no longer retains physical topology specific specifications. Only describes the logical layout of the chip. And should be able to **map to any physical topology with enough connections**

**New MGD Example:**
```cpp
ChipSpec: {
  arch: wormhole_b0,
  ethernet_ports: {
    N: 2,
    E: 2,
    S: 2,
    W: 2,
  }
}

Mesh: [
{
  id: 0,
  topology: [4, 8], // Topology of chips
  host_ranks: [[0, 1]]}, // Multi-host 
{
  id: 1, // Mesh ID
  topology: [4, 4]
  host_ranks: [[0]]},
]

Graph: [
  [M0, M1, 2], // SRC Mesh ID, DST Mesh ID, num links
  [M1, M2, 2]
  ...
]

Constraints: [
{
   [M0D1, M0D2, E], // SRC Fabric Node ID, DST Fabric Node ID, Direction
```

**Key Differences:**
- Can be mapped onto **any physical topology** of devices in the same chip spec
- Contains no device specific information
- Optional Constraints can be added to constrain the graph

### MeshGraph Generation

Mesh Graph generation will re-use mostly what we have in `MeshGraph`, with a few key changes.

1. Connections in inter-mesh connectivity will only specify mesh id connections
2. Intermesh connectivity will no longer have a direction

**Connectivity Data Structures**

| Structure | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `IntraMeshConnectivity` | 3D structure: [mesh_id][chip_id][connected_chip_id] → RouterEdge |
| `InterMeshConnectivity` | 3D structure: [mesh_id][connected_mesh_id] → RouterEdge |

**ChipSpec**

| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `arch` | Chip architecture type (e.g., WORMHOLE_B0) |
| `num_eth_ports_per_direction` | Number of Ethernet ports in each direction (N/E/S/W) |
| `num_z_ports` | Number of Z-direction ports |

**RouterEdge**

| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `port_direction` | Direction of the connection (N/E/S/W/C/NONE) (Only needed for Intramesh) |
| `connected_chip_ids` | List of connected chip IDs fabric node ID |
| `weight` | Cost metric for routing decisions |

## Implementation Tasks

- [ ] Design new MGD format specification
- [ ] Update MGD parser to handle new format
- [ ] Remove board-specific information from MGD processing
- [ ] Update MeshGraph generation to use new format
- [ ] Modify InterMeshConnectivity to remove directional constraints
- [ ] Implement constraints handling in MGD parser
- [ ] Update validation logic for new MGD format
- [ ] Create migration utilities for existing MGD files
- [ ] Update documentation for new MGD format
- [ ] Add backward compatibility layer if needed

## Success Criteria

- [ ] MGD files can describe logical topologies without physical constraints
- [ ] New MGD format supports flexible mesh topologies
- [ ] MeshGraph generation works with new format
- [ ] Intermesh connectivity no longer requires directional specifications
- [ ] Optional constraints are properly handled
- [ ] Migration path exists for existing MGD files
- [ ] Documentation is updated and clear
- [ ] Validation works correctly with new format
- [ ] Performance is maintained or improved

## Dependencies

- Current MGD file format (for migration)

## Technical Notes

### Data Structure Considerations

**Potential Edge/Node Hierarchy:**
| Base Class | Subclasses | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Edge** | Physical Edge<br>Logical Edge<br>Mapped Edge | Mapped Edge inherits from both Physical and Logical |
| **Node** | Physical Node<br>Logical Node<br>Mapped Node | Mapped Node inherits from both Physical and Logical |

We could also consider re-using some of the infrastructure defined in the Physical graph and consolidate the Edge and Chip structure.

- Consider validation tools for new MGD format

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25766: Step 1: Control Plane Graph Physical Chip Discovery
------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Physical Chip Discovery

## Overview

Implement automated physical chip discovery and graph representation that discovers the actual hardware topology by enumerating devices, probing Ethernet links, and building a physical representation of the fabric. This includes chip enumeration, link status verification, and host classification.

## Current State

Currently, physical chip discovery happens after logical generation and is used to validate against logical requirements. This limits flexibility and prevents the system from adapting to different physical topologies.

## Requirements

### Flow

1. **Enumerate Devices** – Query each host through the UMD Cluster API to list attached Tenstorrent chips and capture per‑chip metadata.
2. **Probe Ethernet Links** – Check every Ethernet port's link‑status and record its peer ID through BFS
3. **Classify Links** – Mark each link as **local** (same host) or **remote** (different host).
4. **Build the PhysicalGraph** – Add discovered chips as nodes and Ethernet links as edges.

### Graph Representation

**PhysicalGraph**

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `nodes` | `Dict<ChipID, PhysicalChip>` | All discovered chips |
| `edges` | `List<EthLink>` | All Ethernet links |
| `hosts` | `Dict<HostID, Host>` | Optional host metadata |
| `architecture` | `string` | `wormhole_b0`, etc. |
| Metadata |  | Rest of the information about the physical devices |
| **Methods** |  |  |
| `add_node`, `add_edge` | — | Mutation helpers |
| `neighbors(chip_id)` | `List<ChipID>` | Adjacent chips |
| `bfs(start_id)` | `Iterator<ChipID>` | Breadth‑first traversal |

**PhysicalChip (Node)**

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `physical_chip_id` | `uint32` | Physical ID |
| `ethernet_ports` | `List<EthPort>` | Direction & index |
| `host_id` | `uint16` | Owning host |
| Metadata |  |  |

**EthLink (Edge)**

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `src_chip_id` | `uint32` | Source chip |
| `src_chan_id` | `(dir, idx)` | `(E, 0)` |
| `dst_chip_id` | `uint32` | Destination chip |
| `dst_port` | `(dir, idx)` | `(W, 1)` |
| `weight` | `float` | Cost metric |
| `is_remote` | `bool` | Inter‑host flag |
| `status` | `Enum` | `UP / DOWN / DEGRADE` |

## MPI Compatibility
Syncing across hosts with MPI will require us to serialize the data, ensure that this data structure is serializable.
there's an example in the IntermeshLinkTable that gets transferred today

## Implementation Tasks

- [ ] Implement device enumeration using UMD Cluster API
- [ ] Implement Ethernet link probing and status checking
- [ ] Implement link classification (local vs remote)
- [ ] Create PhysicalGraph data structure
- [ ] Create PhysicalChip node representation
- [ ] Create EthLink edge representation
- [ ] Implement graph traversal methods (BFS, neighbors)
- [ ] Add host metadata collection
- [ ] Implement error handling for discovery failures
- [ ] Add logging and debugging capabilities
- [ ] Add unit tests with Mock UMD Cluster API to test on non-host devices
- [ ] Ensure compatibility with MPI

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Physical chip discovery works independently of logical requirements
- [ ] All attached Tenstorrent chips are discovered and enumerated
- [ ] Ethernet link status is accurately probed and recorded
- [ ] Links are correctly classified as local or remote
- [ ] PhysicalGraph accurately represents the discovered topology
- [ ] Graph traversal methods work correctly
- [ ] Performance is acceptable for large fabric sizes
- [ ] Discovery results are consistent across multiple runs
- [ ] Can test physical discovery without real hardware
- [ ] Make sure the data structure is serializable

## Dependencies

- UMD Cluster API for device discovery

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25765: New Mesh Graph and Routing Table Generation and Validation
------------------------------------------------------------
# Physical Graph Discovery and Control Plane MGD Mapping - Overview

## Overview

This epic outlines the system's graph discovery and control plane mapping architecture, transitioning to a logical-physical separation model for system specification and control.

### Summary of Current vs. Proposed Flow Differences

#### Current Flow
1. **Generate `MeshGraph` from Mesh Graph Descriptor file** - Creates logical representation tied to specific physical topology
2. **Generate Intermesh and Intramesh routing tables using `RoutingTableGenerator`** - Creates routing tables based on logical representation
3. **Run physical chip discovery and map logical representation to physical topology** - Discovers hardware and validates against rigid logical requirements
4. **Validate physical connections against mesh graph requirements** - Ensures physical matches logical exactly

**Key Characteristics:**
- MGD files are tightly coupled to specific physical topologies
- No flexibility in connectivity - must be cabled exactly as specified
- Intermesh connectivity is hard to determine from layout
- Cannot reuse logical configurations across different physical setups

#### Proposed Flow
1. **Run physical chip discovery and create physical graph representation** - Discovers actual hardware topology first
2. **Create parallel logical `MeshGraph` representation from Mesh Graph descriptor file** - Generates flexible logical representation
3. **Map Physical graph representation to Logical graph representation using mapping config file** - Aligns physical and logical through mapping
   - Validate that physical representation can satisfy logical requirements
4. **Create intermesh and intramesh routing tables based on logical representation of the graph** - Generates routing using mapped view

**Key Characteristics:**
- Physical discovery happens first, enabling flexible mapping
- MGD files describe logical requirements without physical constraints
- Support for multiple logical graphs on single physical fabric
- Automatic or config-driven mapping between logical and physical topologies
- Enhanced flexibility for resource allocation and multi-tenant scenarios

#### Key Architectural Changes

**MGD File Evolution:**
- **Current**: Contains physical topology specifications (board types, device topologies, directional connections)
- **Proposed**: Contains only logical topology requirements (mesh shapes, connection counts, optional constraints)

**Intermesh Connectivity:**
- **Current**: Uses directional routing with specific port assignments
- **Proposed**: Uses mesh-based routing without directional constraints

**Mapping Strategy:**
- **Current**: One-to-one mapping with rigid validation
- **Proposed**: Flexible mapping supporting multiple logical graphs per physical fabric

**Resource Allocation:**
- **Current**: Fixed allocation based on MGD specifications
- **Proposed**: Dynamic allocation with automatic or config-driven mapping

## Implementation Steps

### Step 1: Physical Chip Discovery
- **Ticket**: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25766
- **Description**: Implement automated physical chip discovery and graph representation
- **Dependencies**: None

### Step 2: Logical Chip Discovery (Mesh Graph Descriptor & Generation)
- **Ticket**: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25767
- **Description**: Evolve MGD format and MeshGraph generation for logical-physical separation
- **Dependencies**: None

### Step 3: Physical to Logical Chip Mapping
- **Ticket**: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25769
- **Description**: Implement mapping engine for aligning physical and logical representations
- **Dependencies**: Step 1, Step 2

### Step 4: Routing Table Generation
- **Ticket**: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/25770
- **Description**: Update routing table generation to use mapped logical-physical view
- **Dependencies**: Step 3

### Step 5: Device Reset Signal
- **Description**: Come up with system to signal device reset in multi-host, multi-tenant systems

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Physical chip discovery works independently of logical requirements
- [ ] MGD files can describe logical topologies without physical constraints
- [ ] Routing tables are generated correctly from mapped logical-physical view
- [ ] System supports Config-driven mapping
- [ ] Multi-host - multi-tenant scenarios are supported

## Future features

- [ ] Multiple logical graphs can be mapped to single physical fabric
- [ ] System supports automatic mapping

## Dependencies

- Existing MeshGraph and RoutingTableGenerator infrastructure
- UMD Cluster API for device enumeration
- Current MGD file format (for migration path)

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25737: Unwanted dependency on HF transformers within `ttnn/ttnn/torch_tracer.py`
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

[copied text from a Slack message here; [here](https://tenstorrent.slack.com/archives/C06JU0GUNGY/p1753365941607219) is the original bug report on Slack]

> 1. Dependency on transformers
In [this PR](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24966), I noticed that the transformers Python package is now used in ttnn/ttnn/torch_tracer.py. Since it’s being imported directly within the TTNN library, shouldn’t transformers be added as a dependency in pyproject.toml?
Currently, when installing the TTNN library via pip install -e . (from setup.py) and running import ttnn, I get a ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'transformers'.
I’ve handled this by explicitly adding transformers to the requirements of the minimum Python environment used for running TTNN tutorials, but I’m wondering whether it should be listed as a general package dependency instead.

This dependency is unwanted and we should look into removing it.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Go to 'tt-metal' directory
2. `pip install -e .`
3. `import ttnn` with Python
4. See error `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'transformers'`


### Expected behavior

TTNN should not have dependency on HF

### Please complete the following environment information

This dependency is observed on `main` branch.

Issue #25730: TTNN Dev package usage experience
------------------------------------------------------------
Played around with the newly added ttnn package, sharing my experience here.

First, installed `ttnn-runtime` and `ttnn-dev` packages into a standalone solution:
```sh
cmake --install /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/third_party/tt-metal/src/tt-metal/build --prefix /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/add/ttnn-install --component ttnn-runtime
cmake --install /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/third_party/tt-metal/src/tt-metal/build --prefix /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/add/ttnn-install --component ttnn-dev
```

([This](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-mlir/tree/svuckovic/repro-ttnn-pkg-issues/add) is the standalone dir used, minus the `ttnn-install` dir, which is ~1.3G)

### Error 1

Trying to build the solution first errored out with:
```
CMake Error at /opt/ttmlir-toolchain/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/cmake/data/share/cmake-4.0/Modules/CMakeFindDependencyMacro.cmake:78 (find_package):
  By not providing "FindTT-Metalium.cmake" in CMAKE_MODULE_PATH this project
  has asked CMake to find a package configuration file provided by
  "TT-Metalium", but CMake did not find one.

  Could not find a package configuration file provided by "TT-Metalium" with
  any of the following names:

    TT-MetaliumConfig.cmake
    tt-metalium-config.cmake

  Add the installation prefix of "TT-Metalium" to CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH or set
  "TT-Metalium_DIR" to a directory containing one of the above files.  If
  "TT-Metalium" provides a separate development package or SDK, be sure it
  has been installed.
Call Stack (most recent call first):
  ttnn-install/lib/cmake/tt-nn/tt-nn-config.cmake:30 (find_dependency)
  CMakeLists.txt:26 (find_package)
```

Fixed that by installing the `metalium-dev` component:
```
cmake --install /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/third_party/tt-metal/src/tt-metal/build --prefix /localdev/svuckovic/_workspace/repos/tt-mlir/add/ttnn-install --component metalium-dev
```

### Error 2

After that, building yielded another error:
```
CMake Error at /opt/ttmlir-toolchain/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/cmake/data/share/cmake-4.0/Modules/CMakeFindDependencyMacro.cmake:78 (find_package):
```

Issue #25718: Higher from_torch/to_torch latency on multi-device impacting FPS
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

When running on a multi-device setup (WH N300), from_torch and to_torch ops take significantly more time compared to a single device. This increased latency is impacting the overall model FPS and performance.

Observations:
**On WH (N300):**
  - [Single Device] Input tensor shape [2, 3, 640, 640]
    - from_torch time: 0.000803 secs
    - to_torch time: 0.000787 secs
  - [Multi Device] Input tensor shape [1, 3, 640, 640]
    - from_torch time: 0.002397 secs
    - to_torch time: 0.003391 secs
    

1. from_torch latency is ~2.98x higher on multi-device.
2. to_torch latency is ~4.96x higher on multi-device.

This overhead contributes directly to reduced FPS during model e2e inference.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Pick the test from [commit](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/compare/main...keerthana/mesh_mapper_profile)
2. Run the following tests, 
    - For Single device test, run `pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_to_from_torch_with_mapper.py::test_single_device_to_and_from_torch`
    - For Multi device test, run `pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_to_from_torch_with_mapper.py::test_multi_device_to_and_from_torch`
    

### Expected behavior

from_torch and to_torch ops on multi-device should have latency close to single-device runs, with at most a modest overhead (~1.5× – 2×), not 3× – 5× slower.

### Please complete the following environment information

OS: [e.g. Ubuntu 20.04]
Devices: WH(n300)

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25713: [Issue]: nonzero returns shape like input tensor and separate count, complicating index extraction
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

The current behavior of ttnn.nonzero returns two tensors:
1. A tensor holding the count of nonzero elements, padded to the input tensor's shape.
2. A tensor with indices of nonzero elements, also shaped like the input tensor.

This deviates from the expected behavior seen in torch.nonzero, which directly returns a compact list of nonzero indices without redundant padding or the need for count slicing.

**Example**
Given:
`A = [8, 1, 2, 0, 4, 0] `
Expected output (similar to torch.nonzero(A)):
`[0, 1, 2, 4] `
Current ttnn.nonzero output:
 - tensor[0]: count tensor → [4, 0, 0, 0, ..., 0].
 - tensor[1]: index tensor → [0, 1, 2, 4, 0, 0, ..., 0] (same shape as input).

To extract the correct result, we must:
 1. Transfer the count tensor back to the host.
 2. Slice the index tensor accordingly.
This introduces a performance bottleneck due to unnecessary host dispatch and postprocessing.
Reproducible Code:
```
import torch
import ttnn
torch.manual_seed(0)
# Setup
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=0)
input_shape = torch.Size([32])
torch_input_tensor = torch.ones(input_shape)
torch_input_tensor[::2] = 0  # set even-indexed elements to 0
input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT, device=device)
# Apply ttnn.nonzero
output_tensor = ttnn.nonzero(input_tensor, queue_id=0)
# Retrieve count (requires host dispatch)
output_tensor1 = ttnn.to_layout(output_tensor[0], ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT)
output_tensor1 = ttnn.from_device(output_tensor1)
output_tensor1 = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor1)
nonzero_count = output_tensor1[..., 0].item()
# Extract actual indices
index_tensor = output_tensor[1][:nonzero_count]
```
### Desired Behavior
ttnn.nonzero should ideally mimic the PyTorch API by returning a compact tensor of only the nonzero indices (i.e., output.shape == [N] where N is the number of nonzero elements). This eliminates:
- The need for host-side slicing
- Redundant data transfer
- Extra logic to extract the result

### Describe the solution you'd like

Consider modifying the API to return only the relevant tensor with desired shape. That is, the behavior should be like `torch.nonzero` (https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nonzero.html).

### Describe alternatives you've considered

We understand that dynamic tensor allocation is not yet supported due to architectural constraints in `tt-metal`, which leads to the current static, preallocated output format. What can be a suggested workaround solution for now?

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25699: More IDevice API removal in TTNN
------------------------------------------------------------
here is a huge chunk you can delete in ttnn/api/ttnn/device_operation.hpp as well, but perhaps you can do in a follow up
E.g. in:
```
template <DeviceOperationConcept device_operation_t>
typename device_operation_t::tensor_return_value_t launch_on_single_device(
    QueueId cq_id,
    const typename device_operation_t::operation_attributes_t& operation_attributes,
    const typename device_operation_t::tensor_args_t& tensor_args) {
    ZoneScopedN("Launch Device Operation");

    auto tensor_return_value = device_operation_t::create_output_tensors(operation_attributes, tensor_args);
    if (!mesh_device_operation_utils::all_tensors_have_uniform_storage(tensor_args)) {
        mesh_device_operation_utils::filter_tensor_shards(
            mesh_device_operation_utils::extract_tensor_coordinates(tensor_args), tensor_return_value);
    }

    auto first_tensor = tt::stl::reflection::get_first_object_of_type<Tensor>(tensor_args);
    if (auto mesh_device = first_tensor.mesh_device(); mesh_device != nullptr) {
        using MeshCompatibleOp = MeshDeviceOperationAdapter<device_operation_t>;
        launch_operation_with_adapter<MeshCompatibleOp>(
            cq_id, operation_attributes, tensor_args, tensor_return_value, mesh_device);
    } else {
        auto device = first_tensor.device();
        launch_on_worker_thread<device_operation_t>(
            cq_id, operation_attributes, tensor_args, tensor_return_value, device);
    }
    return tensor_return_value;
}

```

launch_on_worker_thread branch and everything downstream can be deleted

Issue #25670: Allow host-triggered DMAs to buffers on the device using ttnn
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently ttnn clients must write to the hardware using a command queue, which either requires copies or requires that the host memory be mapped into the iATU on the device (which is limited to 4GB total memory). If ttnn clients could use WriteToBuffer to upload their data, that could be more efficient because that uses host-triggered DMA which has fewer restrictions.

Issue #25664: Allow format conversions when writing data to buffers
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently ttnn clients need to do format conversions before doing copy_host_to_device_tensor. If the EnqueueWrite*Buffer* functions allowed specifying an input format that format conversion would be unnecessary.

Internally, the conversion could be implemented either by doing it instead of the memcpy to the hugepage, or by performing it on a dispatch core in the device (preferably using tensix).

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25660: Make Read/Write APIs consistent
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

We have several places where the read/write APIs are inconsistent or unexpected, and this is causing confusion with those developing and consuming our code. Specific issues raised by multiple sources:

> EnqueueWriteBuffer is cq, dst, src and EnqueueReadBuffer is cq, src, dst

> noc_async_write and noc_async_read have the orders inverted

> Mesh CQs

> Buffer::create, HWCommandQueue

There is a migration of calls to the TT-Mesh APIs going on (https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/22835), but I would like to see a specific discussion/tracker identifying line items for "API consistency". I want to get these enumerated and synced, so this is to solicit feedback and collate a list of what needs to be changed, deprecated, etc. Thanks!

CC: @TT-BrianLiu @omilyutin-tt @cfjchu @ayerofieiev-tt @tt-asaigal

_No response_

Issue #25656: [Bug Report] ttnn.add + ttnn.embedding - Data mismatch
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug
The ttnn.add and ttnn.emedding throws assertion error because of data mismatch between PyTorch and TTNN output when the indices tensor contains -1  values is added with offset and passed as input to embedding op. 

For more context, here is the exact error message
```

        pcc_passed, pcc_message = comp_pcc(expected_pytorch_result, actual_pytorch_result, pcc)
>       assert pcc_passed, construct_pcc_assert_message(pcc_message, expected_pytorch_result, actual_pytorch_result)
E       AssertionError: 0.017874982399147178
```

To Reproduce

Run the following test:

```
import torch
import ttnn
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc
from models.utility_functions import torch_random
def test_opt_position_embedding(device):
    torch.manual_seed(1234)

    torch_input_tensor = torch.randint(0, 10, (1, 10))
    torch_input_tensor[:, 5:] = -1
    torch_offset_tensor = torch.tensor(2).unsqueeze(-1)
    torch_weights = torch_random((2050, 2048), -0.1, 0.1, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    
    torch_input_tensor+=torch_offset_tensor
    torch_output_tensor = torch.nn.functional.embedding(torch_input_tensor, torch_weights)

    input_tensor = ttnn.to_device(ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor), device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    offset = ttnn.to_device(ttnn.from_torch(torch_offset_tensor), device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    weights = ttnn.to_device(ttnn.from_torch(torch_weights, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16), device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)

    output_tensor = ttnn.add(input_tensor, offset)
    output_tensor = ttnn.embedding(output_tensor, weights, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    assert_with_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor)
```

Expected behavior
The data mismatch between PyTorch and TTNN output should be resolved.

Issue #25652: Add the ability to subscript a MeshCoordinate in python without converting to tuple
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Currently we get the error `Cannot subscript a MeshCoordinate object`. We can do this in C++ so reckon it's just a pybind to expose it. Right now we do this:

`tuple(mesh_device.shape)[0]`



### Describe the solution you'd like

Pybind the MeshCoord subscript operator in C++ to Python.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

Issue #25647: SFPI returns wrong results after long sequence of unrelated instructions (e.g. SFPNOP) on Blackhole
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

To give some context: We have been working on improving the accuracy of some numerical operations (e.g. pow, exp, ...) by re-designing their implementation (c.f. https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25025 )

While the new approximation of the power function works fine on Wormhole, it produces non-sensical results on Blackhole. This is preventing this approximation from being ported on blackhole.

I have been able to narrow down the issue down to a few simple snippets: 
One of them is a simple [`test_condition_bug(base, pow)`](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/f3c44a0ea437a167d05180d6a7c52cb167b5b970/tt_metal/hw/ckernels/blackhole/metal/llk_api/llk_sfpu/ckernel_sfpu_binary.h#L235), that works as folllows:

```
// if POW = 2.0, return value should be 2.f as well
sfpi_inline sfpi::vFloat test_conditional_bug(sfpi::vFloat base, sfpi::vFloat pow) {
    
    // Sequence of 186 SFPNOP
    __builtin_rvtt_sfpnop();
    // ... 
    __builtin_rvtt_sfpnop();

    return pow;
}
```

This function should always return `pow` (in our test, `pow` is passed as `2.f`). 
If we have < 185 SFPNOP/`__builtin_rvtt_sfpnop` instructions then it is indeed what we get. However, with 186, we get a completely wrong result (e.g. `0.f`)

The following observations have been made:
-  I have not noticed any compiler warning or error. The aforementioned code works fine on Wormhole, just not on Blackhole.
- This is also something I have reproduced on two different instances.
- The bug happens with both bfloat16 and float32 data types.
- Other snippets also exhibit similar behaviors (c.f. ....)
- As per `runtime/sfpi/README.md`, the SFPI version is `6.13.0`. 

After talking with @fvranicTT , I believe this might be a SFPI-related compiler bug. 

For deubbing purposes, I have also attached the disassembled code for the 184 and 185 NOP codes at

I haven't gone through *all* the assembly, and I am not a compiler person. But at glance, I see two 'major' differences between both:
- the 'correct' version has two versions of the same 'snippet'. ((1) at LX and (2) at LX)
- the 'incorrect' version only has one (1'), which looks similar to the (1) snippets from the correct version, except for a single instruction (`sfpload	L0,64,0,7` from Line 734 that is missing from 'incorrect' snippet)

[184nop_ok.s.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21391757/184nop_ok.s.txt)

[185nop_bug.s.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21391760/185nop_bug.s.txt)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

The bug can be reproduced with the following [`test_pow.py`](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/nmaurice/sfpi-branch-bug/sfpi-bug/test_sfpi.py) script on the `nmaurice/sfpi-branch-bug` branch. The snippets in questions are in the [`ckernel_sfpu_binary.h`](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/nmaurice/sfpi-branch-bug/tt_metal/hw/ckernels/blackhole/metal/llk_api/llk_sfpu/ckernel_sfpu_binary.h) file, and are 'replacement' for the `power` SFPI function.

1. `./build_metal.sh --debug --build-tests`
2. `python sfpi-bug/test_sfpi.py`

### Expected behavior

_No response_

### Please complete the following environment information

Bug has been reproduced on several blackhole instance (yyzo-bh-17 and yyzo-bh-19 )

Issue #25632: Matmul / Fused / Reduce Documentation
------------------------------------------------------------
Op Documentation formatting guidelines:
- function prototype
- Brief functional description
- list and description of arguments
- Usage example (not involving Torch)
- Usage limitations (supported dtypes, supported input tensor formats, other limitations)
- Usage detail and any more comprehensive documentation (for OPs that need it)
- References to any longer form tech reports related to the OP

If there are program configs, they should have their own doc pages.


PRs should be related to sub-issues.

Issue #25619: To perform RoPE in tt_transformers, more ttnn APIs are needed to operate ttnn.tensor.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Now I have encountered a problem. The API of `tt_transformers ` is designed in the style of GPT-J, while the `transformers` library is designed in the style of GPT-NeoX. [https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/issues/25199](url)
This leads to some problems when doing RoPE, including that the API of tt-metal does not support the transformation of some matrices.

For example, I am bringing up microsoft/phi-1.5 on Wormhole #19415
The biggest difference of phi-1.5 is the use of `partial rotary embedding`
<img width="726" height="441" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a7e3f15a-fc04-4898-85ef-7032b5653a6e" />

However, this is not possible in tt_transformers because of the GPT-J style. (The GPT-J style rotates two adjacent array pairs, while the GPT-NeoX style rotates two pairs of numbers separated by head_dim/2)
So if I perform `partial rotary embedding` according to the `transformers `in the figure, I must perform a more detailed segmentation on the last dimension in `tt_tensor`.
But `ttnn.chunk` can only be split according to fixed numbers.

On the other hand, can the ttnn.experimental.nlp_create_qkv_heads function be modified to make its QK output conform to the GPT-NeoX style?
<img width="982" height="672" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e96774d4-c4bd-4c14-9066-0cc131a515e2" />


### Steps to reproduce the issue

_No response_

### Expected behavior

_No response_

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25612: Building TT-NN wheel locally might error out
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Running commands that build the TT-NN wheel:
```bash
python setup.py bdist_wheel 
```
or
```bash
python -m build
```

may result in an error:
```
error: can't copy 'build/lib.linux-x86_64-cpython-310/ttnn/build/lib/_ttnn.so': doesn't exist or not a regular file
```


### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run
```
python setup.py bdist_wheel
```
on a lab machine that has a `/usr/lib64` dir.

### Expected behavior

The issue seems to be in this line: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/68b4c185069cc1c032491bea65424d8806fd7d1a/setup.py#L30

Changing it from:
```py
        return "lib64" if os.path.exists("/usr/lib64") else "lib"
```
to
```py
        return "lib"
```

solves the issue.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25606: Tracy need to print `logical_shape`
------------------------------------------------------------
# Background

Currently, tracy print padded tensor shape.

Most ops can work with padded shapes, but reshape-related ops require the exact logical shape.

# How to test

I made simple example for run tracy.
1. branch: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/hyungsuk/tracy_test
2. cmd: ./tt_metal/tools/profiler/profile_this.py -n vit -c "pytest ./tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/test_examples.py"
3. check generated csv with `INPUT_0_X` colum. -> it should be logical shape

# What I expected

```Python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("height", [10])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("width", [20])
def test_example(device, height, width):
    torch.manual_seed(0)

    torch_input_tensor = torch.rand((height, width), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_output_tensor = torch_input_tensor

    input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device)
    output_tensor = ttnn.prim.example(input_tensor)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    assert_equal(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor)
```
this example simply copy [10,20] size input to output
so INPUT_0_X in output should be 20

### Describe the solution you'd like

_No response_

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25602: Enable easy models use through tt-installer for BH QB release
------------------------------------------------------------
## Context

We can't run models out of the box with tt-installer, specifically our fancy Llama model, at `.github/workflows/blackhole-llmbox-demo-tests-impl.yaml:41`, on BH QuietBox, because of various issues like having to clone the repo etc.

There is organizational zeal to get it working out of the box, regardless of the infrastructure or developer quality-of-life implications.

## Why I hate what we're about to do

To be very clear, I hate this because:

- it will further discourage us from taking accountability and cleaning up the artifacts that we should be building and publishing
- it will make current ongoing work by our team to improve quality of life more difficult
- it will encourage customers to go down a path of using metal in a way that is unsustainable and difficult to built on top of
- It will further encourage people to not give teams enough time to go back and clean up their technical debt, leading to more of the same in the future

## What we will do

- We will start with upstream container to tt-installer, which should have the entire kitchen sink of All That Is Metal in there.
- Then replace with @knauth 's changes to the release container, pending CI and review by team.
- We will then validate that this works manually
- No plans to integrate tt-installer-on-BH-QB plans yet. Anthony is working on revamping our release process

cc: @ejouretTT @bkeith-TT @bgoelTT @milank94  @knauth

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25598: Trisc1 build failed during `RTL pass rvtt_replay`
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In the latest version of tt-metal, the following build error occurs under specific circumstances:
- `trisc1 build failed. Log: during RTL pass: rvtt_replay`

I was able to reproduce this issue while modifying the moreh_adam operation.
Although the code may not be semantically correct, I believe the sfpi compiler should not fail during this stage. 

Please refer to the attached build log for more details:
📎 [build_error.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21367172/build_error.log)

Additionally, I have also observed a failure during another RTL pass, which logs the following:
- `trisc1 build failed. Log: during GIMPLE pass: rvtt_synth_renumber`

However, reproducing this second case consistently has been difficult, so I am reporting only the first reproducible issue here.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Clone [reproduce branch](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/yechan/reproduce-trisc-build-error/)
2. Build with debug : `./build_metal.sh --debug`
3. Run moreh adam pytest : `pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/test_moreh_adam.py`
5. See error

The modified sources are located under [moreh_adam](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/yechan/reproduce-trisc-build-error/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/moreh/moreh_adam)

### Expected behavior

The sfpi compiler should output **`trisc1 build failed. Log: during RTL pass: rvtt_replay`** and bunch of disassembly dump.

ex ) 
```
2025-07-22 21:17:13.507 | critical |          Always | trisc1 build failed. Log: during RTL pass: rvtt_replay
dump file: stderr



EMERGENCY DUMP:



_start

Dataflow summary:

...

(note 1 0 38 NOTE_INSN_DELETED)
(note 38 1 2 2 [bb 2] NOTE_INSN_BASIC_BLOCK)
(note 2 38 40 2 NOTE_INSN_FUNCTION_BEG)
(debug_insn 40 2 11071 2 (debug_marker) "/root/tt-metal/tt_metal/hw/firmware/src/trisck.cc":51:5 -1
```

### Please complete the following environment information

- OS: Ubuntu 22.04.4 LTS
- Version of software : 
   - base branch : [main](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal)
   - working branch : [reproduce branch](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/yechan/reproduce-trisc-build-error/)
Arch: wormhole_b0 n300s
