Issue #25577: fp32 reduce does not work with all combinations of watcher and dprint
------------------------------------------------------------
```
import pytest
import torch

import ttnn
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc, assert_allclose
from models.utility_functions import comp_ulp, comp_pcc, comp_allclose


#@pytest.mark.parametrize("dim_a", [2048, 3072, 4096])
#@pytest.mark.parametrize("dim_b", [2048, 3072, 4096])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dim_a", [32])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dim_b", [512])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dtype", [ttnn.bfloat8_b, ttnn.bfloat16], ids=["bfloat8_b", "bfloat16"])
def test_layer_norm0(*, device: ttnn.Device, dim_a: int, dim_b: int, dtype: ttnn.DataType) -> None:
    torch.manual_seed(0)

    epsilon = 1e-5
    input_shape = [1, 1, dim_a, dim_b]

    torch_input = torch.randn(input_shape)
    torch_output = torch.nn.functional.layer_norm(torch_input, (input_shape[-1],), eps=epsilon)

    tt_input = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, dtype=dtype)
    tt_output = ttnn.layer_norm(tt_input, epsilon=epsilon)

    tt_output_torch = ttnn.to_torch(tt_output)

    golden = torch_output
    calculated = tt_output_torch
    diff = torch.abs(golden - calculated)
    shape = golden.shape
    i = 0
    for row in range(shape[-2]):
        for col in range(shape[-1]):
            if diff[0,0,row,col] > 2:
                print(f"DIFF {diff[0,0,row,col]} ROW {row} COL {col} GOLDEN {golden[0,0,row,col]} CALC {calculated[0,0,row,col]} INPUT {torch_input[0,0,row,col]}")
                i+=1
                if i > 50:
                    assert_allclose(torch_output, tt_output_torch, atol=0.05, rtol=float("inf"))
    assert_allclose(torch_output, tt_output_torch, atol=0.05, rtol=float("inf"))
    assert_with_pcc(torch_output, tt_output_torch)
    print("GOT HERE")
```

fails when layernorm uses the fp32 reduce (draft PR https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25259) when
- watcher is on, and dprint is off

It looks like a bunch of values are 0.

If watcher is off, or if watcher is on and a lot of data is printed via dprint, then the test passes.
Using the existing code in main makes the test pass regardless.

Expected Behaviour:
the test passes regardless even when fp32 reduce is used.

Issue #25552: Use 2 data movement kernels for argmax
------------------------------------------------------------
Each tensix core in wormhole and blackhole has 2 data movement RISC-V cores. Currently, `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/argmax/device/argmax_program_factory.cpp` creates the kernel to run on only one of the data movement RISC-V cores.

The following needs to be done:
1. Change the work allocation in `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/argmax/device/argmax_program_factory.cpp` so that rows are split into 2 equal halves.
2. Distribute the halves to 2 kernels running on 2 data movement cores.
3. Have unit tests passing in `tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_argmax.py`
4. (Optional) Compare performance before and after to measure gains

Issue #25549: Add native support for tile padding in generic reduce OPs
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, generic reduction ops (e.g ttnn.min, ttnn.max, ttnn.mean, ttnn.sum etc.) do the following:

1. Call `ttnn::fill_implicit_tile_padding` on the input in  `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/generic/generic_reductions.cpp`
2. Invoke appropriate kernels on the device

For performance reasons, it is better to natively support padding in the kernels themselves. This will negate the need for invoking a separate OP before the reduction OPs.

The final deliverables are the following:
1. Natively support tile `padding` in generic reduction kernels.
3. Confirm correct operation with passing unit tests in `tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_reduction.py`
4. (Optional) Prove performance improvement with before/after measurements for some sample inputs

A similar exercise was undertaken for matmul family of OPs (see https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/22333) that serves as an excellent starting point for this issue.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25548: Support scaling in ttnn.mean for reduction over any dimension
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, ttnn.mean uses a single reduce OP call when _**any one**_ of the following is true:

1. We reduce on _all_ dimensions to return a scalar
2. We reduce on only _one_ dimension and that dimension is either the last (also called W dimension) or last but one (also called H dimension) 
3. We reduce on _two_ dimensions and _both_ the dimensions are last and last but one (H and W dimensions)


See `single_reduce_op` in `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/generic/generic_reductions.cpp`

When neither of these conditions are met, we do the following:
1. _transpose_ the reduction dimension to be the H dimension (-2)
2. Invoke reduction kernel
3. _transpose_ again to negate the operation in (1)
4. Finally, use `ttnn.mul_sfpu` to scale down the values.

For performance reasons, we want to avoid (4) and instead pass the scalar to the reduction kernel invocation in (2).



The final deliverables are the following:
1. Modify `reduce_nd_loop` lambda function to accept a scalar value
2. Compute the correct `scalar` according to reduction dimensions
3. Plumb through the lambda function to pass this scalar to the invocation of "SUM" kernel
4. Confirm correct operation with passing unit tests in `tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_reduction.py`
5. (Optional) Prove performance improvement with before/after measurements for some sample inputs

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25547: Fuse negation into compute kernel for "min" reduction OP
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, ttnn.min does the following:

1. Call `ttnn::neg` on the input in  `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/generic/device/reduce_op.cpp`
2. Invoke `ReduceOpMath::MAX` kernel

For performance reasons, it is better to fuse the negation into the MAX kernel.

The final deliverables are the following:
1. Natively support `negation` in MAX operation
2. Modify the compute kernel of MAX to negate values in a tile before invoking max LLK (low level kernel OP)
3. Confirm correct operation with passing unit tests in `tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_reduction.py`
4. (Optional) Prove performance improvement with before/after measurements for some sample inputs

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25526: Unable to extract modulated_deform_conv function definition in UniAD backbone
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In the Uniad model, we use the ResNet101 architecture as the img_backbone module. This model utilizes the ModulatedDeformConv2dPack class from the mmcv package, which internally relies on the modulated_deform_conv_forward function.

However, the actual implementation of this function is not directly accessible, as it is loaded via ext_loader from a compiled shared object (.so) file. Due to this, we're unable to view or modify the underlying function logic.

While attempting to reimplement this functionality entirely in PyTorch, we encountered several limitations:
- **Offset handling:** Shifting sampling positions per pixel is not well-supported in PyTorch, especially when flexibility and efficiency are required.
- **Mask application:** Accurately aligning and applying the modulation masks is complex and error-prone.
- **Numerical accuracy:** Even a close approximation in PyTorch results in low PCC due to subtle differences in interpolation, padding, and mask handling, making it unsuitable for models requiring high precision.

Given these challenges, native kernel support for deformable convolution—such as the one in PR #17076—is necessary.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25523: Depthwise conv2d optimization
------------------------------------------------------------
### Background

Depthwise convolution is a special case of grouped convolution where each input channel is convolved independently with its own filter. This implies:

- The number of input channels equals the number of output channels.
- The number of groups equals the number of input/output channels.

<img width="2560" height="1354" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba0c0f82-0cb1-4099-88b5-c14777587784" />

In the current implementation, depthwise convolution is executed the same way as regular convolution. However, this expands the weight matrix with many zeros, resulting in a large number of unnecessary multiplications.

Example - current grouped conv implementation (weight matrix is groups times larger than effective weights):

<img width="2860" height="1690" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d8cf1ad2-bfdd-4b80-a639-9aea89c3068c" />

### Motivation

Some models (e.g. **YOLOv10**, **MobileNetV2**) rely heavily on compute-bound depthwise convolutions, which become performance bottlenecks.

### Idea

Use a pooling-like approach where:

- Each iteration processes one spatial window
- One output stick is produced
- An extra step multiplies input with weights (compared to regular pooling)
- No unnecessary multiplications as in the current implementation

This is conceptually similar to how avg_pool works - but with multiplication added:

<img width="1928" height="2344" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e3625ce3-e6f7-4714-8b96-50cfc9ef6c2c" />

### Performance expectation
Initial measurements show avg_pool (same input) is 5–10x faster than the current depthwise conv in critical paths. Despite some performance downsides (listed below), this approach still shows promise:

- Added multiply step (extra pack/unpack)
- avg_pool may benefit from better parallelization strategy
- Output may need tilization, which adds overhead (pool output is row major)

Goal is to reach **~2x** of the current perf on depthwise convs.

### Task list
- [ ] Create first PoC by modifying pool kernel for depthwise case and measure perf
- [ ] If needed, request fused mul + reduce from LLK (to avoid extra pack/unpack)
- [ ] Avoid kernel code duplication if possible
- [ ] Add support for all required use cases, prioritizing the most performance-critical ones

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25519: SDXL: ttnn.cos op bad PCC
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Low input values to the `ttnn.cos` function result in poor correlation (low PCC) with expected outputs.

Test:
```
@pytest.mark.parametrize("h", [6])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("w", [128])
def test_cos_sdxl(device, h, w):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    torch_input_tensor = torch.empty((h,w), dtype=torch.bfloat16).uniform_(-0.07, 0.09) #min and max values from SDXL timesteps
    golden_function = ttnn.get_golden_function(ttnn.cos)
    torch_output_tensor = golden_function(torch_input_tensor, device=device)
    input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG, device=device)
    output_tensor = ttnn.cos(input_tensor)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_layout(output_tensor, ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT)
    output_tensor = ttnn.from_device(output_tensor)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    assert_with_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor, 0.999) 
```

### Expected behavior: 
PCC >= 0.999
### Observed behavior: 
PCC = 0.1501023314299341

Tested on N300 with command `WH_ARCH_YAML=wormhole_b0_80_arch_eth_dispatch.yaml  pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/eltwise/test_unary.py::test_cos_sdxl`

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25518: SDXL accuracy alignment
------------------------------------------------------------
## 🎯 Goal

**Stable Diffusion XL**: Align and compare our **CLIP** and **FID** results and NVIDIA’s A100 and H100 results
- The purpose of this analysis is to ensure an **apples-to-apples** comparison between ours and NVIDIA’s results

### 1. **Run on our hardware**
- Measure **CLIP** and **FID** for:
  -  **NVIDIA reference hyperparameters** + (20 denoising steps)
  -  **NVIDIA reference hyperparameters** + (50 denoising steps)
 
### 2. **Run on A100 GPU**
- Measure **CLIP** and **FID** using:

Issue #25517: Need indexed in-place addition feature for VADV2 model
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, TTNN does not support indexed in-place addition operations, which are essential for the spatial cross-attention module in the VADV2 model.

To work around the lack of support for indexed in-place addition in TTNN, I attempted to replace the operation with other PyTorch ops and evaluate their correctness using PCC, before converting them to TTNN.

I tried the following workaround for the op,
1. I replaced the indexed in-place addition with the index_add operation. For small input sizes, the PCC is 1.0, indicating perfect alignment. However, for the full-sized original case, the PCC drops significantly.
 - The code is in test_index_add func of [test_vadv2_issues.py](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/5af9514d83d9511f22c4dd5d3e7b0a3a692eacba/tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py#L61)
 - To run the test use the following command,
`pytest tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py::test_index_add`

2. I also tried to mimic the behaviour using scatter and gather, but this approach also resulted in low PCC.
 - The test code is in test_index_inplace_add_scatter func in [test_vadv2_issues.py](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/5af9514d83d9511f22c4dd5d3e7b0a3a692eacba/tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py#L123C5-L123C35)
 - To run the test use the following command,
`pytest tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py::test_index_inplace_add_scatter`
<br>
You can refer to the original PyTorch implementation of this operation by:

1. Checking out the repository [keerthanar/vadv2_issue](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/keerthanar/vadv2_issue)
2. Reviewing the test_index_inplace_add function in the [test_vadv2_issues.py](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/keerthanar/vadv2_issue/tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py#L28) file
3. To run the code, use the following command,
`pytest tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py::test_index_inplace_add`

Issue #25505: Add support for a host api function to convert from virtual coords to logical coords
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

The problem I'm trying to solve requires optimal dispatching of compute and data-movement kernels to a number of tensix cores. It is achieved by an algorithm which takes advantage of the proximity information between tensix tiles and dram tiles, the algorithm works with virtual coordinates so outputs only virtual coordinates.

The kernels need to be dispatched to tensix cores associated with the virtual coordinates computed by the algorithm. To create a kernel, we need to pass logical coordinates to `CreateKernel`. Currently, there is no support in IDevice class to convert from virtual coordinates to logical coordinates.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I need a host api function something like `my_device->get_logical_coordinate_from_virtual_coordinates()`, currently a related api function exists in `Cluster` class, called `get_virtual_coordinate_from_logical_coordinates` but this does the opposite of what I want to achieve.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25504: Optimization of scaled_dot_product_attention operation.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Currently, we are working on implementing the boltz-2 model, which uses the scaled_dot_product_attention operation extensively. On the P150 , we that noticed casting operands from Bfloat16 to Bfloat8 helps reduced runtime from 3ms to 2ms. Do you see any other ways this op could be optimized further? 

We are currently calling the op like this:
```
  head_o = ttnn.transformer.scaled_dot_product_attention(
      head_q,
      head_k,
      head_v,
      attn_mask=head_triangle_bias,
      is_causal=False,
      scale=self.head_dim**-0.5,
      program_config=ttnn.SDPAProgramConfig(
          compute_with_storage_grid_size=(13, 10),
          exp_approx_mode=False,
          q_chunk_size=256,
          k_chunk_size=256,
      ),
  )
```
The heads are of the shape: [1, 768, 768, 32] BFLOAT8_B on DRAM while attention mask is [1, 1, 768, 768].

Issue #25503: Linear Uses Small Core Grid By Default
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

On a Blackhole P150 card the linear operation defaults to low core grids. We have identified four situations where this is happening, directly impacting our model runtime. 

Example 1: Using the Linear op on a [1, 704, 703, 128] x [128x128] linear op uses a default of 22 cores, while forcing the coregrid to 88 cores decreases overall op time. Both tensors are DRAM interleaved in a Bfloat8 datatype. Why does it the operation default to such a low core grid count?

Example 2:
This is a similar example on a [768, 768, 128] x [128,4] linear op. Both tensors are BFloat16, and the operation only uses 24 cores by default.

Example 3:
This issue arises on a [768, 768, 128] x [128, 512] mixed Bfloat16 by BFloat8 linear, limiting itself to 64 cores. 

Example 4:
This [768,768, 128]x[128,128] bfloat16 operation only uses 24 cores. 

The same code example is included as altering tensor shapes and datatype can be used to switch between test cases.
```
import torch      
import ttnn      
    
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=0)      
    
torch_a = torch.randn((768, 768, 128), dtype=torch.bfloat16)    
torch_b = torch.randn((128, 128), dtype=torch.bfloat16)    
    
tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(      
    torch_a,      
    layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,      
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,    
    device=device,    
    memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG    
)  
tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(      
    torch_b,      
    layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,      
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,    
    device=device,    
    memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG    
)  
  
# Correct compute kernel config  
compute_kernel_config = ttnn.WormholeComputeKernelConfig(  
    math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi4,  
    math_approx_mode=False,  
    fp32_dest_acc_en=True,  
    packer_l1_acc=True,  
)  

print(tensor_a.shape, tensor_b.shape) 

p_out = ttnn.linear(  
    tensor_a,  
    tensor_b,  
    compute_kernel_config=compute_kernel_config, 
    memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG,  
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat8_b,  
)          
  
output = ttnn.to_torch(p_out)  
expected = torch.matmul(torch_a, torch_b)    
    
assert ttnn.pearson_correlation_coefficient(expected, output) >= 0.99    
ttnn.close_device(device)
```

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25502: Including Program Config Leads to Inaccurate Matmul Results
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

When executing a Matmul with the dimensions (1, 32, 704, 704)x(1, 32, 704, 704) on a P150, we get extremely poor core utilization, with the op only executing on 9 cores. As a result, we manually overrid the coregrid by passing a program_config to the matmul. However, that leads to extremely inaccurate results, dropping out model's PCC a large amount.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
import torch    
import ttnn    
  
# Open device    
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=0)    
  
# Create input tensors (keep the same random data for comparison)  
torch_a = torch.randn((1, 32, 704, 704), dtype=torch.bfloat16)  
torch_b = torch.randn((1, 32, 704, 704), dtype=torch.bfloat16)  
  
tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(    
    torch_a,    
    layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,    
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat8_b,  
    device=device,  
    memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG  
)
tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(    
    torch_b,    
    layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,    
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat8_b,  
    device=device,  
    memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG  
)

compute_kernel_config_HiFi4 = ttnn.WormholeComputeKernelConfig(
    math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi4,
    math_approx_mode=False,
    fp32_dest_acc_en=True,
    packer_l1_acc=True,
)

program_config = ttnn.MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCast1DProgramConfig(    
    compute_with_storage_grid_size=(13, 10), 
    in0_block_w=1,
    out_subblock_h=1,    
    out_subblock_w=1,    
    per_core_M=11,   
    per_core_N=11, 
    fuse_batch=True,    
    fused_activation=None,    
    mcast_in0=True,    
)

print(tensor_a.shape, tensor_b.shape)
# Perform matrix multiplication    
result = ttnn.matmul(
    tensor_a,
    tensor_b,
    program_config=program_config,
    compute_kernel_config=compute_kernel_config_HiFi4,
    memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
)

output = ttnn.to_torch(result)  
expected = torch.matmul(torch_a, torch_b)  
  
assert ttnn.pearson_correlation_coefficient(expected, output) >= 0.99  
ttnn.close_device(device)

```

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25458: Add function to exhaustively generate floating-point values.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

To ensure the accuracy of some operations (e.g. element-wise numerical operations such as `pow` or `exp`), we would like to tests inputs as exhaustively as possible. 

There are several orders of magnitude more possible floating point numbers between 0 and 0.25 than between 0.25 and 1. But an uniform distribution is 4 times more likely to pick values in the latter range. 
This makes these functions impractical to use for exhaustive testing, as small inputs are far less likely to be picked. 

Moreover, we sometime want to generate every possible input on a very specific range (e.g. for `acos`, all x in `]-1; 1[`. 

There are cases where testing all possible inputs may not be possible, but where we still want to test several values for each combination of `(sign, exponent)` (e.g. to test floating point inputs).

### Describe the solution you'd like

For element wise operations, a good solution would be a `arange_nulp(start, end, ulp_step, dtype)` function that returns a tensor whoses consecutive values are spaced by N ULPs.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Other alternatives include:

- Generating all possible inputs (`2**16` bfloat16 / 2**32 float32), but filtering what we don't need. It would work for bfloat16 (~131 KiB of data), but it doesn't scale past float32 inputs (17 GB of data).
- `arange(start, end, eps)` (with tiny epsilon) does not work because of huge differences in floating-point resolution.

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

A new matmul pybind is introduced in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24405
This needs to be tuned for performance when integrated with MoE module.

Issue #25443: Replacing run_tools_tests.sh with an independent executable
------------------------------------------------------------
currently, the functionality of the watcher_dump executable is tested using the bespoke shell script tests/scripts/run_tools_tests.sh.

In preparation for shifting left, this script should be replaced with a single executable that tests all the same functionality.

run_tools_tests.sh currently uses the unit_tests_debug_tools executable with flags to create data that watcher_dump then uses. When replacing this script, the specific tests that are used should become part of the executable so it doesn't rely on the existence of unit_tests_debug_tools to function.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25439: Device profiler post-processing step is slow
------------------------------------------------------------
# Summary

Post-processing the device profiler data can take a while on larger models. This is causing the device performance pipelines take a long time, where most of the time is spent on post-processing the profiler data.

```
2025-07-21 14:17:33.671 | INFO     | tt_metal.tools.profiler.process_device_log:core_to_device_timeseries:423 - Importing Data For Device Number : 0
2025-07-21 14:19:16.987 | INFO     | tt_metal.tools.profiler.process_ops_logs:generate_reports:643 - OPs' perf analysis is finished! Generating reports ...
```

The post-processing time was improved in this PR:

-   https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23448/

But we should be able to improve it even further. 

My initial investigation found that most of the post-processing time is in `import_log_run_stats` (>65%) and second slowest is `import_tracy_op_logs` at (>20%)

No linked pull requests found.

------------------------------------------------------------

```
python3 run.py --model Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 --workflow server --device n300 --dev-mode --docker-server
```

gives the following error.

```
ERROR 07-18 20:30:26 [engine.py:453]   File "/home/container_app_user/tt-metal/models/tt_transformers/tt/common.py", line 199, in precompute_freqs
ERROR 07-18 20:30:26 [engine.py:453]     freqs = 1.0 / (theta ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2)[: (dim // 2)].float() / dim))
ERROR 07-18 20:30:26 [engine.py:453] TypeError: arange() received an invalid combination of arguments - got (int, NoneType, int), but expected one of:
ERROR 07-18 20:30:26 [engine.py:453]  * (Number end, *, Tensor out = None, torch.dtype dtype = None, torch.layout layout = None, torch.device device = None, bool pin_memory = False, bool requires_grad = False)

ERROR 07-18 20:30:26 [engine.py:453]  * (Number start, Number end, Number step = 1, *, Tensor out = None, torch.dtype dtype = None, torch.layout layout = None, torch.device device = None, bool pin_memory = False, bool requires_grad = False)
```

The error is caused by the transformers version in tt-transformers being uplifted to 4.53.0 in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/commit/a3941d2ebd3d088b24a4038a6c14e900917e43b6 when Mistral requires `transformers == 4.51.3` as described in this issue https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/23670#issuecomment-2992654764.

This also caused failures in shield ci https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-shield/actions/runs/16377421134

### Expected behavior

The model should run without failure.

Issue #25435: [Fabric] Add coarse grain perf (bandwidth) tracing to fabric router kernel
------------------------------------------------------------
To improve visibility into system performance we will start adding minimal/lightweight performance tracing to fabric.

This performance tracing will consist of capturing the following information (as a tiered approach):

1) Total traffic over active time
  - For each packet sent or received (or both - TBD based on performance cost and accuracy), add to the total data sent.
    - track in local memory
  - timestamp at the start/end of the full inner loop
  - outside of inner loop, if #packets sent > 0, add inner loop time and total traffic sent in inner loop to running total in L1
  
2) Host regulated dump intervals
  - Double buffer the datastructure from 1) in L1. Establish a mailbox or similar signaling scheme with host
  - Whenever host requests/reads data, move onto the next slot. 
    - This way we can add more granularity to monitoring without really adding extra burden to erisc 

3) Extend to n slots deep, possibly make erisc write out to DRAM

4) Extend 3 to track timestamps per packet

4) Add tracing information (i.e. packet IDs)

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25432: Test with device init fabric hanging during final `DumpDeviceProfilerResults` calls (during teardown)
------------------------------------------------------------
On branch:
`snijjar/profiler-dump-during-teardown-issue-branch`

Drop commit (which hacks around issue): 
`2317e5a0b82f25933c82e0227aaca973f6ebaf4b`: "SEPARATE PR: change ordering of when dumping profiler results vs tearing down fabric"

Build with
`./build_metal.sh -e -c --build-tests --enable-profiler`

Run:
`pytest tests/tt_metal/microbenchmarks/ethernet/test_1d_fabric_latency.py -k line`

The first test will hang during test fixture teardown.

Specifically, the first call to `DumpDeviceProfilerResults` in  `DevicePool::close_devices` (`device_pool.cpp`) hangs in an internal call to `Finish`. The hack is to move the call to dump the profiler data to after we teardown fabric  and fast dispatch.

I am currently not sure why this causes the test to complete. My understanding/expectation is that now, since FD is a client of fabric, fabric should be launched with slow dispatch and so FD's command queue should be utterly unaware of its existence.

However, I also confirmed with watcher that during this hang, only fabric and FD kernels are still running (also coincides with the test main body completing and hanging during test fixture teardown).

Note the fixture (`Fabric1DFixture`) is defined in `tests/tt_metal/tt_fabric/common/test_fabric_edm_common.hpp`. This creates a fabric at device init time.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25428: profiler: Add tests to validate zones displayed in the Tracy GUI
------------------------------------------------------------
There should be tests to verify that all zones that we expect to show up in the GUI for a particular test do show up, and that the properties of these zones being displayed (colour, runtime, name, etc.) are what we expect. 

For example, in the following screenshots, the runtime of the selected zone is significantly lower after a change when it shouldn't be, and it's difficult to validate this manually for every single zone. Having a suite of tests to validate that new changes don't break the GUI would make it easier to ensure that new changes don't change the behaviour of the Tracy GUI unexpectedly.

Issue #25417: Move exp2() to exp21f style algorithm
------------------------------------------------------------
@nathan-TT proposed a change to improve accuracy of exp2() that just changes some constants in the exp21f algorithm to directly calculate 2**x - let's implement this as a new method for exp2() rather than the current method.

> For 2**x, you'd only need to replace 0x00b8aa3b with 0x00800000 in the following line:
> sfpi::vInt z = sfpu::_float_to_int32_(val * sfpi::vFloat(0x00b8aa3b) + sfpi::vFloat(0x3f800000));
> This 'first' constant is computed through log2(base)  (with exponent bits set to 0).

Merge this on the metal side first, and then we can push it to the LLK side later.

Please include ULP graphs for the full range in this issue.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25377: Investigate why and fix hanging device close during auto-reset in pytest after workaround changes to correctly call destructor
------------------------------------------------------------
## On CI

Last-known good: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16382710766
Earliest known hang: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16384800065

commit range is:

<img width="1250" height="212" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f78fbe8a-6d4f-46ce-83b1-8b7c9217871d" />

Other two commits shouldn't have anything to do with auto-reset.

## Impact

This is preventing less frequent tests, such as performance and full-ML model tests, from running if one test in the suite breaks

## Description

In Python (`conftest.py`), we have a mechanism to automatically tt-smi reset a device if we see an error on a test during operation in a Python test suite.

In an IRD container or machine with metal + ttnn built, make the following changes:

```
user@322185e98682:~/tt-metal$ git diff
diff --git a/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_device.py b/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_device.py
index 6d91a43183..601a3c6ecb 100644
--- a/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_device.py
+++ b/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_device.py
@@ -32,6 +32,7 @@ def test_l1_size():
 @pytest.mark.parametrize("layout", [ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT])
 @pytest.mark.parametrize("dtype", [ttnn.bfloat8_b])
 def test_worker_l1_size(device, layout, dtype):
+    raise Exception()
     torch_tensor = torch.rand((1, 1, 32, 32), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
```

a quick `raise Exception()` in a simple test that uses the `device` fixture. This arbitrary failure should trigger the auto-reset when you do

```
pytest -n auto tests/ttnn/unit_tests/test_device.py::test_worker_l1_size
```

@tt-dma made changes to put a [workaround](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/commit/1f02ba20a2e0ee46baaeb092a12ed988b78015c9) to call the Cluster destructor to help with ND race we were seeing in the PR/Merge gates. (I forget which issue it was, can find later)

That's a needed change as stability is important, but now the auto-reset capabilities that we added to Pytest are borked and it hangs upon device closure.

Can we get some help with this? @pgkeller 

Example logs

Issue #25361: Pad Operations Only Utilize One Core for Certain Dimensions
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

TTNN's pad operation, when operating on tiled tensors sometimes only maps to one core, leading to massive slowdowns. This example was tested on a P150.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
import torch  
import ttnn  
  
# Open device  
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=0)  
  
# Create a PyTorch tensor with the desired shape  
torch_tensor = torch.rand((1, 686, 686, 128), dtype=torch.bfloat16)  
  
# Convert to TTNN tensor with TILE_LAYOUT  
ttnn_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(  
    torch_tensor,   
    dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,   
    layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,   
    device=device  
)  
  
padding = ((0, 0), (0, 82), (0, 82), (0, 0)) 
  
padded_tensor = ttnn.pad(  
    ttnn_tensor,   
    padding=padding,   
    value=0.0  
)  
  
print(f"Padded tensor shape: {padded_tensor.shape}")  
  
# Convert back to PyTorch to verify  
result_torch = ttnn.to_torch(padded_tensor)  
print(f"Final PyTorch tensor shape: {result_torch.shape}")  
  
# Close device  
ttnn.close_device(device)
```
When this is run, ttnn warns about utilizing only one core. 
```
2025-07-19 05:43:00.159 | warning  |              Op | TILE layout does not have multicore implementation yet. Falling back to 1 core. (pad_op.cpp:129)
```

### Expected behavior

We expect the pad op to only as many cores as possible. As it stands, the op is around 2 orders of magnitude slower compared to similarly sized tensors that can be padded with a larger core grid.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25358: Rewrite run_cpp_unit_tests.sh to not use pytest and ARCH_NAME
------------------------------------------------------------
ARCH_NAME should be deprecated, and using pytest to run a bunch of C++ executables runs into problems where the main process can conflict (via UMD) with the subprocess. We also don't really need subprocesses since the tests have to run sequentially. We should rewrite this script to fix these issues.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25354: Remove Boost dependencies from ttnn public API.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

As part of the TTNN dev package, we're exposing Boost headers via public headers

It causes multiple problems, such as:
- Increased Compile Times 
- Requiring users to install a specific version of Boost manually (Tight Coupling and Dependency Hell)
- API Instability

which could affect the functionality of the ttnn library.



### Describe the solution you'd like

- Remove Boost dependencies from public header files.
- Link with Boost statically(preferred) or ship specific libboost*.so version with ttnn package.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25351: Public-facing TTNN header includes internal experimental header
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

The following header inclusion makes it difficult to use TTNN as a C++ library, the compiler won't be able to find the header without adding an unusual include path that points to the implementation folder.
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/bb198d477c2207080c9839090ff8860b38ecf6c3/ttnn/api/ttnn/run_operation.hpp#L10

### Expected behavior

Public-facing API should be self-contained

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25349: Support INT32 for EmbeddingOp and ReshapeOp
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Embedding and Reshape Ops in tt-metal do not support INT32. After [this PR](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25295) got merged, we need to add support for INT32 type for these ops.
**Motivation**: This will unblock some models, such as `llama 3-2 3B`, to go through and run on the device with the optimizer enabled.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Build `tt-mlir` with `ttrt` flag: 
```bash
cmake --build build -- ttrt
```
2. Save device specs with:
```bash
ttrt query --save-artifacts
```
3. From the given `ttir` model, generate a `ttnn` model with:
```bash
ttmlir-opt --ttir-to-ttnn-backend-pipeline="system-desc-path=ttrt-artifacts/system_desc.ttsys  enable-optimizer=true" -o llama_ttnn.txt llama_ttir.txt
```
4. Generate the flatbuffer with:
```bash
 ttmlir-translate --ttnn-to-flatbuffer llama_ttnn.txt -o llama.ttnn
```
5. Run the flatbuffer on the device (This is where the error happens):
```bash
ttrt run llama.ttnn
```

----

Step 5 results in the following error:
```bash
2025-07-18 18:48:37.414 | critical |          Always | Can only work with bfloat16/float32 or uint32 tensors (assert.hpp:107)
2025-07-18 18:48:37,415 - ERROR - ERROR: test=llama.ttnn experienced an error with exception=TT_FATAL @ /localdev/sgholami/tt-mlir/third_party/tt-metal/src/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/data_movement/reshape_view/device/reshape_device_operation.cpp:22: input_tensor_a.dtype() == DataType::BFLOAT16 or input_tensor_a.dtype() == DataType::UINT32 or input_tensor_a.dtype() == DataType::FLOAT32
info:
Can only work with bfloat16/float32 or uint32 tensors
```

[llama_ttnn.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21322830/llama_ttnn.txt)
[llama_ttir.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21322829/llama_ttir.txt)

### Expected behavior

- The mentioned ops (`Embedding` and `Reshape`) should accept `INT32`.
- llama passes and runs on device with the optimizer enabled.
----

Note:
Before filing the bug, I tired adding the following lines and they seem to resolve the issue (llama passes through and runs on the device successfully). However, I didn't add this since @amorrisonTT asked me to not add them unless I can test it throughly (which I couldn't).
Therefore, I include the changes that will make llama pass the flow and run on the device for the record.

<img width="719" height="233" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2ecf60ab-864f-41aa-a158-9cc4b2f10cb7" />

<img width="719" height="291" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/be63c9f6-c2a1-44f7-8876-93e1238f7487" />

### Please complete the following environment information

- `tt-mlir` commit: b28acd728f56f4df8d5e0a3e0ce0639f6ce86ae5
- `tt-metal` commit: 2bc1c74bcd28050f5f20c4eae50fb0ffc66c7ffe
- Note that you might need to either:
  - Wait for the next metal uplift in `tt-mlir` that includes [this change](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25295),
  - Or to apply [this change](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25295) yourself manually.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25348: Reduce Scatter ND Hang on Blackhole
------------------------------------------------------------
- We get a ND hang while running reduce scatter on blackhole quiet box (
   - **Does not** hang for T3K WH
- Testing Branch: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/compare/main...grechsteinerTT/BH-RS-debugging
- Op: `reduce_scatter_minimal_async`
  - both ring and line impementations
- Machine: `qh-gh02`
  - Also reproduced on `bh-31`
- Test: `tests/nightly/t3000/ccl/test_minimal_reduce_scatter_async.py`
- Sample Shape that ND hangs: 
  - `num_devices=4, num_links=1, rs_input_shape=[1, 1, 256, 4096], dim=3, layout=tile`
  - tested for both interleaved DRAM & interleaved L1
  - tested for both `bfloat16` and `bfloat8_b`
- Sample Shape that doesn't ND hang: 
  - `num_devices=4, num_links=1, rs_input_shape=[1, 1, 256, 256], dim=3, layout=tile`
  - tested for both interleaved DRAM & interleaved L1
  - tested for both `bfloat16` and `bfloat8_b`
- Other shapes (other params same as above) that also ND hang (less frequently as size decreases):
   - `[1, 1, 32, 4096]`
   - `[1, 1, 32, 2048]`
  - `[1, 1, 32, 1280]`
  - `[1, 1, 32, 1024]`
  - `[1, 1, 32, 768]`
- `[1, 1, 32, 640]` is the first `[1, 1, 32, any]` shape that doesn't seem to hang at all (ran 1,000 times)
- ND hang still occurs after gutting out all CB synchronization & the compute kernel
- Get PCIE errors when running with watcher and/or DPRINT
____________________
- Inserting a large number of empty cycles between sequences of noc-reads or fabric-writes eliminates the hang
- Removing either the noc-reads or fabric-writes seems to eliminate the hang (not yet tested for really big shapes)
- If you run watcher with the code hacked to eliminate the hang (therefore eliminating the PCIE error) using either of the two above methods, then watcher doesn't report any errors
- When you update the kernels to do repetitive noc/ethernet transactions in a loop (and nothing else), you can reproduce the hang with a large number of loop iterations
  - These loops are included in the current state of the branch
  - Hang are not observed with the current number of loop iterations (5)
  - I saw hangs with 100 iterations, but the hangs could very well start occurring with less iteration as well

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25347: Control plane does not return the correct number of routing planes.
------------------------------------------------------------
api get_number_of_available_routing_planes always return 2 for T3K, however, in user's perspective, only 1 is available for user to use (the other routing plane is assigned to dispatch). 

in PR https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25297, we were trying to properly skip the tests if number of routing planes is not 1, however, since control plane returns 2, meaning the test will hang.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25345: [Fabric][Blackhole] Add calls to call_base_fw in fabric router in place of context switches
------------------------------------------------------------
See [this comment](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/25030#discussion_r2214663755) for some more context. Note that for this issue there is a context switch in the main loop and also during ethernet handshakes (for wormhole). For blackhole there paths are not enabled. They should be switched to call `call_base_fw` instead.

Add an attribute in the risc config in erisc datamover builder to enable `call_base_fw` and only enable in Blackhole. Update the codepaths in router to call this when it's enabled.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25343: Remove `ttnn/integration_tests` as a type of test from CI, source files, and documentation, and move any tests we want to keep to `models/`
------------------------------------------------------------
## Rationale

- From a sample of ops (Billy R, @s-jovic , ) developers
- The intention of these tests are unclear to model developers like @mbahnasTT , and it led to duplication of testing like in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24569/files
- Metal models team recommends putting all tests into `models/` anyway for clearer abstraction and easier maintenance (thank you @esmalTT )

## Steps

1. Stop running ttnn integration tests in CI
2. Delete models that no one owns and is not tested in other pipelines
3. Tell model owners who want to keep them to move the tests to the respective model folder in `models/` so that they may run in their respective job in the Frequent ttnn + models tests
4. Fix/remove any references in documentation to this folder
5. Delete folder on branch and run all single-card CI to ensure all references are deleted on branch

## Some responsibilities

- resnet tests need to be moved/deleted, but existing ones in `models/` in frequent pipeline may cover things enough according to @s-jovic 
- all MCW models must be moved to their respective folder and stop calling these sub-model tests "integration tests" in customer readiness definition cc: @dvartaniansTT @mbahnasTT 
- all MCW models must have their documentation fixed to reference new tests
- all written by Akhmed must be deleted, except maybe t5

## List of stuff I found

- [ ] `ttnn/tutorials/005.ipynb` uses `t5`, so we may need to keep that or move it to a different folder

## Going forward

I'm not against having "integration tests" in TT-NN. Very good case for it, considering what TT-NN is.

The current `tests/ttnn/integration_tests` are not integration tests. They're just models using the TT-NN API, and are no different from current model tests.

cc: @kkabilarTT

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25342: Functions for retrieving number of cores available not working properly with simulator
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

If I modify wormhole simulator to use  2 cores, and when i simulate i see the printouts mentioning two cores and the dump also consists of two cores but according to the dump file one core does nothing. 

Then i did some printouts in the op factory and it turns out the number of cores the op sees when it queries using the function

  auto compute_with_storage_grid_size = device->compute_with_storage_grid_size();   
 auto [num_cores, all_cores, core_group_1, core_group_2, num_tiles_per_core_group_1,  num_tiles_per_core_group_2] =
        split_work_to_cores(compute_with_storage_grid_size, num_tiles);
is still 1. 


The simulator team tried to printout some information here 

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/tt_metal/llrt/core_descriptor.cpp#L172

And it printed out two cores. 

```
                 Always | INFO     | Grid size for device 0: 1 2
                 Always | INFO     | Grid size for device 0: 1 2
                  Metal | INFO     | Initializing device 0. Program cache is NOT enabled
                  Metal | INFO     | AI CLK for device 0 is:   0 MHz
                 Always | INFO     | Compute grid size for device 0: 1 1
                 Always | INFO     | Compute grid size for device 0: 1 1
```

The problem may then likely be in the functions for querying , which might not be taking into account the simulation mode. 



### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Checkout branch amahmud/simulator_multi_core_issue
2. Setup python test environment, and simulator branch amahmud/worm_2 or edit your branch to make wormhole use 2 cores
3. export TT_METAL_SIMULATOR=path_to versim-wormhole-b0_<the one that uses 2 cores> 
4. RUN ``` NNG_SOCKET_NAME=deviceinit TT_METAL_SLOW_DISPATCH_MODE=1 pytest ./tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/test_examples.py3. Run pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/test_examples.py ```

The print outs from the file  ```ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/examples/example_multiple_return/device/single_core_program_factory.cpp```

Will say it detected only 1 core when doing simulation but on silicon it will detect multiple cores.

Issue #25326: `ttnn.gelu` accuracy should be improved
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

`ttnn.gelu` seems to have accuracy problems in the range [-5, -2].
This seems to be a known problem( #21313  #23669 https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/20562 ) but I want to have a separate tracking issue on Forge side.
This is a hard blocker for 3 variants of ViT (base patch32 384, large patch16 224 and large patch32 384) and Roberta trough tt-xla. It's interesting that some variants of ViT work fine with current levels of inaccuracy while others don't.
In apparent contradiction to investigations done in #21313 , when we use Forge tooling to bypass gelu and do it on the CPU, ViT passes.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the following code
```
import torch
import ttnn
import numpy as np

def spacing(x: torch.Tensor):
    """Calculate spacing (ULP size) for bfloat16 and float32 inputs."""
    # If input is bfloat16, use abs(x) * 2^-7 for normal numbers
    if x.dtype == torch.bfloat16:
        ulp = torch.abs(x).to(torch.float32) * 2**-7
        # For zero, use smallest positive normal bfloat16 (2^-126)
        ulp = torch.where(x == 0, torch.tensor(2**-126, dtype=torch.float32), ulp)
        return ulp
    else:
        # For float32, use nextafter
        direction = torch.where(x >= 0, torch.inf, -torch.inf)
        next_val = torch.nextafter(x, direction)
        return torch.abs(next_val - x)

def ulp_difference(a, b):
    """Calculate ULP (Units in the Last Place) difference between two tensors for bfloat16 and float32."""
    # Ensure inputs are torch tensors
    if not torch.is_tensor(a):
        a = torch.tensor(a)
    if not torch.is_tensor(b):
        b = torch.tensor(b)
    # Move to CPU and ensure same device/dtype
    a = a.detach().cpu()
    b = b.detach().cpu()
    # Handle special cases (inf, nan)
    mask_valid = torch.isfinite(a) & torch.isfinite(b)
    if not torch.any(mask_valid):
        return torch.tensor([float('inf')])
    # Use the larger absolute value for spacing calculation
    max_abs = torch.maximum(torch.abs(a), torch.abs(b))
    # Calculate spacing (distance to next representable float)
    ulp_spacing = spacing(max_abs)
    # Calculate ULP difference
    abs_diff = torch.abs(a - b)
    ulp_diff = abs_diff / ulp_spacing
    return ulp_diff[mask_valid]

RANGE = 4

def test_ttnn_gelu():
    x_values = torch.linspace(-RANGE, RANGE, 2048, dtype=torch.bfloat16).reshape(-1, 32, 32)
    
    with ttnn.manage_device(0) as dev:
        # Convert to ttnn tensor with BFLOAT16 and tile layout
        ttnn_input = ttnn.from_torch(
            x_values,
            dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
            layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
            device=dev
        )
    
        ttnn_result = ttnn.gelu(ttnn_input)
        ttnn_output = ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_result)
        torch_reference = torch.nn.functional.gelu(x_values)
        
        print("\n" + "="*60)
        print("COMPARISON RESULTS")
        print("="*60)
        
        # Calculate various error metrics
        abs_diff = torch.abs(ttnn_output - torch_reference)
        rel_diff = abs_diff.float() / (torch.abs(torch_reference.float()) + 1e-9)
        
        print(f"Max absolute difference: {abs_diff.max():.6e}")
        print(f"Mean absolute difference: {abs_diff.mean():.6e}")
        print(f"Max relative difference: {rel_diff.max():.6e}")
        print(f"Mean relative difference: {rel_diff.mean():.6e}")
        
        # Calculate ULP difference
        ulp_diffs = ulp_difference(ttnn_output, torch_reference)
        
        print(f"\nULP Statistics:")
        print(f"Max ULP difference: {torch.max(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        print(f"Mean ULP difference: {torch.mean(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        print(f"Median ULP difference: {torch.median(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        
        N=30
        # Print some sample comparisons (equally spaced subset)
        total_values = len(x_values.flatten())
        indices = np.linspace(0, total_values - 1, N, dtype=int)
        
        print(f"\nSample comparisons ({N} equally spaced values):")
        print("Input\t\tTorch gelu\tTTNN gelu\tAbs Diff\tULP Diff\tOne ULP size")
        print("-" * 70)
        for i in indices:
            inp = x_values.flatten()[i].item()
            torch_val = torch_reference.flatten()[i].item()
            ttnn_val = ttnn_output.flatten()[i].item()
            abs_d = abs_diff.flatten()[i].item()
            ulp_d = ulp_diffs[i] if i < len(ulp_diffs) else 0
            torch_ulp = spacing(torch.tensor(torch_val, dtype=torch.bfloat16)).item()
            print(f"{inp:8.3f}\t{torch_val:10.6f}\t{ttnn_val:10.6f}\t{abs_d:.2e}\t{ulp_d:8.2f}\t{torch_ulp:.2e}")
        
        reasonable_ulp = torch.sum(ulp_diffs < 50) / len(ulp_diffs) * 100
        print(f"\nPercentage of values with ULP < 50: {reasonable_ulp:.1f}%")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_ttnn_gelu()
```


### Expected behavior

I don't know what level of accuracy I need, but I need it improved. Attached code is for illustration purposes only.

### Please complete the following environment information

Ubuntu 22.04
Metal hash d5c259dd7bc8d0c9879a4389d50e65c321cc0c6f

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25325: `ttnn.tanh` accuracy could be improved
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

`ttnn.tanh` specifically with small values seems to have a high error as measured by ULP difference.
It seems to be a known bug ( #23562 ) but I want to have a separate issue for tracking on Forge side.
This is a blocker for bringup of one (non priority model), VisionTextDualEncoder from tt-xla. This model has multiple outputs, and only the outputs that include tanh in their computation have bad accuracy. Furthermore, using tooling to bypass tanh on device makes the model pass.
As an interesting extra datapoint, another model(albert_xlarge) fails when using tanh, but we can detect the "gelu_new" block (which contains tanh) and replace the entire block with ttnn.gelu to make that model pass.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the following code
```
import torch
import ttnn
import numpy as np

def spacing(x: torch.Tensor):
    """Calculate spacing (ULP size) for bfloat16 and float32 inputs."""
    # If input is bfloat16, use abs(x) * 2^-7 for normal numbers
    if x.dtype == torch.bfloat16:
        ulp = torch.abs(x).to(torch.float32) * 2**-7
        # For zero, use smallest positive normal bfloat16 (2^-126)
        ulp = torch.where(x == 0, torch.tensor(2**-126, dtype=torch.float32), ulp)
        return ulp
    else:
        # For float32, use nextafter
        direction = torch.where(x >= 0, torch.inf, -torch.inf)
        next_val = torch.nextafter(x, direction)
        return torch.abs(next_val - x)

def ulp_difference(a, b):
    """Calculate ULP (Units in the Last Place) difference between two tensors for bfloat16 and float32."""
    # Ensure inputs are torch tensors
    if not torch.is_tensor(a):
        a = torch.tensor(a)
    if not torch.is_tensor(b):
        b = torch.tensor(b)
    # Move to CPU and ensure same device/dtype
    a = a.detach().cpu()
    b = b.detach().cpu()
    # Handle special cases (inf, nan)
    mask_valid = torch.isfinite(a) & torch.isfinite(b)
    if not torch.any(mask_valid):
        return torch.tensor([float('inf')])
    # Use the larger absolute value for spacing calculation
    max_abs = torch.maximum(torch.abs(a), torch.abs(b))
    # Calculate spacing (distance to next representable float)
    ulp_spacing = spacing(max_abs)
    # Calculate ULP difference
    abs_diff = torch.abs(a - b)
    ulp_diff = abs_diff / ulp_spacing
    return ulp_diff[mask_valid]

RANGE = 3

def test_ttnn_gelu():
    x_values = torch.linspace(-RANGE, RANGE, 2048, dtype=torch.bfloat16).reshape(-1, 32, 32)
    
    with ttnn.manage_device(0) as dev:
        # Convert to ttnn tensor with BFLOAT16 and tile layout
        ttnn_input = ttnn.from_torch(
            x_values,
            dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
            layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
            device=dev
        )
    
        ttnn_result = ttnn.gelu(ttnn_input)
        ttnn_output = ttnn.to_torch(ttnn_result)
        torch_reference = torch.nn.functional.gelu(x_values)
        
        print("\n" + "="*60)
        print("COMPARISON RESULTS")
        print("="*60)
        
        # Calculate various error metrics
        abs_diff = torch.abs(ttnn_output - torch_reference)
        rel_diff = abs_diff.float() / (torch.abs(torch_reference.float()) + 1e-9)
        
        print(f"Max absolute difference: {abs_diff.max():.6e}")
        print(f"Mean absolute difference: {abs_diff.mean():.6e}")
        print(f"Max relative difference: {rel_diff.max():.6e}")
        print(f"Mean relative difference: {rel_diff.mean():.6e}")
        
        # Calculate ULP difference
        ulp_diffs = ulp_difference(ttnn_output, torch_reference)
        
        print(f"\nULP Statistics:")
        print(f"Max ULP difference: {torch.max(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        print(f"Mean ULP difference: {torch.mean(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        print(f"Median ULP difference: {torch.median(ulp_diffs):.2f}")
        
        N=30
        # Print some sample comparisons (equally spaced subset)
        total_values = len(x_values.flatten())
        indices = np.linspace(0, total_values - 1, N, dtype=int)
        
        print(f"\nSample comparisons ({N} equally spaced values):")
        print("Input\t\tTorch gelu\tTTNN gelu\tAbs Diff\tULP Diff\tOne ULP size")
        print("-" * 70)
        for i in indices:
            inp = x_values.flatten()[i].item()
            torch_val = torch_reference.flatten()[i].item()
            ttnn_val = ttnn_output.flatten()[i].item()
            abs_d = abs_diff.flatten()[i].item()
            ulp_d = ulp_diffs[i] if i < len(ulp_diffs) else 0
            torch_ulp = spacing(torch.tensor(torch_val, dtype=torch.bfloat16)).item()
            print(f"{inp:8.3f}\t{torch_val:10.6f}\t{ttnn_val:10.6f}\t{abs_d:.2e}\t{ulp_d:8.2f}\t{torch_ulp:.2e}")
        
        reasonable_ulp = torch.sum(ulp_diffs < 50) / len(ulp_diffs) * 100
        print(f"\nPercentage of values with ULP < 50: {reasonable_ulp:.1f}%")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    test_ttnn_gelu()

```

Note: The range being [-0.06, 0.06] for the test case is because in my model the values entering tanh seem to be around that size.

### Expected behavior

I am not sure what level of accuracy I need to make all models pass, but I do need better accuracy in general. 

### Please complete the following environment information

Ubuntu 22.04
Metal commit d5c259dd7bc8d0c9879a4389d50e65c321cc0c6f
N150 board

Issue #25296: [Fabric] Use low-latency routing by default in place of routing-table based per-hop routing
------------------------------------------------------------
Low-latency vs "dynamic" (i.e. per hoop lookup) routing is an implementation detail. Low-latency routing is higher performance because it requires less processing per hop. We should be defaulting to "low-latency" routing for all topologies that support it (at the moment this would be all). There are some complexities with generating low-latency routes for multi-cast, so we may not be able always easily generate low-latency routes from lookup tables.

This optimization and feature work requires several steps to support this optimization.

# Generating Unicast Low Latency Routes

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25293: Adding multi-link and other supports to 6U fabric test infra
------------------------------------------------------------
1. current fabric infra missed the functionality to handle multiple links. Need to add one.
    To add multi-link, first need to duplicate/expand each sender config to multi-link.
    Add allocator changes so that we allocate addresses seperately.
2. Fix Control plane init order so that we can have correct mesh/torus setup.
3. Fix profiling logic so that it handles multi-link.
4. Add end of sync so that each core can report a reasonable perf
5. remove RT args from the infra (except for edm adapter)

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25290: ttnn.sub output is incorrect in second face of a tile
------------------------------------------------------------
```python
import torch
import ttnn
from loguru import logger
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_allclose

torch_input_tensor = torch.arange(32 * 32).reshape([32, 32]).type(torch.bfloat16)
torch_output_tensor = torch_input_tensor - 3

input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device)

output_tensor = input_tensor - 3
output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

for idx, (golden, actual) in enumerate(zip(torch_output_tensor.view(-1), output_tensor.view(-1))):
    if golden != actual:
        logger.info(f"index {idx}: golden: {golden}, actual: {actual} mismatch")
    else:
        logger.info(f"index {idx}: golden: {golden}, actual: {actual}")
assert_allclose(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor)
```

From the logs, we can see that from indices 256-512, there are several elements that mismatch by a constant factor (-2). `pcc` does not catch this, only `all_close` does.

Issue #25283: When using `HF_MODEL` with tt_transformers, system memory usage explodes
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

This is related to https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/23496.

Based on high-level memory profiling (monitoring via `htop`). If a user sets `HF_MODEL` to a relatively large model (i.e., 70B), the system memory usage explodes beyond 350GB during `Loading checkpoint shards`. The symptom of this is behaviour is that the user's process will either be killed or the entire system grinds to a halt. **Importantly**, this does not occur when the user sets `LLAMA_DIR` instead of `HF_MODEL`.

Given that we recommend a minimum spec of 64GB of system memory on our website, we should try to reduce memory usage as much as possible.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run tt-transformers demos with `HF_MODEL=meta-llama/Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct`
2. Examine system memory overrun

### Expected behavior

I believe this might come down to the fact we are holding both the HF weights as well as the checkpoint shards in memory at the same time. The simple solution might just be to deallocate the weights or perform this step in chunks.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25282: TTNN Softmax Output Mismatch
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

TTNN softmax produces different output values compared to PyTorch reference implementation.

Output Comparison
PyTorch: [0.1318, 0.1436, 0.0811, 0.1094, 0.1396]
TTNN:    [0.1328, 0.1445, 0.0820, 0.1035, 0.1396]

Models like Deepseek are highly sensitive to even small mismatches, so is there a way to resolve this?



### Steps to reproduce the issue

[softmax.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21302778/softmax.txt)

Overall ULP Analysis:
ULP max: 18.0
ULP mean: 2.9152519702911377

Highest differing elements:
Location: (tensor(0), tensor(0), tensor(12558), tensor(251))
PyTorch value: 0.03369140625
TTNN value: 0.0311279296875
Absolute difference: 0.0025634765625

Providing default initialization for all member variables clears it up.  The same tests looped repeatedly for 6hrs without failing.

Issue #25266: fp32 reduce can generate bad outputs if binary_op_init_common has certain parameters
------------------------------------------------------------
Running the following pytest
```
from loguru import logger

import pytest
import torch

import ttnn


from models.utility_functions import pad_by_zero, torch2tt_tensor, comp_pcc, is_grayskull, comp_ulp

@pytest.mark.parametrize("h", [32])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("w", [64])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("num_chunks", [1,])# 4])
def test_layer_norm_4D_llama(device, h, w, num_chunks):
    """
    Test specific shapes for LLama and other LLMs
    """
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    n = num_chunks * h * w
    offset = 1e-8
    eps = 1e-5
    torch_input_tensor = (torch.arange(n).reshape([1,num_chunks,h,w]).type(torch.bfloat16))/10 - offset
    #torch_input_tensor = torch.rand((1, num_chunks, h, w), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_weight = torch.ones((w,), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_bias = 0 * torch.rand((w,), dtype=torch.bfloat16)

    compute_kernel_config = ttnn.init_device_compute_kernel_config(
            device.arch(),
            math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi4,
            math_approx_mode=False,
            fp32_dest_acc_en=False,
            packer_l1_acc=False,
        )
    torch_output_tensor = torch.nn.functional.layer_norm(
        torch_input_tensor, normalized_shape=[w], weight=torch_weight, bias=torch_bias, eps=eps
    )

    dtype = ttnn.float32
    input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    weight = ttnn.from_torch(torch_weight, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    bias = ttnn.from_torch(torch_bias, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, dtype=dtype)
    output_tensor = ttnn.layer_norm(input_tensor, weight=weight, bias=bias, epsilon=eps)
    #output_tensor = ttnn.to_layout(output_tensor, ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT)
    output_tensor = ttnn.from_device(output_tensor)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    ulp_threshold = 2
    allow_nonfinite = True
    print(comp_ulp(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor, ulp_threshold))
    passing, output = comp_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor,pcc=1)
    assert passing, output
```
using fp32 reduce can cause a pcc close to 0 if binary_op_init_common has certain parameters.

Repro:
Run the above pytest with commit https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/commit/0878a7aa9ad606cd40d160dc7351acf59c0837b3 except leaving the binary_op_init_common as it was before:
```
binary_op_init_common(cb_in, cb_in, cb_xmm2);
```

main commit: 175a5aae8f
device: WH N150 although see the behaviour on N300 as well

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25262: Create qkv heads op with unconcatenated inputs
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

The experimental transformer ops handling the creation of qkv heads seem to require concatenated inputs. In tt-torch/ tt-mlir, want to fuse some meta/ llama 3.2 attention layer ops into an op that can split heads. However, the ops seems to handle splitting qkv matrices separately. So, if we were to use an experimental ttnn transformer op, we would also need to insert a concatenate op. Instead, it would be better if there was an op that didn't require a concatenated input. 

Here is how the heads are split in attention layer for llama 3.2 in stablehlo mlir. I hope even without knowing MLIR this would show the point about concatenated/ unconcatenated inputs.
```
# query:
    %3 = stablehlo.reshape %2 : (tensor<32x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>
    %4 = stablehlo.reshape %3 : (tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x24x128xbf16>
    %5 = stablehlo.transpose %4, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x24x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x24x32x128xbf16>
# key: 
    %8 = stablehlo.reshape %7 : (tensor<32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>
    %9 = stablehlo.reshape %8 : (tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>
    %10 = stablehlo.transpose %9, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x8x32x128xbf16>
# value:
    %13 = stablehlo.reshape %12 : (tensor<32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>
    %14 = stablehlo.reshape %13 : (tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>
    %15 = stablehlo.transpose %14, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x8x32x128xbf16>
``` 

### Describe the solution you'd like

Please create an op that splits q k and v matrices into heads separately.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Please let me know if there is an op that could what is asked. I hope I didn't overlook any op.

### Additional context

It seems that PyTorch creates q, k and v matrices separately. 
```
module {
  func.func @main(%arg0: tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>, %arg1: tensor<3072x3072xbf16>, %arg2: tensor<1024x3072xbf16>, %arg3: tensor<1024x3072xbf16>, %arg4: tensor<1x32x128xbf16>, %arg5: tensor<1x32x128xbf16>, %arg6: tensor<1x1x32x32xbf16>, %arg7: tensor<3072x3072xbf16>) -> (tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>, tensor<1x24x32x32xbf16>) {
    %cst = stablehlo.constant dense<0xFF800000> : tensor<f32>
    %cst_0 = stablehlo.constant dense<0.000000e+00> : tensor<f32>
    %cst_1 = arith.constant dense<0.088388347648318447> : tensor<1xf64>
    %0 = stablehlo.transpose %arg1, dims = [1, 0] : (tensor<3072x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<3072x3072xbf16>
    %1 = stablehlo.reshape %arg0 : (tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<32x3072xbf16>
    %2 = stablehlo.dot_general %1, %0, contracting_dims = [1] x [0] : (tensor<32x3072xbf16>, tensor<3072x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<32x3072xbf16>
    %3 = stablehlo.reshape %2 : (tensor<32x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>
    %4 = stablehlo.reshape %3 : (tensor<1x32x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x24x128xbf16>
    %5 = stablehlo.transpose %4, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x24x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x24x32x128xbf16>
    %6 = stablehlo.transpose %arg2, dims = [1, 0] : (tensor<1024x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<3072x1024xbf16>
    %7 = stablehlo.dot_general %1, %6, contracting_dims = [1] x [0] : (tensor<32x3072xbf16>, tensor<3072x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<32x1024xbf16>
    %8 = stablehlo.reshape %7 : (tensor<32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>
    %9 = stablehlo.reshape %8 : (tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>
    %10 = stablehlo.transpose %9, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x8x32x128xbf16>
    %11 = stablehlo.transpose %arg3, dims = [1, 0] : (tensor<1024x3072xbf16>) -> tensor<3072x1024xbf16>
    %12 = stablehlo.dot_general %1, %11, contracting_dims = [1] x [0] : (tensor<32x3072xbf16>, tensor<3072x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<32x1024xbf16>
    %13 = stablehlo.reshape %12 : (tensor<32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>
    %14 = stablehlo.reshape %13 : (tensor<1x32x1024xbf16>) -> tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>
    %15 = stablehlo.transpose %14, dims = [0, 2, 1, 3] : (tensor<1x32x8x128xbf16>) -> tensor<1x8x32x128xbf16>
```
In particular, the matrix multiplications creating q, k, and v matrices are %2, %7, %12.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #25261: Facing IndexError with nonzero op in VadV2 model
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

ttnn.nonzero op throws the following error when running in loop,
`E       IndexError: vector::_M_range_check: __n (which is 1) >= this->size() (which is 1)`

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Checkout [keerthanar/vadv2_issue](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/keerthanar/vadv2_issue)
2. Run the following command,
`pytest tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vadv2/test_vadv2_issues.py::test_nonzero`

### Please complete the following environment information

WH (n150 or n300)

No linked pull requests found.

```bash
[yyzo-bh-17-special-sdjordjevic-for-reservation-29278:64036] *** End of error message ***
Segmentation fault (core dumped)
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run the following TTNN Python test to reproduce the issue:
```Python
import ttnn
import torch

device_id = 0
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=device_id)

batch_size, seq_len, embedding_dim, num_embeddings = 1, 32, 512, 128

input_shape = (batch_size, seq_len)
input_index = torch.randint(0, num_embeddings, input_shape)
input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(input_index, dtype=ttnn.uint32, device=device)

weights_shape = (num_embeddings, embedding_dim)
weights = torch.randn(weights_shape, requires_grad=True)
weights_ttnn = ttnn.from_torch(weights, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device)

grad_shape = (1, 1, batch_size * seq_len, embedding_dim)
grad_data = torch.randn(grad_shape, requires_grad=True)
grad_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(grad_data, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device)

output = ttnn.embedding_bw(input_tensor, weights_ttnn, grad_tensor, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16)
print(output)

ttnn.close_device(device)
```


### Expected behavior

The expectation is that the op executes correctly with no errors.

### Please complete the following environment information

OS version: Ubuntu 22.04
Blackhole machine: p100a
Metal commit: Latest main (9afb3d61fa7cbd262e6302d52d2adbbb7c7b30fd)

Issue #25254: Llama TG model perf failure
------------------------------------------------------------
On CI Llama70b TG model perf test is failing with error: 
`AttributeError: 'Function' object has no attribute 'pci_ids'`.

Here is link to run: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16335551105/job/46147288374#step:9:1078

Even though perf is captured there is some bug here. I locally was able to run successfully:
```
TT_METAL_ENABLE_ERISC_IRAM=1 FAKE_DEVICE=TG python -m tracy -p -o /home/divanovic/tt-metal/generated/profiler/tg-llama-demo-device-perf-non-overlapped-dispatch --check-exit-code -t 5000 -m pytest models/demos/llama3_subdevices/tests/test_decoder_device_perf.py::test_llama_demo
```

but this command failed locally which calls above in the subprocess:
```
TT_METAL_KERNELS_EARLY_RETURN=1 TT_METAL_ENABLE_ERISC_IRAM=1 FAKE_DEVICE=TG pytest models/demos/llama3_subdevices/tests/test_decoder_device_perf.py -k test_llama_TG_perf_device_non_overlapped_dispatch
```

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #25251: Improve tile exchange in ttnn sort
------------------------------------------------------------
# Problem 

Currently algorithm transfers only one tile at a time between cores. Initial experiments have shown that the biggest bottlenecks are potentially in transferring data between kernels and synchronizing all cores. 

# Solution

To reduce synchronization overhead transfer more than 1 tile at a time.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

```Python
import ttnn

device_id = 0
device = ttnn.open_device(device_id=device_id)

ones_cache = ttnn.ones(shape=(1,32,64,512), dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
zeros_input = ttnn.zeros(shape=(1,32,1,512), dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)

ttnn.update_cache(ones_cache, zeros_input, 1)

print(ones_cache)

ttnn.close_device(device)
```

### Expected behavior

The expectation is that the op executes correctly with no errors.
