Issue #25012: Argmax with int32 is not supported
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Trying to run `ttnn.argmax` with integers results in an error:
```
RuntimeError: TT_FATAL @ /proj_sw/user_dev/sgligorijevic/jaxbringups/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/reduction/argmax/device/argmax_op.cpp:57: input_tensor_a.dtype() == DataType::BFLOAT16
info:
Only BFLOAT16 is supported for inputs!
```

Trying to workaround argmax to be done in bfloat16 results in an incorrect result. My input looks something like:
```
input_data = torch.tensor([[49406.,   320.,  1125.,   539.,   320.,  2368., 49407.],
                          [49406.,   320.,  1125.,   539.,   320.,  1929., 49407.]])
```
and the output of argmax in bf16 is `[0, 0]` instead of `[6, 6]`, I suspect this is because both 49406 and 49407 when casted to bfloat16 are represented as 49408(at least according to https://flop.evanau.dev/float-converter ). Same happens when casted to TF32, either proper FP32 or INT32 support is needed

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the following code:
```
import torch
import ttnn

input_data = torch.tensor([[49406.,   320.,  1125.,   539.,   320.,  2368., 49407.],
                          [49406.,   320.,  1125.,   539.,   320.,  1929., 49407.]])


with ttnn.manage_device(0) as dev:
    input_ttnn = ttnn.from_torch(input_data, dtype=ttnn.int32, layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT, device=dev)

    res_ttnn = ttnn.argmax(input_ttnn, dim=-1, keepdim=False)
    res = ttnn.to_torch(res_ttnn)

```



### Expected behavior

_No response_

### Please complete the following environment information

Ubuntu 22.04
Metal hash 8fe3da9fb42bf368c275aa7657189f5dfc598d66 (admittedly 5 days out of date as of filing this bug)
N150 board

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24992: Repo clones in CI could be smaller
------------------------------------------------------------
This issue is to track workflow enhancements to not pull more than we need.

`git clone --filter=blob:none --recurse-submodules --also-filter-submodules --tags`

This cuts the size of the tt-metal git clone by about half (800M -> 400M, ish). I need to look at actions/checkout@v4 in the workflow files to see if there are any optimizations that can be done to reduce checkout size beyond the default options.

Also considering the option of having the machine CI provisioning include a local mirror of metal in a persistent workspace. Having runs checked out as worktrees instead of doing a fresh clone each time would be more sustainable from a bandwidth/network perspective.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24990: Functional Send Recv Op using Sockets
------------------------------------------------------------
Add support for a send and receive op that uses the MeshSocket structure, which provides intermesh communication support. The send op will write the host tensor from one mesh to a fifo in another mesh, and the recv op will read the data from that fifo and populate a tensor on its mesh.

This is currently only targeting functionality and not performance.
The send recv op also depends on the input and output tensors having the same spec, which is not something that can be enforced/verified. More generic support and performance optimizations will require send recv to additionally take in a spec of how the tensor gets divided and distributed into the fifo buffers.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24984: PNGs could be smaller
------------------------------------------------------------
We can shave ~30mb off of the shallow checkout running the PNGs through an optimizer. This is for tracking purposes.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24979: [Bug] Shape squeezing in ND sharding results in an incorrect page distribution
------------------------------------------------------------
If you shard a tensor with shape [5, 1, 11, 11] with shard shape [5, 1, 1, 1] it currently gets squeezed into ([55, 11], [5, 1]) which is incorrect. For example page 121 in the original sharding would get on the core 0, but not in the squeezes one.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24978: Use Mesh Coordinates instead of UMD ID for DPRINT file names
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Restarting the device can result in different mesh coordinates having different physical IDs. Resets during debug can make it hard to consistently print from the same device. 

### Describe the solution you'd like

Use Mesh coordinates instead of physical IDs as they're well described and consistent with the programming model.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24969: Use SFPU to Support FP32 Scalars for Avg Pool
------------------------------------------------------------
Even with FP32 DST accumulation average pool is still suffering from fairly significant floating point errors only passing rtol >= 0.01.  Based on this conversation:

https://tenstorrent.slack.com/archives/C062KKRTHKR/p1752251141057019

It should be possible to use the SFPU to support FP32 scalars (this is not possible with the SRC registers).  This will likely be less performant but should provide precise enough result to match Torch, and worst case can be an enable-able feature when the user needs maximum precision and performance is less of a concern.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24968: Use Large Kernel for All Generic Pool Parameterizations
------------------------------------------------------------
With the implementation of DST accumulation there are now no redundant reduction stages in the large kernel, therefore with some slight modifications to support non-max tile reductions, partial face reductions and 8 tile avg pool reductions, it should be possible to use the large kernel for small windows with minimal if any performance degradation.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24956: MoE clean up and minor issues
------------------------------------------------------------
- [ ] Deprecate Topology flag as it's derived implicitly in the `get_route` functions inside kernels
- [ ] Add proper memory estimates when optional output tensors are not set (L1 interleaved input will fail to give a correct memory estimate)
- [ ] Queue up num_links inside the op when it's not specified
- [ ] Allocate global semaphore internally when it's not passed in.
- [ ] Add test for long context length, where the memory usage is high and it switches to the `AllToAllTransferType::PageByPage`
- [ ] Add support for FABRIC_2D_DYNAMIC
- [ ] Add op perf tests for T3K/6U

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24955: Revisit Light-Metal Architecture and Runtime Integration
------------------------------------------------------------
### Issue

The current implementation of Light-Metal is tightly coupled with the runtime implementation, since it places hooks inside all of our APIs. This is not compatible with TT-Mesh, since the feature was never ported to the new codebase. Light-Metal needs to be revisited to figure out which parts to keep, rearchitect, or remove.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24948: Set tensor id for host tensors
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

When running op constraint queries, we get a lot of warnings such as:
```
2025-06-27 12:11:53.180 | warning  |          Always | Tensor doesn't have tensor_id, generating new one. Ideally this should not happen. Please set tensor_id for this tensor ahead of time. (graph_processor.cpp:279)
```

This is because when creating host tensors, we didn't call set_tensor_id, compared to device tensor: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/5ac9623e8939f69a65687caabe474bd75033b4a5/ttnn/core/tensor/tensor.cpp#L560

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Build tt-metal with tests: `./build_metal.sh --build-tests`.
2. Run anything that uses graph capture that creates a host tensor, for example: `./build/test/ttnn/unit_tests_ttnn --gtest_filter=TestGraphCaptureArgumentsTranspose.Transpose`

### Expected behavior

Host tensors should also have tensor ids.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24938: [Fabric] Enable No-op and short-circuited fabric connection open optimization, remove connection close
------------------------------------------------------------
after connection state is persistent in L1, and fabric routers no longer need to receive the upstream's L1 address to send credits to, then the entire fabric connection protocol can be optimized.

The following changes (high-level) can be made

### Fabric Connection Close
- essentially not not needed anymore
- Wait for connection open ack from fabric (avoid race w/ back-to-back ops)
- Implicitly kernel must write barrier at end

### Worker L1
- Add persistent connection-open flag per Fabric router/ethernet channel slot

### Fabric Connection Open
- if connection_open flag is true, do nothing (all info is there anyways) - 
  - at most, load some state from L1 into local memory (i.e. write offset into channel buffer)
- if false, issue noc read from fabric router to get noc x/y of last connection (this field already exists in fabric router L1)
  - send connection open as before
  - send my_x/y to the fabric router's connection field, set connection open flag on router
  - issue write to invalidate the cached connection field on the last worker, proceed as normal
  - set local connection_live to true (take the token)

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24928: comp_pcc returns 1.0 if one input is filled with all the same value
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

`assert_with_pcc` incorrectly passes if one tensor is full of a single value.

The root cause is that `comp_pcc` takes `np.min` of the correlation matrix.  When one input has a standard deviation of zero, this results in a matrix of `[[--, --], [--, 1.0]]`, whose minimum is 1.0.

One approach could be to pick corr_matrix[0, 1], but there is also a check in the same function for masked values and it returns 1.0 in that case (not sure why).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```py
import torch

from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc

def test_pcc():
    shape = (100,)
    assert_with_pcc(torch.full(shape, 1.0), torch.rand(shape), 1.0)
```

### Expected behavior

The test should fail.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24925: LLama TG incorrect profile measurement
------------------------------------------------------------
# Update

After discussing with @kpaigwar, @uaydonat and @sraizada-tt we have a solution for this issue.

We'll be updating `text_demo.py` measurements to include any post-processing (this includes `to_torch()` and anything that comes after until the next `to_torch()` call) and will have an option to not print each token until the very end (similar to what vLLM does).

This will ensure that the host time will be capturing a very accurate decode execution time, as seen in the image below on the measurement from `e2e tsu`.

Further to this I will be adding a reported average of iterations, in chunks of 32. This should give us a good picture of how the SDPA chunk configuration impacts the overall decode performance, as more and more iterations are computed.

<img width="3198" height="824" alt="Image" src="https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8a860ccf-70bf-494c-9aa8-55cf2691cd6a" />

## Context

On LLama TG, on the prefill side we are observing a wrong perf measurement using out demos profiler (basically `datetime`).
The issue was flagged when we run with batch-32, where we would see a perf of over 80 tok/s/u (sadly not real), instead of around 65 t/s/u.

The problem seems to be around the event synchronisation:
```
ttnn.event_synchronize(read_events.pop(0))
tt_out_tok = ttnn.to_torch(ttnn.get_device_tensors(tt_out_toks.pop(0))[0])[0, 0, 0, :32]
profiler.end(...)
```

A bit after this part of the code, we print each user output (every iteration).  To achieve that we decode with the tokenizer (on host) then slice the output to the last 100 characters then logger print those for each one of 32 users.
If we remove this print block, the final decode perf is within our expected numbers; ~65 t/s/u.

Changing what we print will alter the final number quite a lot. The more we print the higher it will be.
This suggests to me that the device computation is continuing in parallel, and when we start the profile (new timestamp) again, it will already be after a couple ms of decode execution.

## Things we've tried

### Force device synchronization
We've also noticed that adding a `ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)` after the `event_sync` gives us a consistent and  accurate performance. 

On 6U this sync seems to lower the output perf by about 3 tok/s/u (on a batch of 32) when compared to the same run without device sync and without output printing.

### ConcatMesh

We also tried forcing  a `ConcatMesh2DToTensor` instead of the original way of reading the output with `get_device_tensors`. 

``` 
tt_out_tok = ttnn.to_torch(tt_out_toks.pop(0), mesh_composer=ttnn.ConcatMesh2dToTensor(mesh_device, dims=(0,1), mesh_shape=tuple(mesh_device.shape)))
tt_out_tok = tt_out_tok[0,0,0,:32]
```

We observe no change with this method.

### Iteration discrepancies

Looking with @djordje-tt we identified that sometimes a decode iteration might report a slower performance but it's typically followed by a very fast iteration.
If you sum up the time of both iteration (in ms) and average out, it will give you a very similar number to any other iteration.

(See @djordje-tt comment: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24925#issuecomment-3062242723) 

This tells us that the host profile is not consistent and that the devices are still running in the background.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24883: [fabric] add fabric connect APIs in presentation style of Metalium dataflow API style
------------------------------------------------------------
Building off of recent additions where fabric router connection metadata is located in L1, fabric APIs can now be improved to expose a generic connect API.

e.g.
`auto connections = tt_fabric::linear::open_connections(fabric_port_ids,...);`
where `fabric_port_ids` would be passed as runtime args, and would be (today) interchangeable with ethernet channel IDs.

Issue #24881: [fabric] Add "route_id" variant APIs to use packet headers in reserved L1 space
------------------------------------------------------------
Depends on #24880

Update existing fabric device APIs to use `route_id` rather than direct packet_header pointers.

Rather than replacing the old functions, this work should just wrap them so that the prior APIs are still usable and they can optionally be deprecated at a later date.

According to the fabric instantiated, a route_id maps to multiple physical packet headers. This is because a user doesn't necessarily know if messages must be split across multiple fabric endpoints or not to initiate the message. Therefore, one ID maps to several contiguous packet headers; one per canonical "direction" in that fabric topology.

The route_id will be an argument for the fabric write APIs.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24880: [fabric] Add reserved L1 packet header space
------------------------------------------------------------
Allocate a pool of memory in worker L1 for fabric packet headers so that users are not required to manually allocate packet headers for every program. 

Suggested starting size for this region is `2 * sizeof(PACKET_HEADER) * NUM_TOPOLOGY_DIRECTIONS` or alternatively some fixed maximum that is atleast that large for 2D: (so 8 packet header slots).

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24870: Missing functionality for matmul/linear with bias and optional output tensor
------------------------------------------------------------
From [rlewczuk](https://github.com/rlewczuk) a comment in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24832:
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24832#issuecomment-3058578020

It seems that bias does not update optional_output_tensor when passed to bound_matmul(), it manifests itself in ttnn.linear(), for example following test fails:
```
def test_linear_with_bias_and_output(device):
    torch.manual_seed(0)

    torch_bias_tensor = torch.randn((32, 32), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_tensor_a = torch.randn((32, 32), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_tensor_b = torch.randn((32, 32), dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    torch_out_tensor = torch.zeros((32, 32), dtype=torch.float32)

    bias_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_bias_tensor,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,device=device)
    tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(torch_tensor_a,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,device=device)
    tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(torch_tensor_b,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,device=device)
    optional_output_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_out_tensor,layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,dtype=ttnn.float32,device=device)

    result_tensor = ttnn.linear(tensor_a, tensor_b, bias=bias_tensor, optional_output_tensor=optional_output_tensor)
    result_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(result_tensor)

    optional_output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(optional_output_tensor)

    assert_with_pcc(optional_output_tensor, result_tensor, pcc=0.9999)
```
It seems that result_tensor will contain result of tensor_a @ tensor_b + bias_tensor, while optional_output_tensor will contain just result of tensor_a @ tensor_b.

Needs to be investigated.

Issue #24857: Support slow dispatch via public metal API
------------------------------------------------------------
This issue tracks support for being able to transparently toggle between slow and fast dispatch modes.  This will enable much easier bringup of simulators, new hardware, or bespoke emulator environments.

Today the onus lies on the tests and many of them are written as such:

```c++
void launch_program(tt_metal::IDevice* device, tt_metal::Program& program) {
    if (getenv("TT_METAL_SLOW_DISPATCH_MODE")) {
        tt_metal::detail::LaunchProgram(device, program);
    } else {
        tt::tt_metal::CommandQueue& cq = device->command_queue();
        EnqueueProgram(cq, program, false);
        Finish(cq);
    }
}
```

But this is not scalable to all workloads that we'd like to run from various levels of the stack such as TTNN tests / models, forge models, or other users of metal.

It seems feasible to abstract this below the public metal API so the above code could be rewritten to be just:
```c++
void launch_program(tt_metal::IDevice* device, tt_metal::Program& program) {
    tt::tt_metal::CommandQueue& cq = device->command_queue();
    EnqueueProgram(cq, program, false);
    Finish(cq);
}
```

Under the hood, metal can have a virtualized (dummy) cq which running in slow dispatch mode that properly calls the correct overload.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24854: TileSlice's tile_index does not respect CB's FIFO Limit
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Tile Slice does not check to see if it is incrementing past the FIFO limit, when added the tile_index to the original pointer. Causing it to access unowned memory, and printing invalid data.

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/8257775ae4838e9962a50c090e8ef9b8229e2be5/tt_metal/hw/inc/debug/dprint_tile.h#L217C1-L218C1

This can be seen here. Solution would look like checking if the ptr now exceeds the fifo_limit, and then subtracting it by the fifo_size. My only concern is that this information may not be currently available on every TRISC core.



### Steps to reproduce the issue

This can be seen, if you ever input a tile number that is larger than the cb_length to print_full_tile
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/35cd25343a9900b92ba154d64a0f91920b8a9149/tt_metal/hw/inc/debug/dprint_pages.h#L74

### Expected behavior

It should loop around the end of the CB instead of continuing into unowned memory.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

Issue #24853: Add a compatibility layer to let 1D fabric APIs work correctly on ND Mesh topologies
------------------------------------------------------------
Design work still pending.

Some notes:

## API Portability
- objectives: "1d ring fabric kernel" just works on 2D torus

- validation: Add ddevice side (or host side if possible) validation with immediate failure
  - pass in a macro for current fabric, for each fabric API call, static_assert or define the impls if incompatible

- Compatibility/Translation layer 1D -> 2D
  -  Unicast: should just work for free
    - would require something on host   
    - MISSING: low-latency route generation from dest chip ID -> define
  - MCAST:
    - mcast_root_node -> direections/distance
      - mapping on host 

## Approach 1:
- program factory queries for ports for directions in cluster/mesh
- those ports are passed as runtime args in some canonical order
- if the underlying fabric is for a mesh, the port list is automatically grown to map to the 4 mesh directions
  - according to the direction that the line/ring CCL is laid out, the appropriate port directions are requested (N/S or E/W) while the unused ones are left empty.
- on device side, this canonical port ordering determines which ports the "fwd" and "bwd" hop distances are fed to.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24852: Migrate 1D Fabric API presentation style to Metalium dataflow API style
------------------------------------------------------------
This issue tracks the addition/migration of the following 1D fabric APIs to be inline with the intended API design (like those data-movement APIs in dataflow_api.h)

The existing fabric device API file can be used as a conceptual reference: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/tt_metal/fabric/hw/inc/tt_fabric_api.h

# Base Fabric Write APIs

The tentatively agreed upon naming convention is as follows:

namespace: `tt_fabric::linear::experimental`
file/directory: `tt_metal/fabric/hw/inc/linear/api.h`
fabric write function: `fabric_<fabric_level_pattern>_noc_<noc_cmd_type>(args)`

where `fabric_level_pattern` maps to the fabric level traffic patterns supported:
- unicast
- multicast

and `noc_cmd_type` maps to the different noc level commands supported:
- unicast_write
- unicast_atomic_inc
- scatter_write
- unicast_inline_write
- fused_unicast_with_atomic_inc

From the above, there should be 10 base write APIs.

### Arguments:
For the time being, the arguments should be consistent with 2D:
|arg idx | argument | incremental argument |
|-|-|-|
|0| tt_l1_ptr ClientInterfaceType client_interface | WorkerToEdmAdapter ptr |
| 1, ..., n-2| traffic pattern specific arguments | |
|n-1 | route_id | default=0; packet_header pointer until issue #24881 is resolved |


# Set State/Set Route APIs
These APIs are analogous to the current 1D fabric "*_to_(unicast|multicast)" packet header APIs. There are 

The desired final convention for these APIs is:
`void tt_fabric::linear::set_fabric_<pattern>(route_id: int, <pattern_specific_args...>);

However, to support a route_id directly, issue #24881 must first be completed. As an intermediate step, a temporary version of these APIs could be provided where instead of `route_id: int` as an argument, we have `packet_header: PACKET_HEADER_TYPE *` as the argument.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24850: [Fabric] 1D/2D API Consolidation Master Issue
------------------------------------------------------------
## 1D Fabric API migration/compatibility
- [ ]  #24852 
- [ ] #24880 
- [ ] #24883
- [ ] #24881
- [ ] #24884
- [ ] #24853
- [ ] #25750


## API cleanup
- [ ] Remove 2D pull APIs
- [ ] Move mesh API file and namespace to new `mesh` namespace and directory
- [ ] Migrate existing CCLs to use final APIs (those outlined by earlier issues)

## New Fabric Device API Summary

**Directory Structure:** tt_metal/fabric/hjw/inc/<topology>/api.h
Topology: {linear, mesh2d, torus2d}

**Function Naming Structure:** fabric_<fabric_level_pattern>_noc_<noc_level_cmd>
Fabric level: {unicast, multicast}
Noc Level: {unicast_write, semaphore_inc, inline_write, scatter_write, fused_write_atomic_inc}

### Function Argument Structure
There are 3 main types of arguments that must be passed to each of the fabric write functions:
1. Fabric connection bundle
 a. This is an opaque type that is different depending on the launched fabric
   i. i.e. for 1D, this would be two connections (fwd,bwd) and for 2D it would be 4 (north, east, south, west)
   ii. Arguments are allowed to be some NULL-like value to indicate there is no connection in that direction 
2. Main function arguments (e.g. src and destination addresses, number of hops each direction, etc.)
  a. * these are unique for each function
3. Route ID (default: 0)

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24847: matmul op does not check compatibility of output_tile and optional_output_tensor when both are provided
------------------------------------------------------------
In https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/matmul_op.cpp get_output_tile does not look at optional output tensor.

A TT_FATAL should check that at most one of them is provided.

And the else clause should check for the existence of optional_output_tensor and return its tile info if optional_output_tensor exists.

Should investigate what happens currently if a tiny tile optional_output_tensor is provided 
- output_tile is not provided (if there are no existing unit tests, some will need to be added)
- output_tile is a different tiny tile size (if there are existing unit tests, they will need to be removed since they would trigger the fatal)

Issue #24840: Conv2d graph capture does not correspond to real invocation
------------------------------------------------------------
In Forge we use graph capture to test whether a ttnn op works for a given set of inputs and parameters.

What I am observing is there is a Conv2d op which passes graph capture (does not throw any exception) and after parsing trace we get L1 footprint that is within allowed boundaries.  However, when I run it on device I get an OOM error. 

Branch: `rpavlovic/conv2d_repro`
Repro: 
```
./build_metal.sh -e  -b Debug --build-ttnn-tests
./build/test/ttnn/unit_tests_ttnn_emitc
```

Test output:

1st graph capture executes normally and these are numbers we parse from the trace
```
2025-07-10 12:52:52.930 | info     |              Op | Graph capture API passed (conv_test_repro.cpp:154)
2025-07-10 12:52:52.930 | info     |              Op | cb_peak_size_per_core: 581696 (conv_test_repro.cpp:165)
2025-07-10 12:52:52.930 | info     |              Op | l1_buffers_peak_per_core: 604192 (conv_test_repro.cpp:166)
2025-07-10 12:52:52.930 | info     |              Op | l1_output_buffer_per_core: 133120 (conv_test_repro.cpp:167)
```

Followed by on device run which fails:
```
2025-07-10 12:52:54.098 | critical |          Always | Statically allocated circular buffers in program 4 clash with L1 buffers on core range [(x=0,y=0) - (x=7,y=7)]. L1 buffer allocated at 543552 and static circular buffer region ends at 681600 (assert.hpp:107)
```

I understand that we can not poke allocators during NO_DISPATCH mode run, but here I have few questions that would help me understand more about memory allocation in the metal layer:
1. Is memory footprint from trace calculated correctly? Or does it look like we have some inaccuracies there. Mostly I am aiming at CB peak size 581696 versus `static circular buffer region ends at 681600`. These numbers are similar but still different.
2. Does `l1_buffers_peak_per_core` include `cb_peak_size_per_core` or are they exclusive of each other? 
3. What does this failed check represent? 
      i. How can we model it in the compiler? 
      ii. Is it actually memory problem as we ran out of it?
      iii. Or is it an op implementation bug where 2 memory segments overlap when they shouldn't?

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24834: EmptyTensorTestWithShape/EmptyTensorTest.Combinations/2 Fails w/ ASan enabled
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16175050039/job/45658747932

This test passes, but when ASan is turned on, an invalid read of 2 bytes is exposed.

```
[ RUN      ] EmptyTensorTestWithShape/EmptyTensorTest.Combinations/2
2025-07-09 16:50:52.807 | info     |   SiliconDriver | Opened PCI device 0; KMD version: 2.0.0; API: 2; IOMMU: disabled (pci_device.cpp:198)
2025-07-09 16:50:52.827 | info     |            Test | Running test with shape=Shape([]), dtype=DataType::BFLOAT16, layout=Layout::ROW_MAJOR, memory_config=MemoryConfig(memory_layout=TensorMemoryLayout::INTERLEAVED,buffer_type=BufferType::L1,shard_spec=std::nullopt,nd_shard_spec=std::nullopt,created_with_nd_shard_spec=0) (test_create_tensor.cpp:126)
=================================================================
==115==ERROR: AddressSanitizer: heap-buffer-overflow on address 0x5020006f45b1 at pc 0x560f45223fba bp 0x7ffc817960d0 sp 0x7ffc81795890
READ of size 2 at 0x5020006f45b1 thread T0
    #0 0x560f45223fb9 in __asan_memcpy (/usr/bin/tt-nn-validation-basic+0x99afb9) (BuildId: d90a2a17da3e32f2)
    #1 0x7f0f630ca789 in void tt::tt_metal::memcpy_to_device<false>(void*, void const*, unsigned long) /work/tt_metal/impl/dispatch/memcpy.hpp:94:17
```

The test checks the behavior of writing an empty tensor to the device.

Why do we need to read 2 bytes from an empty buffer?

Something is wrong in `ttnn`.

I did my best to root cause this issue, but I am burning too much time.

What I found was that the `mesh_buffer` associated with the created `Tensor` thinks it is of size 2. This happens to be the size of BFLOAT16 ... so that is suspicious.

I believe the problem lies somewhere in the creation of the Tensor or creation of TensorSpec / TensorLayout.

Somewhere here: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/d2a0e76220cbd2a14ce5e2422efed183a85be581/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/gtests/tensor/common_tensor_test_utils.cpp#L51

I am going to disable this test so that I can make progress on my work.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. `./build_metal.sh --build-type ASan --build-tests`
2. `export TT_METAL_HOME=$(pwd)`
3. `./build/test/ttnn/unit_tests_ttnn_tensor --gtest_filter=EmptyTensorTestWithShape/EmptyTensorTest.Combinations/2`
4. See error


### Expected behavior

Don't fail

### Please complete the following environment information

Issue #24810: random Event Order Issue on BH
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

after running multipe conv2d back to back (number of invocation might vary) the issue appears (see at the bottom).
my suspicion is it's a symptom of OO L1 Memory issue.

Issue #24809: Profiling TM ops
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, the profiler assigns a latency of 1 for all data movement ops. However, the TM ops are actually bound by the latency of noc transactions, and by the LLK compute time if applicable. In this issue, we will discuss a general model that estimates the latency of TM ops based on the noc bandwidth and the tensor specs.
The model below focuses on computing the op latency based on noc transactions only. The LLK overhead of the TM ops that include LLK compute kernels will be added later.
We will base our calculations on this excel sheet (https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nN1ZPUNc2f0d6YEenBOHS7BdkpGlBysZ6SCArinGOrE/edit?gid=2113527795#gid=2113527795) that assigns bandwidth values for different noc transaction types and sizes
The idea of the model is as follows:
1- Extract the specs from the input and output tensors and find the transaction size
2- Find the number of read transactions required by the input tensor and the write transactions required by the output tensor, and distribute the work among multiple cores if possible
3- Calculate the latency needed by the noc transactions per core based on the excel sheet values

For each of the steps, the model makes a set of assumptions. Below is a brief explanation of the step and the assumptions

1- The tensor specs are extracted from the input and the output tensors: layout, memory configuration, element size, etc. The transaction size is set to a tile size in case of tile layout, and to the row size in case of row major layout. For sharded tensors, the transaction size is set to the shard width size, and it also spans over multiple rows in the case of RM height sharded tensors.
 
2- To properly parallelize the op while avoiding congestion, the model limits the number of cores used to the aggregate bandwidth available. The model increments the number of cores until all the channels are saturated, or the maximum number of cores is reached. The limits are specific to the device architecture (wormhole vs grayskull). For a wormhole machine for example, we have a maximum of 12 channels for DRAM, and the maximum bandwidth per channel is around 35 GB/s. For L1, we assume that noc0 is assigned to the read kernel and noc1 to the write kernel, and the effective bandwidth for each can reach 30GB/s for (8 + 8) noc lines.The model also assumes that the distribution of transactions is balanced across all cores

3- The model assumes full parallelism between cores, so the latency can be computed based on the transactions in a single core.
For the read transactions, we only assume that they can be local if the input and output tensors are sharded over L1  on the same core grids. The worker cores are the sharding grid cores in this case. Otherwise, the transactions are not considered local, and the bandwidth values are selected accordingly.
We use the excel sheet values to compute the total latency of all the read transactions per core based on the type and the size of the transaction, and we use linear interpolation if the size is not in the table (for RM tensors for example)
Similar approach is applied to the write transactions

3- To account for the overlap between the reader and the writer, the model assigns the total latency to the maximum of the reader and the writer latencies.

This roofline model was tested on a few TM ops like pad, fill_pad, reshard, reshape, move, repeat and slice
The above assumptions were added to keep it general and to solve the outliers seen in these tests

In general, the model roofline shows a low utilization for most of the tests, but can reach high values for some: reshard can reach up to 92% for example
This can be due to the op implementation. For example in pad op, tile layout does not have multicore implementation and falls back to 1 core. In this op, the maximum utilization doesn’t exceed 70% and can be so low for some tests.
It can be also due to some overestimation or underestimation in the model. Some of those are discussed below.

This model has some optimistic/pessimistic assumptions that can explain the results:
- Assuming perfect overlap between reader and writer, which might not be always the case
- Assuming perfect load balancing between the cores
- Being conservative about the transaction size; it can be larger if we are aware of the data distribution and the work
- Noc transactions can be local for some worker cores
- The computed number of cores might not be ideal. It might be better to have a lower number of cores for sharded tensors for example, or a higher number of worker cores for some high load tests
- Tested on unit_tests of ops separately, which might not be representative of the op
- Generalizing for TM ops: This can be targeted if we customize the model for each op separately
Some observations for example:
    - Fill pad op can reach a higher utilization if the model assumes that the tiled tensors are also read as RM tensors, similar to the implementation of the op
    - Slice op just needs to consider writer kernels


Note: the model output is currently stored in PM compute column of the output excel sheet
The link to the draft PR: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24812

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24800: AllGather needs to support wider range of shapes
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

TT-train to needs support of CCL ops for distributed training.
More information about shapes: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/18lQ_dJpodMkoDFZjt7TfHdt0cEGsa5GCxxRKDzErGvM/edit?gid=0#gid=0

Please make sure that operation work for different mesh shapes (as an example: (1, 2), (2, 1), (2, 2), (1, 4), (4, 1), (2, 4), (4, 2), (1, 8), (8, 1))

CCL operation should be implemented with FABRIC2D as it will be used with multi-host setup (which require FABRIC2D). 


Test code to verify that it works
```
import ttnn
import torch
import pytest

def check_all_gather(dim, shape, mesh_shape, output_shape, cluster_idx=None):
    multiplier = mesh_shape[0] * mesh_shape[1] if cluster_idx is None else mesh_shape[cluster_idx]
    expected_shape = list(shape)
    expected_shape[dim] *= multiplier
    for i in range(len(expected_shape)):
        if expected_shape[i] != output_shape[i]:
            return False
    return True

@pytest.mark.parametrize("dim", [0, 1, 2, 3])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("cluster_idx", [0, 1, None])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("shape", [[1, 1, 1, 1],
                                   [1, 1, 1, 8], 
                                   [1, 1, 16, 16], 
                                   [1, 1, 32, 32],
                                   [1, 1, 32, 32], 
                                   [1, 1, 16, 32], 
                                   [1, 1, 4096, 16], 
                                   [1, 1, 4096, 32], 
                                   [1, 1, 4096, 64], 
                                   [1, 1, 4096, 128], 
                                   [1, 1, 4, 2048],
                                   [1, 1, 8, 2048],
                                   [1, 1, 16, 2048],
                                   [1, 1, 32, 2048],
                                   [1, 1, 64, 2048],
                                   [1, 1, 2048, 2048],
                                   [1, 32, 4096, 16],
                                   [1, 32, 4096, 32],
                                   [2, 32, 4096, 16],
                                   [4, 32, 4096, 16],
                                   [8, 32, 4096, 16],
                                   [16, 32, 4096, 16],
                                   [16, 32, 4096, 32]])
@pytest.mark.parametrize("mesh_device", [pytest.param((2, 4), id="2x4_grid"), pytest.param((1, 8), id="1x8_grid")], indirect=True)
@pytest.mark.parametrize("device_params", [{"fabric_config": ttnn.FabricConfig.FABRIC_1D}], indirect=True)
def test_all_gather_async(dim, cluster_idx, shape, mesh_device):
    print(mesh_device)
    print(mesh_device.shape)

    volume = 1
    for s in shape:
        volume *= s
    torch_tensor = torch.arange(0, volume, dtype=torch.bfloat16).reshape(shape)

    # Shard input activations on batch dimension to devices in the mesh
    with ttnn.distribute(ttnn.ReplicateTensorToMesh(mesh_device)):
        ttnn_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(
            torch_tensor,
            dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
            layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
            device=mesh_device,
        )

    ccl_sub_device_crs = ttnn.CoreRangeSet({ttnn.CoreRange(ttnn.CoreCoord(0, 0), ttnn.CoreCoord(7, 1))})
    global_semaphores = [ttnn.create_global_semaphore(mesh_device, ccl_sub_device_crs, 0) for _ in range(2)]

    print('ttnn shape:', ttnn_tensor.shape)

    ttnn_gathered_tensor = None
    if cluster_idx is None:
        ttnn_gathered_tensor = ttnn.experimental.all_gather_async(
            ttnn_tensor,
            dim,
            global_semaphores[0],
            num_links=1,
            topology=ttnn.Topology.Linear
        )
    else:
        ttnn_gathered_tensor = ttnn.experimental.all_gather_async(
            ttnn_tensor,
            dim,  # [0, 1, 2, 3]
            cluster_idx,  # 0 or 1
            mesh_device,
            ttnn.Topology.Linear,
            global_semaphores[0]
        )

    result_shape = list(ttnn_gathered_tensor.shape)
    print(ttnn_gathered_tensor.shape)

    mesh_device_shape = list(mesh_device.shape)

    ttnn.close_mesh_device(mesh_device)
    del mesh_device

    assert check_all_gather(dim, shape, mesh_device_shape, result_shape, cluster_idx), \
        f"All gather failed for dim {dim}, shape {shape}, mesh_shape {mesh_device_shape}, " \
        f"result_shape {result_shape}, cluster_idx {cluster_idx}"
```

------------------------------------------------------------

Issue #24786: Add to_list() method to TT-NN Tensor Python API
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Currently, when working with TT-NN’s Python API, converting a tensor to a native Python list requires two steps:

`py_list = ttnn_tensor.to_torch().tolist()`

This approach is verbose and depends on PyTorch. 
Ideally, we should be able to call ttnn_tensor.to_list() directly to get a list.

`py_list = ttnn_tensor.to_list()`

### Describe the solution you'd like

We should add a to_list() method on TT-NN’s Tensor type that:

- For a device tensor, copies the data back to host memory and returns a nested Python list.
- For a host tensor, directly returns a nested Python list without extra copies.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

_No response_

Issue #24771: Migrate tensor caches to the new multi-host aware serialization format
------------------------------------------------------------
The old format did not record any tensor topology information, and instead serialized and flushed the tensor as N buffers to disk one-by-one. This became problematic with the adoption of `DistributedHostBuffer` that requires this information, to properly support multi-host.

The workaround was to require `MeshDevice` in `ttnn.load_tensor` - we will use device shape to create `DistributedHostBuffer`.

In addition, the old serialization relied on the legacy `DistributedTensorConfig` to determine how the N buffers were distributed across mesh. `DistributedTensorConfig` was replaced by `DistributedHostBuffer` at the metal layer and extended to support multi-host, so with the tensor cache migration, we will be able to remove `DistributedTensorConfig` from the code base as well.

Steps to make this happen:
1. Redirect `load_tensor` / `dump_tensor` to the new serialization functions.
2. Switch the shared file system access in CI set up to `rw` access instead of `ro`.
3. Run the pipelines, which should automatically re-generate and write the tensor caches to the file system.

No linked pull requests found.

### Additional context

Added by Raymond:

Unfortunately, this may be more painful, depending on the status of certain issues.

There are several categories of machines, all of which may require different changes to get what we need here.
Refer to the table. In general:

- B2 CIv1 bare metal machines should be quite frictionless and easy to set up IOMMU (unless we need to make BIOS changes)
- C3 may be a bit more friction, but we can loop in CIv2 team on this and we already did this for BH. I suspect we can just enable it for WH and slowly move over any metal CI running on CIv2 from non-iommu runners to iommu. Contact here is Anirudh on cloud team
- B3 CIv1 VMs is still a big chunk of metal CI, and that is going to be the big killer... I believe this is a cross-team effort with [cloud](https://github.com/tenstorrent/cloud/issues/3581) and they are busy with other things currently. So if you need ALL runners in Metal CI to use iommu, this is going to be an issue. We could start with certain tests running on iommu, and others still with hugepages. We also can switch certain machines with each box, but not all machines in one box have to iommu. We can discuss.

CIv1 = metal only, managed by metal infra, not shared, runs big chunk of metal CI, but not PR or merge gates
CIv2 = shared, managed by cross-teams, run on PR + merge gates on metal CI

Issue #24749: ttnn.repeat does not support Int32 as the input dtype
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

ttnn.repeat does not support Int32 as the input dtype

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: © 2025 Tenstorrent Inc.

# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0

from functools import reduce
from math import prod

import pytest
import torch
import ttnn

from models.utility_functions import comp_pcc
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc

layouts = [ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT, ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT]

dtypes = [(torch.int32, ttnn.int32)]
shapes = [(1,), (2,), (2, 3), (4, 16, 3, 1), (4, 3, 1, 2, 2)]
repeat_shapes = [
    (1,),
    (1, 2),
    (4, 3, 2, 1),
    (2, 3, 4, 5, 2),
    (2048,),
]


def _get_size(larger, smaller) -> int:
    return prod([a * b for a, b in zip(((1,) * (len(larger) - len(smaller)) + smaller), larger)])


def _get_final_size(shape, reshape):
    if len(shape) > len(reshape):
        return _get_size(shape, reshape)
    else:
        return _get_size(reshape, shape)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("layout", layouts)
@pytest.mark.parametrize("dtype", dtypes)
@pytest.mark.parametrize("shape", shapes)
@pytest.mark.parametrize("repeat_shape", repeat_shapes)
def test_repeat(device, layout, dtype, shape, repeat_shape):
    torch_dtype, ttnn_dtype = dtype

    if layout == ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT and (
        prod(shape) % ttnn.TILE_SIZE != 0 or _get_final_size(shape, repeat_shape) % ttnn.TILE_SIZE != 0
    ):
        pytest.skip("Tensor not suitable for tile layout")

    if len(repeat_shape) < len(shape):
        pytest.skip("PyTorch repeat dim must be >= tensor dim (although we can handle this).")

    mul = lambda x, y: x * y
    torch_input_tensor = torch.arange(0, reduce(mul, shape, 1), dtype=torch_dtype).reshape(shape)

    torch_result = torch_input_tensor.repeat(repeat_shape)
    input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(torch_input_tensor, layout=layout, device=device, dtype=ttnn_dtype)

    output = ttnn.repeat(input_tensor, ttnn.Shape(repeat_shape))
    output = ttnn.to_torch(output)
    assert (
        output.shape == torch_result.shape
    ), f"Output shape {output.shape} does not match torch shape {torch_result.shape}"

    assert_with_pcc(torch_result, output, 0.9999)
```

When running the above test with `pytest` I get the following error: `Can only work with bfloat16/float32 or uint32 tensors`

### Expected behavior

We don't expect this error during the test execution

### Please complete the following environment information

tt-metal branch: main

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24744: Significant Compute Execution Unit Latency on First API Invocation.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

**Overview**
While profiling the performance of an SFPU kernel (kind of accumulations), I observed that the first API invocation to trigger SFPU execution consistently takes significantly longer (~5451 cycles) than all subsequent calls (~191–193 cycles), regardless of the specific API used.
Also, i got to know that this issue is not limited to SFPU after doing more experiments.

So here i provide a version of software to reproduce the issue targeting `add_tiles` API used in `cumsum` OP.

**Describe the bug**  
 Being mentioned above, There appears to be a large one-time latency cost on the very first `add_tiles` API invocation. This cost (~5451 cycles) is consistently observed on first invocation, while subsequent invocations exhibit normal performance (~400–500 cycles).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Clone the repository: [yechan/reprod-first-call-inflation](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/yechan/reprod-first-call-inflation)

2. Checkout the working branch (based on latest main)

3. Build with profiler
`./build_metal.sh --enable-profiler`

4. Run the following profiling command:
- To profile, 
```TT_METAL_DEVICE_PROFILER=1 TT_METAL_PROFILER_DIR=`pwd` TT_METAL_PROFILER_SYNC=1 
pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_cumsum.py::test_cumsum```
- To analyze target profiled zones, 
```python analyze.py ./.logs/profile_log_device.csv 1 1 ADD_TILES```
   - I wrote this script to help you guys to analzye the result easier. 
   - `1 1` is core coordinates and `ADD_TILES` is zone name.
   - If it doesn't work, please check out the generated `profile_log_device.csv` manually.


### Expected behavior

If you run the `analyze.py` script, it will show below resutls on your terminal.
The first line is a list of profiled durations against `DeviceZoneScopedN("ADD_TILES")`
```

Issue #24731: Add PyTorch-like __ttnn_function__ Protocol for Universal TTNN Tensor Wrapping
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

I want to be able to write tensor wrapper classes that can track provenance, trace, debug, or modify tensor ops, similar to what __torch_function__ allows in PyTorch or __array_ufunc__ in NumPy.

Right now, since TTNN ops don’t dispatch on custom/wrapped tensor types, it’s not possible to build wrappers that work seamlessly across the API (e.g., for tracing or meta-programming) without monkeypatching every function.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I want to be able to do something like
```
class TracedTTNNTensor(ttnn.Tensor):
  def __init__(self, tensor):
    .....

  def __ttnn_function__(self, func, types, args, kwargs):
    print("Tracing:", func.__name__)
```
Then,
```
x = TracedTTNNTensor(ttnn.from_numpy(...))
y = ttnn.add(x, x)  # Would trigger __ttnn_function__ on x
```
### Describe alternatives you've considered

I've tried monkey patching every element in the module ttnn, but that breaks when the patching is not done in the beginning of the program. Other methods I've tried were too brute force/invasive to existing code.

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24723: MoE CCL metadata remap ->  sparse matmulToken2Expert
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

MoE matmul implementation requires a [b,s,1,E/D] tensor that indicates that a given token has been activated by an expert on a device and contains the corresponding  top K score

### Describe the solution you'd like

A simple local TM Op that transforms the "global" MoE CCL metadata tensors to local metadata tensors expected by the MoE sparse matmul.
Inputs:
• expert mapping [D (/D, 1, E, D]. Maps experts to devices
• expert metadata [D(/D), B, S, K] maps tokens to experts
• TopK scores [D(/D), B, S, K] Scores for top K experts for each token.

Output:
Token2Expert [1, B, S, E (/D)]. Indicates if a token was activated by a given on expert on a given device; if so the entry is the TopK score for that expert, 0 if not.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

Described here https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DbwoV6neTNAgEGVzzGEKOXwJm6MnnQUfMQ4f5OQjFJc/edit?tab=t.0
and here https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24405#issuecomment-3045488116

Issue #24688: sharded permute op configuration in ufld_v2 affects mobilenet_v2 PCC
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

- mobilenet_v2 fails with PCC assertion error due to **ufld_v2** sharded permute op configuration in device perf CI ([here](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16126692042/job/45505653758#step:5:3958)).
- After executing a sharded permute op unit test (ufld_v2 input configuration) followed by running the Mobilenet_v2, Mobilenet_v2 model's PCC drops significantly to `0.83`.


### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Go to branch - [venkatesh/fix_permute](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/tree/venkatesh/fix_permute)
2. Run the unit test for the sharded permute op configuration: 
 `pytest tests/ttnn/integration_tests/ufld_v2/test_unit_permute.py::test_sharded_permute`
3. Now, Run MobileNetV2 device perf test:
`pytest models/demos/mobilenetv2/tests/test_perf_mobilenetv2.py::test_perf_device_bare_metal_mobilenetv2`




- mobilenet_v2 passes with correct PCC after a hardware reset via tt-smi.
- Note: The interleaved format of permute does not impact mobilenet_v2 PCC. (Validated by its corresponding unit test -  [here](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/compare/main...venkatesh/fix_permute#diff-61e36e4ce804d8ec6baa0fd684e8b27e8d8003e266e7a23783044c5685834b58R18))

Expects Passing PCC for Mobilenet_v2.

Issue #24681: DRAM-sharded matmul hanging when invoked twice
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

During the MLP bringup for DeepSeek we noticed that, when running in decode mode, the module would not produce a result and would just hang for over 10 minutes after the execution was killed. We managed to narrow the issue down to the second run of a matmul hanging the execution. If running in slow dispatch mode, this issue appears once we run the op. If ran in the fast dispatch mode (the default), this becomes noticeable once we try to print the contents of the tensor (i.e. when it has to be read back). This also happens on a single-card execution.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Run the script below in the environment build from main:
```python
import torch
import ttnn
from ttnn.types import TILE_SIZE

COMPUTE_KERNEL_CONFIG_LOFI = ttnn.WormholeComputeKernelConfig(
    math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.LoFi,
    math_approx_mode=False,
    fp32_dest_acc_en=False,
    packer_l1_acc=True,
)


def dram_sharded_weight_config(k, n, dram_grid_size):
    """Create DRAM-sharded memory config for width-sharded tensors"""
    dram_cores = dram_grid_size.x  # WH has 12 dram cores, P150 has 8, P100 has 7
    assert dram_grid_size.y == 1, "Current dram sharding assumes y dim is 1"
    dram_weight_grid = ttnn.CoreRangeSet(
        {
            ttnn.CoreRange(
                ttnn.CoreCoord(0, 0),
                ttnn.CoreCoord(dram_grid_size.x - 1, dram_grid_size.y - 1),
            )
        }
    )

    shard_spec = ttnn.ShardSpec(
        dram_weight_grid,
        (k, ttnn.core.roundup(ttnn.core.divup(n, dram_cores), TILE_SIZE)),
        ttnn.ShardOrientation.ROW_MAJOR,
    )
    return ttnn.MemoryConfig(ttnn.TensorMemoryLayout.WIDTH_SHARDED, ttnn.BufferType.DRAM, shard_spec)


def report_tensor(tt_tensor, name):
    for e in [
        ".buffer_address()",
        ".device()",
        ".dtype",
        ".is_allocated()",
        ".is_sharded()",
        ".layout",
        ".logical_volume()",
        ".memory_config()",
        ".padded_shape",
        ".storage_type()",
        ".tensor_id",
        ".volume()",
    ]:
        print(f"{name}{e} = {eval('tt_tensor' + e)}")
    print(f"{name} = {tt_tensor}")


def big_print(msg):
    """Print a message with a big border."""
    border = "=" * (len(msg) + 4)
    print(f"\n{border}\n  {msg}\n{border}\n\n")


def test_forward_pass():
    mesh_device = ttnn.open_mesh_device(ttnn.MeshShape(1, 1))

    activation_sharding_num_cores = 7
    batch_size = 32
    dim = 7168
    hidden_dim = 18432

    input_memory_config = ttnn.MemoryConfig(
        memory_layout=ttnn.TensorMemoryLayout.WIDTH_SHARDED,
        buffer_type=ttnn.BufferType.L1,
        shard_spec=ttnn.ShardSpec(
            grid=ttnn.num_cores_to_corerangeset(
                activation_sharding_num_cores, (mesh_device.core_grid.x, mesh_device.core_grid.y)
            ),
            shard_shape=(
                ttnn.core.roundup(batch_size, TILE_SIZE),
                ttnn.core.roundup(ttnn.core.divup(dim, activation_sharding_num_cores), TILE_SIZE),
            ),
            shard_orientation=ttnn.ShardOrientation.ROW_MAJOR,
        ),
    )
    print(input_memory_config)

    w1 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch.rand((1, 1, dim, hidden_dim)),
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat4_b,
        layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
        device=mesh_device,
        memory_config=dram_sharded_weight_config(
            dim,
            hidden_dim,
            mesh_device.dram_grid_size(),
        ),
        mesh_mapper=ttnn.ttnn.shard_tensor_to_mesh_mapper(mesh_device, 1),
    )
    w2 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch.rand((1, 1, hidden_dim, dim)),
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat4_b,
        layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
        device=mesh_device,
        memory_config=dram_sharded_weight_config(
            hidden_dim,
            dim,
            mesh_device.dram_grid_size(),
        ),
        mesh_mapper=ttnn.ttnn.shard_tensor_to_mesh_mapper(mesh_device, 0),
    )
    tt_input = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch.randn(1, 1, batch_size, dim),
        device=mesh_device,
        mesh_mapper=ttnn.ReplicateTensorToMesh(mesh_device),
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        memory_config=input_memory_config,
        layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
    )

    # Run the ops
    big_print("w1_out = ttnn.linear(tt_input, **cfg['w1'])")
    w1_out = ttnn.linear(
        tt_input,
        input_tensor_b=w1,
        memory_config=ttnn.L1_WIDTH_SHARDED_MEMORY_CONFIG,
        program_config=ttnn.MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCastDRAMShardedProgramConfig(
            in0_block_w=1,
            per_core_M=1,
            per_core_N=ttnn.core.divup(hidden_dim, TILE_SIZE * activation_sharding_num_cores),
            fused_activation=None,
        ),
        compute_kernel_config=COMPUTE_KERNEL_CONFIG_LOFI,
    )
    report_tensor(w1_out, "w1_out")

    big_print("tt_output = ttnn.linear(w1_out, **cfg['w2'])")
    tt_output = ttnn.linear(
        w1_out,
        input_tensor_b=w2,
        memory_config=ttnn.L1_WIDTH_SHARDED_MEMORY_CONFIG,
        program_config=ttnn.MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCastDRAMShardedProgramConfig(
            in0_block_w=1,
            per_core_M=1,
            per_core_N=ttnn.core.divup(dim, TILE_SIZE * activation_sharding_num_cores),
            fused_activation=None,
        ),
        compute_kernel_config=COMPUTE_KERNEL_CONFIG_LOFI,
    )
    report_tensor(tt_output, "tt_output")
    big_print("FINISHED EXECUTION")


test_forward_pass()
```

### Expected behavior

The script finishes execution within at most 15 minutes.

### Please complete the following environment information

system: T3K (IRD)
docker: latest build

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24671: Multi-host aware TT-NN debugging facilities
------------------------------------------------------------
Existing debugging / logging infra needs to be extended to support multi-host. User journey (feedback from @yieldthought):
* What will the user see on their terminal if they put a print statement in their code? What if that statement is only hit on one of the remote hosts?
* What will my program see if an exception is raised on one of the remote hosts?
* What will my program see if one of the hosts goes into an infinite loop and doesn’t hit the next collective?
* What will the user see if they put breakpoint()  in their code intending to drop into pdb when it is hit?

Some no brainer action items:
1. Every print needs to be accompanied with a host rank. It needs to be clear where the output is coming from
2. Every error / exception similarly has a rank ID attached, along with as much of the debugging info (tensor shapes, etc).
3. All communications between hosts need timeouts. This is tricky one, as currently Metal has busy waits in several places in the code base.
4. Need to provide a way to terminate the process(es).
5. Need a way to query for a host rank, to allow limited conditional execution for a designated host (e.g. rank 0 host to read/write outputs). 



CC @cfjchu

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24669: New pipeline for CCL op perf testing
------------------------------------------------------------
We don't need to run end to end models to test CCL op perf. A new pipeline is needed.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24668: memcpy_to_device is broken for small source buffers
------------------------------------------------------------
This code assumes that the source buffer is at least 32 bytes in size.

If it is not, it will read beyond the end of the allocated buffer, triggering [Address Sanitizer violations](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/16121234355/job/45488111680).

Could potentially cause segfault.

Here is the culprit line:

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/e4ebdd7455faed29dbfc2109fceb634d47b90d90/tt_metal/impl/dispatch/memcpy.hpp#L66

Note that the size of the source buffer is not an input to the function, so it blindly assumes that the source buffer is > 32 bytes, and it has no way to confirm that.

This is not a drop in replacement for memcpy.

Probably this function should be rewritten or deleted.

Is it really giving performance gain?

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24651: [BH][Fabric] Switch to noc_async_writes for credits sent to workers
------------------------------------------------------------
We use noc inline DW writes for sending credits to workers. On BH, these are lower performance if writing to non-stream-register memory.

For BH - noc inline dw writes are implemented as:
- write word to L1 in reserved memory
- issue noc_async_write
- do noc write sent barrier

Given the above, it would be more performant for us to just do our own sequence of:

- write word to reserved location in L1 
  -  this is not runtime reserved, but just a minor carve-out in fabric router's L1 map
- issue stateful noc_async_write
- **DON'T BARRIER**

We can skip the barrier in the specific case, specifically because we are using unbounded counters, monitonically increasing counters, so it's okay if the value is written multiple times before the noc actually executes the send.

Alternatively, we can negotiate with runtime and see if we can reserve some non-auto-inc stream registers for this purpose and keep code-paths the same for WH and BH

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24635: ttnn.subtract and ttnn.remainder do not support implicit broadcast on both operands
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

ttnn.subtract and ttnn.remainder operations do not support broadcast on both operands

### Steps to reproduce the issue

The following test captures the failing scenarios:

```
import pytest

import torch

import ttnn
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import assert_with_pcc

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "shapes",
    [
        [[1, 1, 1], [8, 16, 32]],
        [[1, 1, 32], [8, 16, 32]],
    ],
)
def test_sub_implicit_broadcast(device, shapes):
    torch.manual_seed(0)

    torch_input_tensor_a = torch.rand(shapes[0], dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_input_tensor_b = torch.rand(shapes[1], dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_output_tensor = torch.sub(torch_input_tensor_a, torch_input_tensor_b)

    input_tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_tensor_a, dtype=ttnn.float32, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )
    input_tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_tensor_b, dtype=ttnn.float32, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )

    output_tensor = ttnn.sub(input_tensor_a, input_tensor_b, dtype=ttnn.float32, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    assert_with_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor, 0.9999)

@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    "shapes",
    [
        [[1, 1, 1], [8, 16, 32]],
        [[1, 1, 32], [8, 16, 32]],
        [[1, 16, 32], [8, 16, 32]],
        [[8, 16, 1], [1, 1, 32]],
        [[1, 1, 32], [8, 16, 1]],
        [[8, 1, 32], [8, 16, 1]],
        [[8, 16, 1], [8, 1, 32]],
    ],
)
def test_remainder_implicit_broadcast(device, shapes):
    torch.manual_seed(0)

    torch_input_tensor_a = torch.rand(shapes[0], dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_input_tensor_b = torch.rand(shapes[1], dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_output_tensor = torch.remainder(torch_input_tensor_a, torch_input_tensor_b)

    input_tensor_a = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_tensor_a, dtype=ttnn.float32, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )
    input_tensor_b = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_tensor_b, dtype=ttnn.float32, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, device=device, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG
    )

    output_tensor = ttnn.remainder(input_tensor_a, input_tensor_b, dtype=ttnn.float32, memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output_tensor)

    assert_with_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor, 0.9999)
```

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24634: Conv3D: Fatal error when using custom strides
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

The `conv3d` operation currently enforces that strides must be `(1,1,1)`.  
Passing a different stride configuration, e.g., `(2, 14, 14)`, triggers a `TT_FATAL` in device code.

```
RuntimeError: TT_FATAL @ /root/home/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/experimental/conv3d/device/conv3d_device_operation.cpp:75: config.stride[0] == 1 && config.stride[1] == 1 && config.stride[2] == 1
info:
Strides must be (1,1,1). got (2, 14, 14)
backtrace:
--- /root/home/tt-metal/ttnn/ttnn/_ttnn.so(+0xff416a) [0x7fb569d1f16a]
```
It seems the current implementation enforces strides to always be (1,1,1).



### Steps to reproduce the issue
```
import torch
import pytest
import torch.nn as nn
import ttnn
from loguru import logger
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import check_with_pcc

def prepare_input(input_shape, device):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    input_tensor = torch.randn(*input_shape)
    tt_input = ttnn.from_torch(
        input_tensor.permute(0, 2, 3, 4, 1),  # NCDHW → NDHWC
        device=device,
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT
    )
    return input_tensor, tt_input


def prepare_weights(weight_tensor, device):
    weight_tensor = weight_tensor.permute(2, 3, 4, 1, 0)  # (O, C, kT, kH, kW) → (kT, kH, kW, C, O)
    return ttnn.from_torch(weight_tensor, device=device, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("input_shape", [(1, 3, 2, 14, 14)])
def test_qwen_style_conv3d(device, input_shape):
    out_channels = 1280
    kernel_size = (2, 14, 14)
    stride = (2, 14, 14)
    padding = (0, 0, 0)
    padding_mode = "zeros"

    # PyTorch Conv3D for ground truth
    torch_conv = nn.Conv3d(
        in_channels=3,
        out_channels=out_channels,
        kernel_size=kernel_size,
        stride=stride,
        padding=padding,
        bias=False
    )
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    input_tensor, tt_input = prepare_input(input_shape, device)
    torch_output = torch_conv(input_tensor)

    # Prepare weights for TTNN
    tt_weight = prepare_weights(torch_conv.weight.data, device)

    # Create TTNN conv3d config
    conv_config = ttnn.Conv3dConfig(
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        weights_dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        output_layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT,
        kernel_size=kernel_size,
        stride=stride,
        padding=padding,
        padding_mode=padding_mode,
        output_channels=out_channels,
        compute_with_storage_grid_size=device.compute_with_storage_grid_size()
    )

    kernel_config = ttnn.init_device_compute_kernel_config(
        device.arch(),
        math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi2,
        math_approx_mode=False,
        fp32_dest_acc_en=True
    )

    # Run TTNN conv3d
    tt_output = ttnn.experimental.conv3d(
        input_tensor=tt_input,
        weight_tensor=tt_weight,
        bias_tensor=None,
        config=conv_config,
        compute_kernel_config=kernel_config
    )

    # Convert output back to torch and check shape
    tt_output = ttnn.to_torch(tt_output, device=device).reshape(torch_output.shape)
    assert tt_output.shape == torch_output.shape

    # Validate PCC
    pcc_passed, pcc_message = check_with_pcc(torch_output, tt_output, pcc=0.999)
    logger.info(f"[Conv3D Test] PCC Comparison: {pcc_message}")
    assert pcc_passed, pcc_message

```

### Expected behavior

It would be great to know:
- If fixing this (to support custom strides) is planned or feasible.
- Or if there’s an alternative recommended way to achieve Conv3D with custom strides.

### Please complete the following environment information

- Ubuntu 22.04.5
- Python 3.10.12
- ttnn==0.59.0rc25.dev515+gg265ed453a7

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24633: Conv3D: Fatal error when using custom strides
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

The `conv3d` operation currently enforces that strides must be `(1,1,1)`.  
Passing a different stride configuration, e.g., `(2, 14, 14)`, triggers a `TT_FATAL` in device code.

```
RuntimeError: TT_FATAL @ /root/home/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/experimental/conv3d/device/conv3d_device_operation.cpp:75: config.stride[0] == 1 && config.stride[1] == 1 && config.stride[2] == 1
info:
Strides must be (1,1,1). got (2, 14, 14)
backtrace:
--- /root/home/tt-metal/ttnn/ttnn/_ttnn.so(+0xff416a) [0x7fb569d1f16a]

```

It seems the current implementation enforces strides to always be (1,1,1).



### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
import torch
import pytest
import torch.nn as nn
import ttnn
from loguru import logger
from tests.ttnn.utils_for_testing import check_with_pcc


def prepare_input(input_shape, device):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    input_tensor = torch.randn(*input_shape)
    tt_input = ttnn.from_torch(
        input_tensor.permute(0, 2, 3, 4, 1),  # NCDHW → NDHWC
        device=device,
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT
    )
    return input_tensor, tt_input


def prepare_weights(weight_tensor, device):
    weight_tensor = weight_tensor.permute(2, 3, 4, 1, 0)  # (O, C, kT, kH, kW) → (kT, kH, kW, C, O)
    return ttnn.from_torch(weight_tensor, device=device, dtype=ttnn.bfloat16, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT)


@pytest.mark.parametrize("input_shape", [(1, 3, 2, 14, 14)])
def test_qwen_style_conv3d(device, input_shape):
    out_channels = 1280
    kernel_size = (2, 14, 14)
    stride = (2, 14, 14)
    padding = (0, 0, 0)
    padding_mode = "zeros"

    # PyTorch Conv3D for ground truth
    torch_conv = nn.Conv3d(
        in_channels=3,
        out_channels=out_channels,
        kernel_size=kernel_size,
        stride=stride,
        padding=padding,
        bias=False
    )
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    input_tensor, tt_input = prepare_input(input_shape, device)
    torch_output = torch_conv(input_tensor)

    # Prepare weights for TTNN
    tt_weight = prepare_weights(torch_conv.weight.data, device)

    # Create TTNN conv3d config
    conv_config = ttnn.Conv3dConfig(
        dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        weights_dtype=ttnn.bfloat16,
        output_layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT,
        kernel_size=kernel_size,
        stride=stride,
        padding=padding,
        padding_mode=padding_mode,
        output_channels=out_channels,
        compute_with_storage_grid_size=device.compute_with_storage_grid_size()
    )

    kernel_config = ttnn.init_device_compute_kernel_config(
        device.arch(),
        math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi2,
        math_approx_mode=False,
        fp32_dest_acc_en=True
    )

    # Run TTNN conv3d
    tt_output = ttnn.experimental.conv3d(
        input_tensor=tt_input,
        weight_tensor=tt_weight,
        bias_tensor=None,
        config=conv_config,
        compute_kernel_config=kernel_config
    )

    # Convert output back to torch and check shape
    tt_output = ttnn.to_torch(tt_output, device=device).reshape(torch_output.shape)
    assert tt_output.shape == torch_output.shape

    # Validate PCC

    logger.info(f"[Conv3D Test] PCC Comparison: {pcc_message}")
    assert pcc_passed, pcc_message


```

Issue #24631: MeshCoordinate: add support for N/E/S/W style computation
------------------------------------------------------------
Today we have various parts of the codebase that manually check for Fabric Type, direction and computed a MeshCoordinate:

```
    MeshShape mesh_shape = mesh_coord_range.shape();
    MeshCoordinate N(src_mesh_coord[0] - 1, src_mesh_coord[1]);
    MeshCoordinate E(src_mesh_coord[0], src_mesh_coord[1] + 1);
    MeshCoordinate S(src_mesh_coord[0] + 1, src_mesh_coord[1]);
    MeshCoordinate W(src_mesh_coord[0], src_mesh_coord[1] - 1);

    if (has_flag(fabric_type, FabricType::TORUS_X)) {
        E = MeshCoordinate(src_mesh_coord[0], (src_mesh_coord[1] + 1) % mesh_shape[1]);
        W = MeshCoordinate(src_mesh_coord[0], (src_mesh_coord[1] - 1 + mesh_shape[1]) % mesh_shape[1]);
    }
    if (has_flag(fabric_type, FabricType::TORUS_Y)) {
        N = MeshCoordinate((src_mesh_coord[0] - 1 + mesh_shape[0]) % mesh_shape[0], src_mesh_coord[1]);
        S = MeshCoordinate((src_mesh_coord[0] + 1) % mesh_shape[0], src_mesh_coord[1]);
    }
```

We should have an api that has the above logic `get_mesh_coordinate(MeshCoordinate src, RoutingDirection dir, FabricType fabric_type)`.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24611: ttnn.linear: different PCC values observed between executions on N150 and N300
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug
While working with the SDXL model, a difference in PCC values has been observed when comparing execution on N150 and N300.
The goal is to simulate the timestamp embedding sequence: Linear → SiLU → Linear

A unit test reproducing this issue has been implemented in: /tt-metal/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_linear.py
Test for problem reproduction is shown here 
```
 @pytest.mark.parametrize(
        "input_size, weights1_size, bias1_size, weights2_size, bias2_size, activation, dtype",
        [
            ((1, 2816), (1280,2816), (1280,), (1280, 1280), (1280,), "silu", ttnn.bfloat16),
            ((1, 320), (1280,320), (1280,), (1280, 1280), (1280,), "silu", ttnn.bfloat16), 
        ],)
def test_sdxl_linear(device, input_size, weights1_size, bias1_size, weights2_size, bias2_size, activation, dtype):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    torch_input_tensor = torch_random(input_size, -0.1, 0.1, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_weight1_tensor = torch_random(weights1_size, -0.1, 0.1, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_bias1_tensor = torch_random(bias1_size, -0.01, 0.01, dtype=torch.float32)
    torch_weight2_tensor = torch_random(weights2_size, -0.1, 0.1, torch.float32)
    torch_bias2_tensor = torch_random(bias2_size, -0.01, 0.01, torch.float32)
    
    
    torch_output_tensor = torch.nn.functional.linear(
        torch_input_tensor, torch_weight1_tensor, bias=torch_bias1_tensor)
    torch_output_tensor = torch.nn.functional.silu(torch_output_tensor)
    torch_output_tensor = torch.nn.functional.linear(
        torch_output_tensor, torch_weight2_tensor, bias=torch_bias2_tensor)
    
    ttnn_input_tensor = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input_tensor, dtype=dtype, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )
    weights1 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_weight1_tensor.movedim(-1, -2), dtype=dtype, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )
    bias1 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_bias1_tensor.reshape((1, -1)), dtype=dtype, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )
    weights2 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_weight2_tensor.movedim(-1, -2), dtype=dtype, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )
    bias2 = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_bias2_tensor.reshape((1, -1)), dtype=dtype, device=device, layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT, memory_config=ttnn.L1_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )
    compute_kernel_config = ttnn.WormholeComputeKernelConfig(
            math_fidelity=ttnn.MathFidelity.HiFi2,
            math_approx_mode=False,
            fp32_dest_acc_en=False,
            packer_l1_acc=True,
        )
    output = ttnn.linear(ttnn_input_tensor, 
                         weights1, 
                         bias=bias1, 
                         activation=activation,
                         compute_kernel_config=compute_kernel_config) 
    output = ttnn.linear(output, 
                         weights2, 
                         bias=bias2, 
                         compute_kernel_config=compute_kernel_config)
    output_tensor = ttnn.to_torch(output)
    _, pcc = assert_with_pcc(torch_output_tensor, output_tensor, 0.999)
    print(f"pcc: {pcc}")
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

execute tests on N300 and N150

Observed behavior:
test1 results:
N150 PCC: 0.9998533623605041
N300 PCC: 0.9998533619670886
delta = 3.934155e-10

test2 results:
N150 PCC: 0.9999256752644259
N300 PCC: 0.9999256753504736
delta = -8.60477e-11



### Expected behavior

PCC for N150 and N300 should be the same.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24609: Add INT32 support for square
------------------------------------------------------------
Add INT32 support for square
- Needed for squared difference as well
