### More context/details:
Problematic file is: `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/transformer/sdpa_decode/device/kernels/compute/sdpa_flash_decode.cpp`.
Code snippet on line 136:
```
    if constexpr (tilize_q) {
        tilize_init(cb_q_rm, q_chunk_tiles, cb_q_in);
        cb_wait_front(cb_q_rm, q_chunk_tiles);
        cb_reserve_back(cb_q_in, q_chunk_tiles);
        tilize_block(cb_q_rm, q_chunk_tiles, cb_q_in);
        tilize_uninit(cb_q_rm, cb_q_in);
        cb_push_back(cb_q_in, q_chunk_tiles);
        cb_pop_front(cb_q_rm, q_chunk_tiles);

        mm_init_short(cb_q_in, cb_k_in);
    } else {
        mm_init(cb_q_in, cb_k_in, cb_qk_im);
    }
```
In the passing tests, it goes through the `else` block, and in the failing test the `tilize_q` block where it does an additional tilize before mm.

Issue #24171: Infra for querying mesh topology
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently CCLs have some bespoke code that tries to compute physical connectivity of devices:

```cpp
// Configuration structure for a device, containing its receiver and sender device ids.
struct SenderRecieverConfig {
    uint32_t device_index = 0;
    std::optional<chip_id_t> sender_device_id;
    std::optional<chip_id_t> receiver_device_id;
};

// Returns `SenderRecieverConfig` for a given device, given topology.
SenderRecieverConfig get_device_sender_receiver_config(
    const IDevice* target_device, const std::vector<IDevice*>& devices, ttnn::ccl::Topology topology);

// Returns `SenderRecieverConfig` for a given device in a ring topology with a given cluster axis.
SenderRecieverConfig get_device_sender_receiver_config_in_ring(
    const MeshCoordinate& mesh_coord, const distributed::MeshDevice* mesh_device, uint32_t cluster_axis, int ring_size);
```

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23852 adds similar functionality for getting device neighbors in all 4 directions on the 2D grid.

This should be provided by TTNN infra for convenience.

@sjameelTT @amorrisonTT - let me know if you have other examples we gotta fix!

================================================================================

Issue #24159: Multi-threaded workload/program/kernel compilation starve Taskflow thread pool
------------------------------------------------------------
See https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24009#issuecomment-3005737046

All "compile" methods should ensure that no blocking work is enqueued on the Taskflow thread pool. Otherwise, the thread pool is starved, reducing the number of cores available to do the actual work.

Ideally, all of the relevant methods are asynchronous, with an ability to compose callbacks to run in the correct order on the correct executor.

@ayerofieiev-tt @pgkeller @daminakaTT FYI

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24155: Binary op make flag use_legacy optional except typecast and block format
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently a lot of test cases require binary ops with flag use_legacy=False, because they only work with binary_ng. We need to add new heuristics as following to make sure without the flag, it still direct to binary_ng.

Int type, int32, uint32, uint16, uint8 
Rank > 4
Row_broadcast other than ADD, SUB, MUL
Lhs_activations >1 or rhs_activiations is not empty (runtime error, sad path)
Subtile broadcast type row_a_col_b, col_a_row_b

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

------------------------------------------------------------
Currently a lot of test cases require binary ops with flag use_legacy=False, because they only work with binary_ng. We need to add new heuristics to make sure without the flag, it still direct to binary_ng.

Maybe more places in composite op needs to take a look. Consider rename some test files with ng only names.

Issue #24115: Remove `DistributedTensorConfig` from TTNN
------------------------------------------------------------
`DistributedTensorConfig` is used to specify how a list of tensor shards should be distributed across mesh device. This was replaced by `DistributedHostBuffer` abstraction. The remaining action items:
1. (done in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/24546) Make `aggregate_as_tensor` API "multi host aware". Instead of accepting `DistributedTensorConfig`, it should accept parameters necessary to create a multi-host tensors.
2. Migrate old tensor caches to the new serialization format (or find another workaround that will allow creating `DistributeHostBuffer` from the list of tensor shards directly). Tracked in ~~https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/22262~~ https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/24771.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Previously done for sharding APIs in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23684

Issue #24112: Remote Device Eth Links and Board ID Query Support for BH
------------------------------------------------------------
Querying any intermesh link attributes in `ControlPlane` is currently unsupported for Blackhole. This is primarily because this concept does not currently exist on BH.

As a consequence, we are currently not querying the unique board ID programmed by boot FW on BH either. This is done for WH. This also needs to be correctly spec'd out, since the 64bit `board_id_hi | board_id_lo` in `chip_info_t` may not be unique for BH systems.

fyi @abhullar-tt please add anything I may be missing in this context.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_math_eltwise_unary_datacopy.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_math_reduce.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_pack.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_pack_common.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_A.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_AB.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_AB_matmul.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_common.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_reduce.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_tilize.h
- [ ] ./tt_metal/third_party/tt_llk_blackhole/llk_lib/llk_unpack_untilize.h

## Current Implementation
Currently, the code uses a boolean template parameter `is_dest_accum_en` to control destination accumulation behavior.

## Requested Change
Replace the boolean template parameter with an enum class:
```cpp
enum class DestAccumulation { Disable, Enable };
```
Example:
```cpp
template <PoolType type, ReduceDim dim, bool is_fp32_dest_acc_en, int MATH_FIDELITY_DESC = 0, bool is_int_fpu_en = false>
inline void _llk_math_reduce_(const uint dst_index, bool narrow_tile = false, const uint num_faces = 4)
```
is converted to:
```cpp
template <PoolType type, ReduceDim dim, DestAccumulation fp32_dest_accumulation, int MATH_FIDELITY_DESC = 0, bool is_int_fpu_en = false>
inline void _llk_math_reduce_(const uint dst_index, bool narrow_tile = false, const uint num_faces = 4)
```

## Requirements
1. Replace all occurrences of `is_dest_accum_en` boolean template parameter with the new enum
2. Update all template instantiations to use `DestAccumulation::Enable` or `DestAccumulation::Disable`
3. Modify implementation to use the enum class instead of boolean checks
4. Update any documentation or comments to reflect the new enum parameter
5. Ensure all existing functionality works identically after conversion

## Definition of Done
1. All boolean template parameter usage of `is_dest_accum_en` converted to enum class and parameter is renamed to `fp32_dest_accumulation`
2. All code compiles without errors or warnings
3. All existing tests pass

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #24107: QwQ TTFT too long and Evaluations Hang
------------------------------------------------------------
Pursuant to [this issue](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-inference-server/issues/207#issuecomment-2914342857) for Model Readiness from Customer Success POV, QwQ performance does not meet requirements.

In addition, accuracy evaluations hang indefinitely. In Release <0.58 this was not an issue.

### Steps to reproduce the issue


Using vLLM server and [tt-inference-server](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-inference-server/tree/dev):

Using "benchmarks" workflow, that is:
python run.py --model "QwQ-32B" --workflow benchmarks --device t3k --disable-trace-capture --docker-server

Note: must update model_configs.py from tt-inference-server to use updated tt-metal and vllm commits. One can comment out all other Model Configs and edit QwQ-32B to have the following update:
```
ModelConfigTemplate(
        impl=tt_transformers_impl,
        device_model_spec_map={
            DeviceTypes.T3K: DeviceModelSpec(
                max_concurrency=32,
                max_context=128 * 1024,
                default_impl=True,
            ),
        },
        weights=["Qwen/QwQ-32B"],
        tt_metal_commit="v0.59.0",
        vllm_commit="f028da1",
        status="testing",
```

### Expected behavior

36ms for TTFT

Accuracy evaluations complete.

### Please complete the following environment information

Internal LoudBox
tt_metal_commit="v0.59.0",
vllm_commit="f028da1",

Issue #24095: Narrow row condition in transpose
------------------------------------------------------------
Inside `ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/data_movement/transpose/device/kernels/compute/transpose_wh_rm.cpp` there are two statements that determine narrow row calculation and row width incorrectly. Instead of:
```
 constexpr bool use_narrow_row = last_output_row_num_datums < TILE_WIDTH ? true : false;
 constexpr uint32_t row_size = last_output_row_num_datums < TILE_WIDTH ? last_output_row_num_datums : TILE_WIDTH;
```
it should be:
```
 constexpr bool use_narrow_row = last_output_row_num_datums < FACE_WIDTH ? true : false;
 constexpr uint32_t row_size = last_output_row_num_datums < FACE_WIDTH ? last_output_row_num_datums : FACE_WIDTH;
```

However, with the latest tt-metal main, these changes make the test fail with a PCC error:
`pytest models/demos/yolov4/tests/pcc/test_ttnn_yolov4_bh.py::test_yolov4[device_params0]`

We should investigate why such changes breaks the behavior of this op.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #24088: [DM] Investigate and measure 2D matmul data movement patterns #23120
------------------------------------------------------------
### What 

Investigate and measure performance of different 2D matmul scenarios.

#### Matmul Program Configurations

1. **`MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCast1DProgramConfig`** (`"1D"`)  
   *Used for width or height sharded tensors, or very narrow interleaved tensors.*

2. **`MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCastProgramConfig`** (`"2D"`)  
   *Used for block sharded tensors, and general interleaved tensors.*

3. **`MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCastDRAMShardedProgramConfig`** (`"DRAM"`)  
   *A very specialised config for very narrow tensors in DRAM.*


### 1st variant  to look into - DRAM Sharded #3 from list

#### Test commands
`pytest "tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_matmul.py::test_matmul_in1_dram_sharded_tiny_tile"`

#### Relevant kernels
`ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_in0_sender_dram_sharded.cpp`

`ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_in1_sender_dram_sharded.cpp`

Quasar: 
`software/dm_tests/2d_matmul_in0_sharded`

### 2nd variant  to look into - MatmulMultiCoreReuseMultiCastProgramConfig #2 from list

#### Test commands
`pytest "tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_matmul.py::run_matmul_2d_multiple_output_blocks_per_core"`

`pytest "tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_matmul.py::run_matmul_2d_tiny_tile"`

`pytest "tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_matmul.py::test_padded_2d_matmul"`

`pytest "tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/matmul/test_matmul.py::test_sharded_matmul_with_multiple_out_block_values"`

#### Relevant kernels
'ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_in0_sender_receiver_padding_block_sharded.cpp`

`ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/matmul/device/kernels/dataflow/reader_bmm_tile_layout_in0_receiver.cpp`

Issue #24087: Sharing multiple cards across users in LoudBox
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Our customer has Loudbox with 4 cards. Their objective is to split 2 cards across 2 developers. They want to split the cards in bare metal configuration.

The alternative solution provided by engineering is to use tt-metal docker containers.

The customer wants to understand the internal complexity and issues that are obstructing us from using the split card configuration (2 cards across 2 developers).

`RuntimeError: TT_THROW @ /home/user/test_submesh/tt-metal/tt_metal/llrt/tt_elffile.cpp:177: tt::exception
info:
/home/user/.cache/tt-metal-cache/0545e2f38f/4098/kernels/cq_dispatch_subordinate/10409338902865627150//ncrisc/ncrisc.elf: cannot map elf file into memory: No such file or directory
backtrace:`

Issue #24085: Clarification ask on program caching
------------------------------------------------------------
Looking to understand program caching mechanisms and how they affect performance.

My current mental model:
1. First run of a model will compile the kernels (programs?) and store these on host. Each subsequent run will skip the compilation and reuse the compiled kernels/programs, and thus be more performant.
2. Enabling program cache (i.e. `device->enable_program_cache()`) will store the kernels/programs onto device DRAM, so that host can only "point" to the stored kernel/program, enabling even more performance.

My mental model is incorrect, so I'm looking for clarification.

I wrote a simple test to measure perf at different stages of caching journey.

To run the test:
```
git checkout svuckovic/program-cache-perf-test

source python_env/bin/activate
export TT_METAL_HOME=$(pwd)
export PYTHONPATH=$(pwd)

./build_metal.sh --build-ttnn-tests
./build/test/ttnn/unit_tests_ttnn_emitc --gtest_filter=EmitC.ProgramCache
```
Relevant driver code: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/compare/main...svuckovic/program-cache-perf-test#diff-9943820bc594be19f59dacabc71cf74c0d3c0e802260b886399f45bc3eeb9912R182-R202

The test will run the model 5 times (runs 0-4), then turn on program cache and run another 5 times (runs 5-9).

Run 0 is by far the slowest. Runs 1-4 are faster and have similar times. Program cache is turned on. Run 5 is faster. Runs 6-9 are even faster.

Example log:
```
2025-06-24 16:28:42.963 | info     |           Metal | Initializing device 0. Program cache is NOT enabled (device.cpp:432)
2.72385 seconds for run: run 0
0.00256098 seconds for run: run 1
0.00269541 seconds for run: run 2
0.00190609 seconds for run: run 3
0.00216226 seconds for run: run 4
Enabling program cache
2025-06-24 16:28:46.837 | info     |           Metal | Enabling program cache on MeshDevice 1 (mesh_device.cpp:535)
0.0011379 seconds for run: run 5
5.6706e-05 seconds for run: run 6
5.4722e-05 seconds for run: run 7
5.0805e-05 seconds for run: run 8
7.0212e-05 seconds for run: run 9
[       OK ] EmitC.ProgramCache (4246 ms)
```

Questions:
a. What exactly happens in run 0 so that runs 1-4 are faster?
b. What exactly happens when program cache is turned on that makes run 5 faster?
c. Why are runs 6-9 faster than run 5?

Issue #23995: Tilize and possibly other LLKs dont pass input cb to llk_math_eltwise_unary_datacopy
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

llk_math_eltwise_unary_datacopy uses unpack src and dst dataformats to determine wether to use unpack to dest or ordinary datacopy, for that it needs access to input cb index.
Unfortunately that argument (named `operand`) has a default value of 0 and that value is thus not passed down from compute api layer.
This can lead to issues where cb being tilized has a different dataformat from cb0.

_No response_

_No response_

_No response_

Issue #23882: [Multi-Host - 2D Fabric] Modify append_fabric_connection_rt_args to use FabricNodeId
------------------------------------------------------------
### Problem

The following API is currently exposed by `tt_metal/api/tt-metalium/fabric.hpp` to setup a fabric connection between a user kernel (writer/src) running on a tensix core and the destination chip it must write to:

```
void append_fabric_connection_rt_args(
    chip_id_t src_chip_id,
    chip_id_t dst_chip_id,
    uint32_t link_idx,
    tt::tt_metal::Program& worker_program,
    const CoreCoord& worker_core,
    std::vector<uint32_t>& worker_args,
    CoreType core_type = CoreType::WORKER);
```

This works well for single host systems where we have access to the Physical IDs for all fabric nodes in the graph.

In the multi-host scenario, this is not true - the host deploying the writer/src kernel does not know the Physical ID of the destination chip, if this chip is mapped to another host. Additionally, the use of Physical IDs in Fabric APIs is something we want to avoid in the long term.

### Proposal
The cleanest way to get around this is to modify the API to be as follows:

```
void append_fabric_connection_rt_args(
    FabricNodeId src_node_id,
    FabricNodeId dst_node_id,
    uint32_t link_idx,
    tt::tt_metal::Program& worker_program,
    const CoreCoord& worker_core,
    std::vector<uint32_t>& worker_args,
    CoreType core_type = CoreType::WORKER);
```
Each host has a full view of the Mesh Graph (expressed using `FabricNodes` and directions), i.e. it knows how to route traffic between different `FabricNodeIDs` when using 2D fabric (this is encapsulated in the routing tables).

This means that, with 2D fabric (the only solution for Multi-Host/Multi-Mesh routing), the logic inside `append_fabric_connection_rt_args` does not require the use of physical IDs.

As a start, the function can be modified to conditionally lookup Physical IDs for the 1D Fabric case, which unblocks the use of this API for Multi-Host systems, since 2D Fabric data-paths will only rely on `FabricNodeId`.

fyi @ubcheema @tt-snijjar @aagarwalTT

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23879: Inconsistent version of `reflect` dependency

Issue #23876: ttnn.mean gives incorrect result
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

ttnn.batch_norm produces incorrect result when in training mode due to ttnn.mean (Refer here : [Link](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/21363#:~:text=compared%20to%20bfloat16.-,Incorrect%20mean%20and%20variance%20computation%20in%20training%20mode,-On%20ensuring%20both) )

### Steps to reproduce the issue

```
import torch
import pytest
import ttnn
from tests.ttnn.unit_tests.operations.eltwise.backward.utility_funcs import compare_equal


@pytest.mark.parametrize("testing_dtype", ["bfloat16", "float32"])
def test_mean(testing_dtype, device):
    torch_dtype = getattr(torch, testing_dtype)
    ttnn_dtype = getattr(ttnn, testing_dtype)
    
    torch_input = torch.ones([1, 32, 480, 640], dtype=torch_dtype) * 1.0

    golden = torch.mean(torch_input, (0, 2, 3), True)

    tt_in = ttnn.from_torch(
        torch_input,
        dtype=ttnn_dtype,
        device=device,
        layout=ttnn.TILE_LAYOUT,
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG,
    )

    tt_result = ttnn.mean(ttnn.mean(tt_in, dim=[2, 3], keepdim=True), 0, True)
    print(tt_result, golden)

    comp_pass = compare_equal([tt_result], [golden])
    assert comp_pass

```
The above code gives the below as result on computing mean

- FP32 : 0.99707
- BF16 : 0.99707

### Expected behavior

Expected mean result is 1.0

No linked pull requests found.

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Related:  #23830 
CC: @blozano-tt 

### Describe the solution you'd like

See if this library can meet our needs while improving compile times.

Issue #23873: All-post-commit model unit tests - ViT Model fails with KeyError: 'test' after cats-image dataset update
------------------------------------------------------------
On models-unit-tests:
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/15784007302/job/44502339013#step:5:1288
```
tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vit/test_ttnn_optimized_sharded_vit_wh.py:385: 
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ 

self = DatasetDict({
    train: Dataset({
        features: ['image'],
        num_rows: 1
    })
})
k = 'test'

    def __getitem__(self, k) -> Dataset:
        if isinstance(k, (str, NamedSplit)) or len(self) == 0:
>           return super().__getitem__(k)
E           KeyError: 'test'

/opt/venv/lib/python3.10/site-packages/datasets/dataset_dict.py:58: KeyError

[...]
FAILED tests/ttnn/integration_tests/vit/test_ttnn_optimized_sharded_vit_wh.py::test_vit[sequence_size=224-image_channels=3-image_size=224-batch_size=8-model_name=google/vit-base-patch16-224] - KeyError: 'test'
```

Reverting to an older revision of the `cats-image` dataset fixes the issue
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23867

Starting with HF revision `a66f2ae2cc2626bce7dae62f24225e4ac246a25f` (from https://huggingface.co/datasets/huggingface/cats-image/commits/main) the dataset key is `train` and not `test` when loaded during the test. Changing the key from `test` to `train` also fixes the issue despite the dataset card still specifying `test` as the only valid split: https://huggingface.co/datasets/huggingface/cats-image/blob/main/README.md
This may indicate an issue with the version of the datasets library being used in tt-metal ([2.9.0](https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/tt_metal/python_env/requirements-dev.txt#L44)).

On infra CI machines this was confirmed by the following:
- deleting the .cache directory on the runner and rerunning the test to force a redownload of the dataset at the [latest, breaking, last known good] revisions: [4613f5f, a66f2ae, ccdec0a]
- seeing the following behavior [fail, fail, pass]
- expected split key with datasets==2.9.0: [test, train, test] 
- actual split key with datasets==2.9.0: [train, train, test]

Issue #23859: ND Hang when running CCL unit tests and (potentially) end to end llama while running open_mesh_device
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

On TG in tt-metal/conftest.py while running ttnn.open_mesh_device inside the mesh_device() function, we are seeing an ND hang. This first appeared on CI on June 13 though we have since been able to reproduce it both in latest main and on main on the night of June 11. We have also seen it happen on multiple machines but most often it fails when g06cs03 is the runner, where it fails about 50% of the time when the test suite pytest tests/nightly/tg/ccl is run. It however is also sometimes seen during the end to end llama stress test starting about the same time though that is a far longer duration test and we often have to wait longer to see it fail there. I don't have proof on whether it is the same hang though it appeared at the same time.

The recently rebased branch jvega/test_ccl_extra_logs_on_g06cs03 omits all the tests except for tests/nightly/tg/ccl in tg-nightly-tests and it added debug logs around the suspected function. We can see it hanging in https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/runs/15784272982/job/44497482093 where 
"2025-06-20 18:29:25.673 | DEBUG    | conftest:mesh_device:420 - set fabric config"
was printed but the subsequent log on line 422 of conftest.py was not printed narrowing this as the point where the hang occurred.

Also the specific test within the suite that hangs varies. It is not specific to a particular test but seems to occur randomly within the suite. Likely the suite exposes the issue due to the large number of CCL tests this suite runs rather than something inherent to the tests.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Go to branch  jvega/test_ccl_extra_logs_on_g06cs03
2. Run (TG) Choose Your Pipeline workflow selecting tg-nightly and g06cd03 as the extra tag
3. Repeat the above 2 or 3 times as it will stall about 50% of the time on that runner


### Expected behavior

It should not hang

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.


Running the command with no arguments works and it uses [wormhole_b0-True-16] and [wormhole_b0-True-32] as the standard arguments. 

Should we replace [wormhole_b0-True-1-32-output] for [wormhole_b0-True-32] ?

------------------------------------------------------------

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-shield/actions/runs/15767016500/job/44446244054#step:8:1386

- v0.57.0-rc71, from Apr 21st 2025

The scenario that fails:
```
#   isl        osl        max_concurrency num_prompts
--- ---------- ---------- --------------- ------------
14  3000       64         32              128
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Take the latest branches:
- tt-metal@main
- tenstorrent/vllm@dev
- tt-inference-server@dev

2. Build tt-metal

3. Install vLLM - Detailed instructions are available here:
https://github.com/tenstorrent/vllm/tree/dev/tt_metal#environment-creation
```
cd vllm
export VLLM_TARGET_DEVICE="tt"
export vllm_dir=$(pwd)
# setuptools and wheel might need to be upgraded
pip install -U pip setuptools wheel
pip install -e . --extra-index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
```


4. Download the weights:
```
export HF_HOME=<where_huggingface_hub_is>
export HF_TOKEN<your_huggingface_token>
huggingface-cli download meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
```

4. Start the vLLM server:
```
cd vllm
MESH_DEVICE=N150 WH_ARCH_YAML=wormhole_b0_80_arch_eth_dispatch.yaml HF_MODEL="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct" VLLM_RPC_TIMEOUT=900000 python3 examples/server_example_tt.py --model "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
```

3. Run the benchmarks:
```
cd tt-inference-server
python3 run.py --workflow benchmarks --model Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct --dev-mode --device n150
```
4. See error


## Shorter iteration times

In order to shorten the iteration time, you can narrow down the benchmarks to the problematic case.
1. In tt-inference-server, change the following file like this:
```
benchmarking/benchmark_targets/test_benchmarks_override.json
{
    "Llama-3.1-8B": {
        "n150": [
            {
                "isl": 3000,
                "osl": 64,
                "max_concurrency": 32,
                "num_prompts": 128
            }
        ]
    }
}
```
2. Set environment variables
```
export ONLY_BENCHMARK_TARGETS=1
export OVERRIDE_BENCHMARK_TARGETS=$INFERENCE_SERVER_DIR/benchmarking/benchmark_targets/test_benchmarks_override.json
```
3. Run the benchmarks as described above.


## Testing with older tt-metal
I managed to use the latest vLLM and tt-inference-server with the older tt-metal (v0.57.0-rc71, from the bug description) with one small change.
In `models/tt_transformers/tt/generator_vllm.py` remove this line:
```
from vllm.model_executor.models.mllama import MLLAMA_IMAGE_TOKEN_ID, MLLAMA_IMAGE_TOKEN
```

## Expected behavior

All benchmarks should be able to execute.

### Please complete the following environment information

Using tt-metalium latest docker

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23850: LLK reduce tile precision issue
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

There is a precision issue when using reduce_tile() for summing values across tiles. This becomes especially problematic with wider tensors or inputs containing large/small floating-point values, where accumulated floating-point errors lead to incorrect results.

The issue was observed while implementing cross_entropy_bw and softmax operations. A test PR has been created to demonstrate this behavior and can be used for reproducing and analyzing the bug:
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23741

I’m duplicating some of the results here to improve visibility and provide more context directly in the bug.
To investigate this further and provide clear evidence, I created a set of controlled experiments comparing:
1. reduce — Using reduce_tile() directly
Each tile is reduced via a single-threaded accumulation inside a register.
2. matmul — Using matmul_tiles() with a one-hot row vector
Input tiles are copied and accumulated using add_binary_tile(), and then reduced via matrix multiplication with a tile that has 1s in the first row and 0s elsewhere.

Test Setup
• Tensor shape: [1, 1, 1, W]
• Value range: randomized using uniform distributions ([0.0, 1.0] and [-1.0, 1.0])
• Number of tests: 1000
• Baseline: xt::xarray used to compute reference results
• Output: JSONL file with raw results for plotting and further inspection

1. W = 32, uniform[0.0, 1.0]
One-tile comparison. Shows that even in simple cases, reduce_tile() introduces visible error.
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5aa5b970-2358-4e76-a816-c9973cc4d7b6)

2. W = 1024, uniform[-1.0, 1.0]
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5d60edaa-9a55-4dc5-95ba-3eb82f5c14af)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Take a look into PR:
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23741


### Expected behavior

I assumed that reduce_tile() would offer at least the same level of precision as the matmul-based reduction — if not better — since it’s a dedicated op for summing

### Please complete the following environment information

- OS version: Ubuntu 22.04
- Version of software (commit): 3556a17c214dba51e42a6c1f0c43ba8c331bdd70

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23845: Optimize processing of replicated buffers in `DistributedHostBuffer`
------------------------------------------------------------
Now that `DistributedHostBuffer` can store multiple references to the same `HostBuffer` when using replicated configurations, we can optimize the work by avoiding this duplicated work.

See how replicated buffers are created: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/c96d4acd3d7a7c724489e0043e1957f8592217be/ttnn/core/distributed/distributed_tensor.cpp#L278-L305

And transform/apply, which need to be optimized: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/c96d4acd3d7a7c724489e0043e1957f8592217be/tt_metal/distributed/distributed_host_buffer.cpp#L108-L143

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23839: Retire `test_accuracy.py`
------------------------------------------------------------
Thanks, @yieldthought, for the recommendation to separate out this sub-issue. Quoting message on Slack from Mark (with edits):

Let’s make removing test_accuracy.py a separate PR. It is quite involved:
- Make sure the demo sends accuracy information to superset (through "Upload Benchmark Data" in CI test) when run in this new mode
- Potentially rerun all model, accuracy and machine combinations to regenerate PERF.md top-5 scores given we have changed the methodology. I don’t think we need to regenerate the reference token files, I think they already contain top-5 reference tokens but we should check.
- Port test_accuracy code to the demo to assert that accuracy is not regressing for models that appear in the file (just print a warning if the model name can’t be found in the file).
  - `test_accuracy.py` uses the embedding op from the reference model to process the teacher token and `simple_text_demo.py` uses the ttnn embedding op for that; @epam-ioannis-alexiou is seeing accuracy difference between them and will investigate the root cause.
- Remove all CI accuracy tests that use test_accuracy.py
- Check all CI tests pass on branch
- Remove test_accuracy.py

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23826: Problems with ttnn
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

struct ToLayout {
    static Tensor invoke(
        const ttnn::Tensor& tensor_arg,
        const ttnn::Layout layout,
        const std::optional<ttnn::DataType>& dtype = std::nullopt,
        const std::optional<ttnn::MemoryConfig>& memory_config = std::nullopt,
        IDevice* device = nullptr);

    static Tensor invoke(
        const ttnn::Tensor& tensor_arg,
        const ttnn::Layout layout,
        const std::optional<ttnn::DataType>& dtype = std::nullopt,
        const std::optional<ttnn::MemoryConfig>& memory_config = std::nullopt,
        MeshDevice* device = nullptr);
};
Hey！We found that there are design irrationality in this interface，For example, when the next three option parameters of the two interfaces are used with default values, the compiler cannot match the template. When the last option parameter is used with default values, there is also a situation that the compiler cannot match the template. At the same time, we suggest that it is reasonable to put the parameters with discrimination before the parameters without discrimination. For example, the IDevice* device parameter and MeshDevice* device parameter are placed in const std::optional<ttnn::DataType> &dtype = std::nullopt,
const std::optional<ttnn::MemoryConfig> &memory_config = std::nullopt before both arguments

### Steps to reproduce the issue

We found out when compiling with parameters, such as two parameters, three parameters, four parameters for the interface, so that at compile time, the compiler can not distinguish which interface should be called

### Expected behavior

It is hoped that modifying the related interfaces will make the design more reasonable and make the two interfaces more distinguishable

### Please complete the following environment information

tt-metal v0.58.0-rc36

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23821: Figure out how to get failure signatures at the right level of granularity
------------------------------------------------------------
Figure out how to post-process failure signatures to get the right level of granularity, and store that info as the failure signature in the DB.

Current elastic dashboard stores either the type of failure (e.g. TT_ASSERT) which is too coarse, or the detailed message (e.g. "pcc=0.99682736233786") which is too fine grained.

Investigate how to generate better error signatures such that they can be better tied together when it comes the root cause or the solution that binds them together.

Issue #23801: [DM] Remove Arch-dependent HW calls from dataflow_api.h
------------------------------------------------------------
dataflow_api.h contains functions that contains NOC commands which are arch dependent:
```// TODO: write docs
// this issues only a single packet with size <= NOC_MAX_BURST_SIZE (ie maximum packet size)
template <bool inc_num_issued = true>
FORCE_INLINE void noc_async_read_one_packet_with_state(
    std::uint32_t src_noc_addr, std::uint32_t dst_local_l1_addr, uint8_t noc = noc_index) {
    /*
        Read requests - use static VC
        Read responses - assigned VCs dynamically
    */
    RECORD_NOC_EVENT_WITH_ADDR(NocEventType::READ_WITH_STATE, static_cast<uint64_t>(src_noc_addr), 0, -1);
    if constexpr (inc_num_issued) {
        if constexpr (noc_mode == DM_DYNAMIC_NOC) {
            inc_noc_counter_val<proc_type, NocBarrierType::READS_NUM_ISSUED>(noc, 1);
        }
    }

    WAYPOINT("RP4W");
    while (!noc_cmd_buf_ready(noc, read_cmd_buf));
    WAYPOINT("RP4D");

    WAYPOINT("NATW");

    // In order to sanitize, need to grab full noc addr + xfer size from state.
    DEBUG_SANITIZE_NOC_READ_TRANSACTION_WITH_ADDR_AND_SIZE_STATE(noc, src_noc_addr, dst_local_l1_addr);

    NOC_CMD_BUF_WRITE_REG(noc, read_cmd_buf, NOC_RET_ADDR_LO, dst_local_l1_addr);
    NOC_CMD_BUF_WRITE_REG(noc, read_cmd_buf, NOC_TARG_ADDR_LO, src_noc_addr);
    NOC_CMD_BUF_WRITE_REG(noc, read_cmd_buf, NOC_CMD_CTRL, NOC_CTRL_SEND_REQ);

    if constexpr (inc_num_issued) {
        if constexpr (noc_mode == DM_DEDICATED_NOC) {
            noc_reads_num_issued[noc] += 1;
        }
    }

    WAYPOINT("NATD");
}
```

All of these functions need to be arch agnostic in dataflow api, and should be lowered to noc_nonblocking instead

@atatuzunerTT @vvukomanovicTT

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23788: Unable to write 67MB sharded buffer to DRAM
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Issue #23778: Decouple input and output memory configs - pool2d
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Currently, in pool2d, the input and output memory configs are not decoupled. If the input is sharded across X cores, the output will also be sharded across X cores. However, in some cases, the output effectively uses fewer cores than the input. Similar issue as https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/23639

Issue #23767: Eltwise ops performance monitoring
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

I have an idea of implementing a performance measurement tool for our eltwise ops in an convenient way
Currently, measuring performance for eltwise operations involves several manual steps:

1. Editing the test file to specify the desired op and configurations.


2. Running the profiling tool manually.


3. Manually inspecting the generated CSV to extract performance metrics like kernel duration.



This process is tedious, and time-consuming, especially when testing multiple ops or iterating frequently.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Solution i am suggesting is something like below 
`./perf_script.sh --op=add`
Or even more simpler 
`./perf_script.sh add`

It will automatically run the profile for the mentioned op and analyse the CSV data and give us the kernel op duration and any other data as we needed

Just a single command to automatically get the kernel op duration in our eyes 

### Describe alternatives you've considered

_No response_

### Additional context

Allowed flags and complete details: TBD (@Aswinmcw )

Another random thought: Once implemented we can connect a sheet to a machine and make it to run all ops to collect the perf at mentioned interval so that we can regularly monitor the perf of each ops, we can make it colour coded to identify if perf degraded or improved

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23766: Add support for ND shard shape rank being less than tensor shape rank
------------------------------------------------------------
We have two primary motivations for this:
1. We can interpret shard shape rank < tensor shape rank to have the same meaning as in 2D sharding, making any 2D block sharding convertible to ND, significantly extending compatibility
2. Accessor performance is highly dependent on ranks. So having the ability to automatically decrease the rank for some cases would significantly improve accessor performance for those cases.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

------------------------------------------------------------
We need a simple and intuitive way for a user to be able to shard a tensor without manually specifying shard shape which must follow alignment requirements.

Issue #23760: Provide 6U Implementation for intermesh links
------------------------------------------------------------
This PR starts to introduce query APIs for inter-mesh ethernet links: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23163. 

The current implementation supports T3000 systems using ethernet routing fw. 6U Galaxy rely hav ethernet routing fw disabled, and we'll need to source the information for inter-mesh links in a different way than what is currently being done for the T3000 systems. This issue is to capture 6U implementation for intermesh links.

Issue #23755: Add Translation from MeshCoordinate to FabricNodeId to MeshDevice
------------------------------------------------------------
We currently have the following APIs in `MeshDevice`:

```
IDevice* get_device(chip_id_t physical_device_id) const;
IDevice* get_device(const MeshCoordinate& coord) const;
DeviceIds get_device_ids() const;
```
`device->id()` provides the physical (UMD based ID for a specific device). With TT-Fabric being exposed to TTNN and op developers, we want the ability to look up the `FabricNodeId` for a particular `MeshCoordinate`. TT-Fabric host and device APIs rely on the `FabricNodeId`, and hence this information will be required by Op developers as they start writing more complex CCL ops.

 fyi @ubcheema @cfjchu

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23751: Move Ethernet + Fabric Query and State Management APIs from Cluster to Control Plane
------------------------------------------------------------
### Background
`tt_cluster` was originally intended as a lightweight wrapper providing TT-Metal access to UMD for IO and chip level queries. This class eventually started exposing APIs to manage, track and query ethernet state and connectivity information.

With `ControlPlane` being defined as the new module for managing single and multi-mesh resources (ex: generating routing tables, setting up intermesh connections, monitoring and querying ethernet links statuses), the separation of responsibilities between the two classes becomes unclear.

### Proposal
- Move all ethernet related state-management and queries from `tt_cluster` to `ControlPlane`. For example, the following APIs need to be migrated:

```
Cluster::reserve_ethernet_cores_for_fabric_routers(uint8_t num_routing_planes);
Cluster::release_ethernet_cores_for_fabric_routers();
Cluster::get_fabric_ethernet_routers_between_src_and_dest(chip_id_t src_id, chip_id_t dst_id);
Cluster::is_ethernet_link_up(chip_id_t chip_id, const CoreCoord& logical_core);
Cluster::set_internal_routing_info_for_ethernet_cores(bool enable_internal_routing, const std::vector<chip_id_t> &target_mmio_devices);
Cluster::is_external_cable(chip_id_t physical_chip_id, CoreCoord eth_core);
Cluster::get_eth_cores_with_frequent_retraining(chip_id_t chip_id) const;
Cluster::get_eth_routing_info(chip_id_t chip_id) const;
```
- Keep using the existing cluster class for UMD based I/O, TLB setup, harvesting and other chip level queries/management. Eventually, the goal is for UMD to not expose Ethernet backed I/O and only expose PCIe chips. 
- The Control Plane will eventually be built to manage and expose multiple-hosts and generic queries for system health, connectivity, etc.

**Note:** https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/pull/23694 provides an example of the immediate cleanup work required.


fyi @ubcheema @cfjchu @tt-snijjar @abhullar-tt  @aliuTT

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23733: Optimize Multicast Write Packet Headers
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently the fabric will assemble the uint64_t noc address for multicast writes, based on information in the packet header, e.g.

```
    const auto mcast_dest_address = get_noc_multicast_addr(
        mcast_write_header.noc_x_start,
        mcast_write_header.noc_y_start,
        mcast_write_header.noc_x_start + mcast_write_header.mcast_rect_size_x,
        mcast_write_header.noc_y_start + mcast_write_header.mcast_rect_size_y,
        mcast_write_header.address);
```

This address can be generated on the worker side and packed directly into the packet header so the fabric can simply load the uint64_t instead of generating it on the fly.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23731: Add buffer config struct to fabric datamover builder
------------------------------------------------------------
struct FabricEriscDatamoverOptions {
    FabricEriscDatamoverType edm_type = FabricEriscDatamoverType::Default;

    bool enable_dateline_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;

    bool enable_dateline_upstream_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_adjacent_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;
};

these should be put into a separate struct to make things clear.
 bool enable_dateline_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_adjacent_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;

for example, 
struct FabricEriscDatamoverBufferConfig {
    bool enable_dateline_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_sender_extra_buffer_slots = false;
    bool enable_dateline_upstream_receiver_extra_buffer_slots = false;

};

Issue #23687: Hitting a hang with trace enabled but the op works with just program cache enabled (T3K)
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

I'm not sure if there's some issue in my setup. When I run with trace on I get the following terminal log before a hang:

```
2025-06-17 21:50:36.104 | INFO     | tests.nightly.tg.ccl.test_all_to_all_dispatch:run_all_to_all_dispatch_test:368 - Capturing Warmup
2025-06-17 21:50:36.104 | INFO     | tests.nightly.tg.ccl.test_all_to_all_dispatch:run_all_to_all_dispatch_test:371 - Capturing Warmup 1 iterations
2025-06-17 21:50:36.104 | INFO     | tests.nightly.tg.ccl.test_all_to_all_dispatch:run_all_to_all_dispatch_test:373 - Trace capture started
FAILED
```

`FAILED` indicates to me it hits an assert but nothing seems to get piped out.

When I run gdb and suspend the process at the hang the backtrace is the following:

```
0x00007ffff7ce7117 in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6
(gdb) bt
#0  0x00007ffff7ce7117 in ?? () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6
#1  0x00007ffff7ce9a41 in pthread_cond_wait () from /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6
#2  0x00007fff85bbde41 in std::condition_variable::wait<tt::tt_metal::distributed::FDMeshCommandQueue::clear_expected_num_workers_completed()::$_0>(std::unique_lock<std::mutex>&, tt::tt_metal::distributed::FDMeshCommandQueue::clear_expected_num_workers_completed()::$_0) (this=0x55555faf5ca8, __lock=..., __p=...) at /usr/bin/../lib/gcc/x86_64-linux-gnu/12/../../../../include/c++/12/condition_variable:102
#3  0x00007fff85bbdc54 in tt::tt_metal::distributed::FDMeshCommandQueue::clear_expected_num_workers_completed (this=0x55555fac51c0) at /localdev/sjameel/tt-metal/tt_metal/distributed/fd_mesh_command_queue.cpp:189
#4  0x00007fff85bbd4c6 in tt::tt_metal::distributed::FDMeshCommandQueue::~FDMeshCommandQueue (this=0x55555fac51c0) at /localdev/sjameel/tt-metal/tt_metal/distributed/fd_mesh_command_queue.cpp:99
#5  0x00007fff85bbdcf9 in tt::tt_metal::distributed::FDMeshCommandQueue::~FDMeshCommandQueue (this=0x55555fac51c0) at /localdev/sjameel/tt-metal/tt_metal/distributed/fd_mesh_command_queue.cpp:91
```

Seems like some workers do not complete here. My guess is that some are hitting a FATAL and not completing as a result. Since the FATAL doesn't pipe out here I don't know what's going on.


Code snippet on the Python side. I don't think I enqueue any writes or pull back the data on to host so I don't see what would break trace. I put a comment beside the op call that causes the hang – it's the second call to all_to_all_dispatch in the test.


```
def run_op(n_iters, store_all_results=True):
        tt_output_list = []
        tt_metadata_list = []

        for i in range(n_iters):
            buffer_index = 0 if trace_mode else i
            ttnn.all_to_all_dispatch(
                input_tensors[buffer_index],
                expert_indices_tensors[buffer_index],
                expert_mapping_tensors[buffer_index],
                axis=1,
                num_links=num_links,
                topology=topology,
                memory_config=output_memory_config,
                global_semaphore=ccl_semaphore_handles[buffer_index],
                subdevice_id=worker_sub_device_id,
                output_tensors=[output_tensors[buffer_index], metadata_tensors[buffer_index]],
            )

            tt_out_tensor = output_tensors[buffer_index]
            tt_metadata = metadata_tensors[buffer_index]

            if not trace_mode:
                ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)
            if store_all_results:
                tt_output_list.append(tt_out_tensor)
                tt_metadata_list.append(tt_metadata)
        if store_all_results:
            return tt_output_list, tt_metadata_list
        else:
            return [tt_out_tensor], [tt_metadata]

    if trace_mode:
        # compile run:
        logger.info("Compiling model")
        tt_out_tensor_list = run_op(1, store_all_results=False)

        logger.info("Capturing Warmup")

        if warmup_iters > 0:
            logger.info(f"Capturing Warmup {warmup_iters} iterations")
            trace_id_warmup = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(mesh_device, cq_id=0)
            logger.info(f"Trace capture started")
            run_op(warmup_iters, store_all_results=False)  #HANGS HERE
            logger.info(f"Op run for {warmup_iters} iterations completed")
            ttnn.end_trace_capture(mesh_device, trace_id_warmup, cq_id=0)
            logger.info(f"Warmup trace capture completed")
            ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)
            logger.info(f"Device synchronized")

        logger.info("Capturing Trace")
        trace_id = ttnn.begin_trace_capture(mesh_device, cq_id=0)
        tt_outs = run_op(num_iters, store_all_results=False)
        ttnn.end_trace_capture(mesh_device, trace_id, cq_id=0)
        ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)

        logger.info("Starting Trace perf test...")
        profiler.start("reduce-scatter-trace-warmup")
        if warmup_iters > 0:
            ttnn.execute_trace(mesh_device, trace_id_warmup, blocking=False)
            ttnn.release_trace(mesh_device, trace_id_warmup)
            ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)
        profiler.end("reduce-scatter-trace-warmup")

        signpost("start")
        profiler.start("reduce-scatter-trace")
        ttnn.execute_trace(mesh_device, trace_id, blocking=False)
        ttnn.release_trace(mesh_device, trace_id)
        ttnn.synchronize_device(mesh_device)
        profiler.end("reduce-scatter-trace")
        signpost("stop")

        time_taken = profiler.get_duration("reduce-scatter-trace") - profiler.get_duration(
            "reduce-scatter-trace-warmup"
        )
        logger.info(f"Time taken e2e: {time_taken} s")
```



### Steps to reproduce the issue

This test runs on T3K.
1. Checkout `sjameel/trace_all_to_all_bug`
2. ./build_metal.sh -b Debug
3. `tests/nightly/tg/ccl/test_all_to_all_dispatch.py::test_all_to_all_dispatch_trace`
4. See error


### Expected behavior

Issue #23681: Fix Average Pool with 32 Row Reductions and C_Tiles % 8 == 1
------------------------------------------------------------
Average pool currently supports 32 row reductions for most cases.  But, for large kernels with `C_tiles > 8 && C_tiles % 8 == 1` it does not work with 32 row reductions.  This should be fixed.

To Reproduce:
- checkout branch: `wransom/LargeKernelBug2`
- run: `pytest tests/ttnn/nightly/unit_tests/operations/pool/test_avgpool2d_32_row_large.py`
- notice that the cases matching the above condition fail.

Also note that in this branch 32 row reductions have been enabled for all cases (except partial tiles).  In main these failing test cases pass, but this is because 32 row reductions will be disabled for these cases.

This is not an issue with max pool.

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23674: Fix Generic Pools With Very Large Kernels
------------------------------------------------------------
Generic pools currently only works as long as `kernel_h * kernel_w <= max_rows_per_reduction^2` due to the use of the intermediate buffer which can only accommodate a fixed number of intermediate results.  This kernel needs to be redesigned to support an arbitrarily large kernel.

To reproduce:
- checkout branch: `wransom/LargeKernelBug`
- run: `pytest tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/pool/test_maxpool2d_large_kernel.py`
- notice that the cases where the above requirement is not satisfied fail

Issue #23669: Experiment developing FPU activation functions
------------------------------------------------------------
Develop some recipes for constructing activation functions on the FPU, with the intent of having higher performance than the SFPU implementations. We can use this to either speed up activations, or keep performance constant and increase accuracy through more complex approximations.

This is based on the assumption that some of our activations can be approximated by piecewise polynomials, and it should be possible to evaluate polynomials on the FPU, operating on much more data per-cycle than the SFPU. A predicate for good performance is operating on at least tile-sized inputs, which is the case in most models.

In most cases, we still need to do some evaluation on the SFPU, for example for conditional evaluation for the piecewise aspect of the problem.

GeLU is a good starting point. Other functions may follow if we can figure out how to map them to the FPU. This is also a good general opportunity to become more familiar with the FPU.

Some interesting milestones to potentially try out:
- Create a polynomial evaluation function on the FPU
- Benchmark FPU evaluated polynomials compared to SFPU polynomials
- Investigate conditional execution, for piecewise polynomials, likely where the SFPU has to do some merging between tiles evaluated with different polynomials, conditional on the input value (see also the issue tracking an SFPU ternary operator https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/19975)
- Evaluate performance of piecewise polynomial functions.
- If we hold performance constant against the existing SFPU implementations, can we increase the polynomial order to increase accuracy?

Related case: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/issues/20546

@KalaivaniMCW @nvelickovicTT - FYI

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

------------------------------------------------------------

- Removed from tests/ttnn/unit_tests/gtests/CMakeLists.txt
- Add to tests/ttnn/benchmark/cpp/CMakeLists.txt
- Updated to use BENCHMARK instead of TEST_P, which may require additional updates to other files
- smoke test needs to be verified to not run the tests (via https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/actions/workflows/blackhole-post-commit.yaml)

Issue #23656: requirements-dev.txt: update `tt-smi` to at least 3.0.20
------------------------------------------------------------
If you follow the setup directions and install the latest driver, you will get driver 2.0.0 (at time of writing).

However, tt-metal's create_venv.sh script installs tt-smi 3.0.12, which is not fully compatible with driver 2.0.0: https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-smi/issues/110

I think if this is modified to be at least 3.0.20, the user will not have to figure out how to update tt-smi in their own environment.

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/30c80b774c9fbb7ab47086b4ce3ed93a3423ac61/tt_metal/python_env/requirements-dev.txt#L8

Issue #23654: Remove mixed use of ND sharding and legacy sharding
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Even if a tensor is created with ND sharding in memory config, it may be converted to legacy sharding in `TensorSpec::populate_sharding_spec`.

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/b707cdaeb47b583f13bd20ab54677467b11496e5/ttnn/core/tensor/tensor_spec.cpp#L144

This is confusing because in a program factory we must check whether the distribution spec of a buffer is type of `ShardSpecBuffer` or `BufferDistributionSpec` although it is guaranteed that the tensor is created with ND sharding. Is there any specific intention on this logic?

### Describe the solution you'd like

Remove conversion between ND sharding and legacy sharding.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Always use tensor instead of buffer to get ND sharding info of memory config in the tensor.

### Additional context

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23652: [Bug Report] `Buffer::aligned_size_per_bank` does not handle ND sharding
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

In case of creating CBs on the L1 tensor using `CircularBufferConfig::set_globally_allocated_address`, the maximum allowed size (`max_size`) is calculated by `Buffer::aligned_size_per_bank`. However, it always assumes that pages are evenly distributed in each cores, which is wrong in ND sharding. That is, the maximum allowed size can be smaller than the actual size of the tensor on a core.

https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/3fbd3ff68830cbe5fc3ae69cfb8ae036138a1ebd/tt_metal/impl/buffers/buffer.cpp#L579

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Create `CircularBufferConfig` and set L1 address with `set_globally_allocated_address`.
2. `max_size` is smaller than the actual tensor size per core.

### Expected behavior

`max_size` must be the tensor size per core at least.

### Please complete the following environment information

_No response_

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23644: topk not correct when part of the input contains inf values
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

For ttnn.top_k the output is not correct if a part of the input tensor contains inf values, e.g.
consider an input tensor of shape [1,1,32,16*1024] with inf in [:,:,1,:], when computing topk over the last dim=3, the results are not correct, including the first row that had normal bfp8 values in them.

Note: same works of bf16; however, it's not only the precision loss from converting the inputs to bfp8 with inf values in the same block, since converting the bfp8 inputs back to bf16 and applying topk on those results in correct outputs!

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Check out branch` jrock/topk-bug`
2. Run topk unit test: `pytest /proj_sw/user_dev/jrock/tt-metal/tests/ttnn/unit_tests/operations/reduce/test_topk.py::test_topk_sub_core_grids`

This will pass for the test without inf overwrites and bf16 inputs, but will fail the test with inf overwrites and bfp8 inputs.

Implication and priority
- Relevant for TG llama 80 (sampling module)
- We have a workaround for this issue: typecast to bf16 before topk op
- This is blocking TG llama 80 t/s/u perf targets

No linked pull requests found.

Issue #23639: Decouple input and output memory configs - conv2d
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Currently, in conv2d, the input and output memory configs are not decoupled (the width sharded convolution is an exception). If the input is sharded across X cores, the output will also be sharded across X cores. However, in some cases, the output effectively uses fewer cores than the input.

Repro branch: `skrstic/repro-wrong-out-mem-config-conv2d`
Repro command: `pytest tests/ttnn/nightly/unit_tests/operations/conv/test_conv2d.py::test_conv2d_and_add`

In this repro, the input is sharded across 64 cores, but the output is effectively sharded across only 50 cores. If there is an add operation following the conv2d, it will hang on the last 14 cores. The output memory configuration needs to be corrected.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23619: v0.58.1: `TT::Metalium` CMake target doesn't bring Boost include directory
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

Seems like the tt-metalium headers (specifically `host_api.hpp`) transitively brings in `boost/core/span.hpp`, so if you use the `host_api.hpp` header you will get a compilation error

`hello.cpp`:
```c++
#include <tt-metalium/host_api.hpp>

int main() {
    return 0;
}
```

Here's the CMakeLists.txt. If I install my own Boost 1.86.0  and bring it along (the commented out lines), then I can compile

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.25)
project(Examples)

add_executable(hello hello.cpp)

find_package(tt-metalium REQUIRED)
#find_package(Boost REQUIRED)

target_link_libraries(hello PRIVATE TT::Metalium)
#target_include_directories(hello PRIVATE ${Boost_INCLUDE_DIRS})
```

Issue #23617: Single channel average pool does fails.
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the bug

The following test will fail:
```
import ttnn
import torch
import pytest

@pytest.mark.parametrize("device_params", [{"l1_small_size": 24576}], indirect=True)
def test_single_channel_pool(device):

    x = torch.randn(1, 1, 56, 56, dtype=torch.bfloat16)
    golden = torch.nn.functional.avg_pool2d(x, kernel_size=2, stride=2, padding=0)

    tt_x = ttnn.from_torch(x.reshape(1, 1, 56*56, 1), layout=ttnn.ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT, device=device)

    output = ttnn.avg_pool2d(tt_x, 
        batch_size=1, 
        input_h=56, 
        input_w=56, 
        channels=1, 
        kernel_size=[2, 2], 
        stride=[2, 2], 
        padding=[0, 0], 
        applied_shard_scheme=ttnn.TensorMemoryLayout.WIDTH_SHARDED, 
        memory_config=ttnn.DRAM_MEMORY_CONFIG)
    output_cpu = output.to_torch()

    assert torch.allclose(output_cpu, golden, rtol=0.02)
```

With error:  
```
E       RuntimeError: TT_FATAL @ /localdev/lpanos/tt-metal/ttnn/cpp/ttnn/operations/conv/conv2d/conv2d_utils.cpp:311: channels % num_cores_channels == 0
E       info:
E       Channels: 1, num core channels: 2
```

It fails when the input is in both `TILE_LAYOUT` and `ROW_MAJOR_LAYOUT` for all input sharding configurations: `HEIGHT_SHARDED`, `WIDTH_SHARDED`, `BLOCK_SHARDED` although you will get a different error than the one above depending on the config.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. Run the test file above.


### Expected behavior

It compiles and executes with the correct output data.

### Please complete the following environment information

- Ubuntu 22.04 - metal IRD docker image
- Wormhole_b0

Issue #23608: Ideal Runtime Estimate for Eltwise
------------------------------------------------------------
See parent issue for high level overview and a reference to the infra that needs to be filled out.

I think the general strategy should be to categorize eltwise OPs into a couple of compute effort categories:
- Elementary (add, mult, etc.)
- Small (anything evaluated with a simple calculation or LUT)
- Medium (Anything with a finite iteration or polynomial approximation, or piecewise polynomials)
- Complex (anything with a more complex evaluation)
- Composite
And come up with a compute effort estimate for each category. The categorization can be done empirically, based on measurements of the OPs and binning them.

In addition, compute the data movement bound, based on whether the input or output is in DRAM, L1 interleaved or L1 sharded - based on bandwidth limits.

The overall OP estimate will be the max of compute or data movement.

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================

Issue #23606: Installing torch for Basic Examples in TTNN
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the documentation issue

Every example in the Basic Examples uses `torch` library. 
https://github.com/tenstorrent/tt-metal/blob/main/docs/source/ttnn/ttnn/usage.rst

The usage does not seem that significant and is mostly revolve around `torch.rand` function. 

### Describe the documentation solution you want to see

Expect to remove the reliance on torch for basic examples.
This may be solved by creating our own torch.rand and/or torch.zero functions.

Issue #23602: Streamline the 3 installtion options
------------------------------------------------------------
### Describe the documentation issue

There are 3 different installation paths presented in the INSTALLING.md guide.

1. From Source
2. From Wheel
3. From Docker Release Container

These three options do not have the same follow up instructions. 

The section "You are all set" applies only to #1 "From Source". 
You can not use the examples from `ttnn.examples.usage.run_op_on_device` as this package is not present in the Python wheel.


### Describe the documentation solution you want to see

I believe the section you are all set needs to move to the Source option or we need to come up with an alternative that works for all 3 different options.

### Describe the diagrams or screenshots (if any) that the documentation should have

_No response_

Issue #23594: Change reduce sum calls in Fused Ops
------------------------------------------------------------
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Currently to improve the accuracy of layernorm, we have investigated the reduction sum step of layernorm. The conclusion being that calculation the sum through pairwise addtion seemed to be the best way to improve accuracy.

### Describe the solution you'd like

 Since this should increase accuracy and performance, we should look to implement this across all fused ops that calculate a sum.

Issue #23586: Plan out new approach to automatically choosing a matmul program config
------------------------------------------------------------
Currently, if a matmul program config is not provided, it is chosen by the op. There were two separate paths (one in C++ and one in Python) that were moved somewhat closer together and somewhat intertwined. There was additional work done to choose parameters with better perf for larger dram interleaved tensors. However, a larger refactoring effort needs to be done to have a single algorithm that chooses reasonably efficient program configs.

The work involves:
- looking at the existing program config limitations
- creating an impl doc for creating the new algorithm
- making a rough schedule of the work involved (including updating model perf / pcc values for pipelines)

No linked pull requests found.
================================================================================
